features.title: /carte walkthrough film — self-shooting guided tour embedded under the atlas
features.problem: The /carte atlas shows the product's structure as a static graph, but a first-time visitor still has to imagine the actual journey (home → intake → login → magic link → /me → a real session with Marc's reply). A demo video would communicate it, but a baked MP4 drifts from the product the moment any copy or layout changes, and a per-language cut doubles the asset. There was no moving demo of the real flow.
features.analysis: WalkthroughFilm renders below MapCanvas on /carte (FR) and /en/map (EN) as a coda — the interactive atlas stays the page's lead. A poster frame (committed capture) shows until an explicit click, which then mounts an iframe of public/walkthrough/player.html. The player animates a camera + cursor over freshly-captured, committed screenshots, reading captions live from timeline.js by ?lang — so one player serves both languages with no per-language video file. Chapter chips re-key the iframe to play the full cut or a single scene (?scene=). The capture pipeline (scripts/walkthrough/) names ANCHORS, never pixels: capture.mjs records each anchor's bounding box from the live DOM into manifest.json, so layout can move and the shot still lands.

decisions.decision: Iframe the live player instead of a baked <video>
decisions.rationale: Captions render live from timeline.js keyed on ?lang, so a single player serves FR + EN. A baked video would need one file per language and would re-render stale the moment copy or styling changed. The iframe player aims a camera over freshly-captured frames, so re-capturing (not re-editing) keeps the tour honest to the product.
decisions.recommendation: Keep timeline.js pixel-free (anchors only). To re-cut pacing/captions edit timeline.js; to track product changes just re-run npm run walkthrough:capture.

decisions.decision: Click-to-play: the iframe only mounts after an explicit click
decisions.rationale: Defers the player load off the /carte critical path, and means nothing animates for a prefers-reduced-motion visitor until they ask for it. The poster is a committed frame; if it 404s the panel still works (posterFailed fallback).
decisions.recommendation: If autoplay is ever wanted, gate it behind a matchMedia('(prefers-reduced-motion: reduce)') check, never unconditionally.

decisions.decision: Commit frames/ + manifest.json, gitignore video/
decisions.rationale: The embedded player animates over frames + manifest at runtime, so those must ship to prod — same discipline as the committed e2e visual baselines. The rendered *.webm files are a heavier share/export artifact the embed never loads, so they stay out of git.
decisions.recommendation: Refresh committed frames with npm run walkthrough:capture and re-commit whenever the app's look changes, the same way visual baselines are regenerated.

decisions.decision: CSP-clean player (external player.js, self-hosted fonts)
decisions.rationale: The player loads under the production _headers CSP with no inline script and no Google Fonts CDN — visitor IP never reaches a third party, holding the Loi 25 line the rest of the site keeps.
decisions.recommendation: Any new player asset must stay same-origin; do not add a third-party font or script tag to player.html.

features.successCriteria:
- Renders below the atlas on /carte and /en/map
- Poster shows until click; click mounts the iframe player on the selected chapter
- Chapter chips switch scenes (full cut + 6 per-page shorts) and re-key the iframe cleanly
- Captions render in the visitor's language live (?lang), no per-language video file
- Poster 404 → panel still works (play button alone)
- Loads under the production CSP; no third-party font or script
- FR + EN copy in Québécois register, em-dash-free captions
knownLimitations: Capture is manual (npm run walkthrough:capture) — not yet wired into CI or the build, so committed frames can go stale if the app changes and nobody re-captures. The README carries a CI sketch for automating it against the backend e2e harness.
The /me and /session frames can only be captured against the Functions backend with a seeded, signed session (the public routes shoot fine against vite preview alone).
The rendered video/*.webm share clips are gitignored; regenerate locally with npm run walkthrough:video.
Embedding on /carte shifts the page screenshot — the e2e visual baselines must be regenerated on the PR.

statusHistory.reason: Shipped: guided-tour film embedded on /carte ÷ /en/map with the self-shooting capture pipeline and FR/EN live captions.

features.title: Custodian-subscription reconciliation (digest-cron piggyback)
features.problem: The payment webhook keeps sessions.custodian_status in lockstep with Stripe in real time, but a webhook can be missed (Stripe delivery failure, or our endpoint briefly down during a deploy). When that happens our record drifts from Stripe's truth: a lapsed subscription we still show 'active' (over-counted MRR, a non-paying visitor still in custodian mode), or a recovered subscription we still show 'past_due' (a paying visitor stuck behind a past-due banner). Nothing caught that drift. Closes prod-readiness gap #10.
features.analysis: Split into a pure comparator and a thin IO wrapper. computeCustodianDrift(rows, activeSubIds) is pure: it walks our active/past_due custodian rows and flags the two drift directions against the set of subscription ids Stripe reports active. reconcileCustodians(env) does the IO: graceful no-op when STRIPE_SECRET_KEY is unset, listActiveSubscriptions() (cursor-paginated GET /v1/subscriptions?status=active), the SELECT of active/past_due custodian rows, computeCustodianDrift, and a single admin_alerts write. It runs from the daily digest cron as piggyback housekeeping #4 — no new auth surface, no new schedule.

decisions.decision: Alert-only — never auto-flip custodian_status
decisions.rationale: Auto-healing would race the webhook (which may deliver the same event moments later) and could move a visitor between paying states with no human glance. Writing one admin_alerts row and letting Marc reconcile in the Stripe Dashboard matches the manual-review shape already used for refunds.
decisions.recommendation: If drift becomes frequent and the safe direction is unambiguous (e.g. past_due→active when Stripe confirms active), revisit a narrow auto-heal for that one case only.

decisions.decision: Piggyback the daily digest cron instead of a new endpoint
decisions.rationale: A standalone endpoint would need either CSRF exemption (for a token cron) or a dedicated admin button — new surface for a low-frequency check. The digest cron already runs daily, is token-gated, and is where the other housekeeping sweeps live. Reconciliation is idempotent and one Stripe API call at this scale.
decisions.recommendation: If reconciliation needs a cadence independent of the digest (e.g. hourly during a billing incident), promote it to its own token-gated endpoint then.

decisions.decision: One open alert at a time (dedup on unresolved kind='custodian-reconcile')
decisions.rationale: A daily cron would otherwise stack an identical alert every 24h while the same drift persists. Suppressing a new insert while an open one exists keeps the operator inbox honest; resolving the alert (after reconciling) re-arms detection.
decisions.recommendation: If multiple distinct drifts should each be tracked separately, key the dedup on subscription id instead of kind.

features.successCriteria:
- No-op (skipped:no_stripe_key) when STRIPE_SECRET_KEY is unset
- active row absent from Stripe's active set → drift db_active_not_in_stripe
- past_due row present in Stripe's active set → drift db_past_due_active_in_stripe
- consistent rows → no drift, no alert
- drift → exactly one admin_alerts row (kind custodian-reconcile)
- drift while an open alert exists → no second row
- a Stripe API failure must not fail the digest (best-effort in the cron)
knownLimitations: Alert-only: drift is reported, not auto-corrected (deliberate — see decisions)
Runs at the digest's daily cadence, not the weekly cadence originally scoped (daily is cheap at this volume and catches drift sooner)
current_period_end is captured but not yet used — the check is presence-in-active-set, not renewal-date comparison
Cron registration on cron-job.org is a manual step (same as the digest itself); until then it runs whenever the digest is invoked

statusHistory.reason: Shipped as BACKLOG.md B8 / prod-readiness gap #10.

features.title: Sentry quota watchdog (digest-cron piggyback)
features.problem: Sentry is the practice's only error-reporting processor, on the free plan (~5000 errors/month). A bad deploy that throws on every request burns the quota silently, and once it's exhausted Sentry DROPS events — the one tool that would flag the problem goes blind exactly when it matters. Nothing watched the quota. Closes prod-readiness gap #8.
features.analysis: Two pure helpers + a thin IO wrapper, same shape as the custodian reconciliation. evaluateQuota(used, quota, threshold) decides whether 30-day usage crossed the threshold (guards quota<=0 so a misconfig can't divide-by-zero into an alert storm). sumErrorQuantity(json) extracts the total from a Sentry stats_v2 response, tolerant of a missing/changed shape (returns 0 rather than throwing). checkSentryQuota(env) does the IO: skip when SENTRY_AUTH_TOKEN/SENTRY_ORG unset, timed fetch of /organizations/{org}/stats_v2 (category=error, 30d), evaluate against SENTRY_MONTHLY_ERROR_QUOTA (default 5000), and write one admin_alerts row (kind sentry-quota) when over, deduped to one open alert. Runs from the daily digest cron as housekeeping #5.

decisions.decision: Configured monthly quota (default 5000) rather than introspecting Sentry's plan quota
decisions.rationale: Sentry's actual quota cap lives behind plan/subscription endpoints that vary by tier and are fragile to scrape. A single SENTRY_MONTHLY_ERROR_QUOTA var (defaulting to the free-plan 5000) is robust, obvious, and one edit away from matching a plan change.
decisions.recommendation: If the plan changes, update SENTRY_MONTHLY_ERROR_QUOTA in wrangler.toml [vars]. If Sentry ships a stable quota endpoint, swap the constant for a live read.

decisions.decision: Alert-only, deduped, on the digest cron — no dashboard, no new endpoint
decisions.rationale: Same reasoning as the custodian reconciliation: lowest surface, no new auth/CSRF path, and the daily digest is where the other housekeeping sweeps already live. One open alert at a time keeps the operator inbox honest.
decisions.recommendation: If quota pressure becomes a frequent, time-sensitive concern, add a /admin/today tile reading the same evaluation.

features.successCriteria:
- No-op (skipped:unconfigured) when SENTRY_AUTH_TOKEN or SENTRY_ORG is unset
- usage >= 80% of quota → over, one admin_alerts row (kind sentry-quota)
- usage < 80% → no alert
- over while an open sentry-quota alert exists → no second row
- quota<=0 misconfig never reports over
- malformed Sentry response → 0 usage (treated as not-over), no crash
- a Sentry API failure must not fail the digest
knownLimitations: Quota cap is a configured constant, not read live from Sentry (deliberate — see decisions)
Threshold is a fixed 80%; not env-tunable (one constant change if it should be)
Counts category=error only — does not watch transaction/replay/attachment quotas
Requires a Sentry auth token with org:read; that token + the cron registration are manual setup steps

statusHistory.reason: Shipped as BACKLOG.md B9 / prod-readiness gap #8.

features.title: Hero — the home-page cover (manifesto paragraph + live capacity)
features.problem: The Hero is the visitor's first paragraph of contact with the practice. It carries one empathy-first manifesto sentence ('Tu as une affaire plate qui revient chaque semaine...') and a live capacity readout (1 active + 1 in triage = open or paused). Capacity is fetched from /api/capacity at request time — visitor sees the truth, not a marketing claim.
features.analysis:
Renders DICT[lang].hero copy. One live data source fetched on mount: getCapacityLive (returns the current 1+1 cap state; flips the CTA label between 'Crée ton compte gratuit' and 'Rejoindre la liste d’attente'). Logged-in visitors see 'Démarrer une nouvelle proposition' instead, plus a 'Voir mes sessions' link that resolves to /me or /admin/inbox depending on isAdmin. The body is the h1 (LCP + SEO anchor); the closer is a sibling

styled identically so the two read as one paragraph visually but announce as two distinct blocks to screen readers. The closer span gets italic + sage emphasis. The h1 *is* the LCP element, so its first-paint animation is a colour transition (text-faint → text) rather than an opacity fade — a coloured element counts as contentful and LCP fires on the prerendered first paint while the ink visibly absorbs.

decisions.decision: Capacity is fetched live, not a static claim
decisions.rationale: Saying 'I take 1 active build at a time' is a structural claim — it must be testable. Showing the LIVE state turns it from claim into observation. Capacity cap race fix (AUDIT P1.7) makes this trustworthy.
decisions.recommendation: Don't switch to static copy. The live readout IS the trust statement.

decisions.decision: Empathy-first manifesto, not a self-introduction headline
decisions.rationale: Previous design opened with 'Marc-Antoine, là pour résoudre des problèmes importants pour ma communauté' — a self-introduction. Replaced with the empathy paragraph ('Tu as une affaire plate qui revient chaque semaine...') because the first sentence should name the visitor's situation, not Marc's. Marc gets the signature line. The closer 'Raconte-moi ça.' carries italic + sage emphasis so the eye lands on it before the CTA.
decisions.recommendation: Don't add back the 4-line display headline. The voice has to be 'tu' first, 'je' second.

decisions.decision: Drop the clock-aware greeting and VÉRIFIÉ stamp
decisions.rationale: Previous design carried a clock-aware greeting (‘encore là?’ late-night) + a VÉRIFIÉ stamp echoing the OG card. The redesign strips them: the greeting added a date/timestamp feel Marc cooled on, and the stamp competed with the open-paper layout. The stamp may return later as a watermark; the greeting is gone.
decisions.recommendation: Don't restore the stamp as a corner decoration — if it returns, it lives behind the headline as a watermark or it doesn't return.

decisions.decision: Remove the signature block; split closer into its own paragraph
decisions.rationale:
Design review (2026-05-27, second pass): the .hero__sig (sage hairline + small-caps name) sat between the closer and the CTA as a visual speed bump where the eye should be accelerating toward the button. Hero only needs to do three things — name the situation, name the response, take the click. Separately, the h1 was announcing both sentences (body + closer) as one heading; splitting the closer into a sibling

with shared typography keeps the visual paragraph intact while letting screen readers announce them as two blocks.
decisions.recommendation: Don't add the signature back. If a name credit is wanted, place it BELOW the CTA as a small mono right-aligned line so it doesn't gate the click.

features.successCriteria:
- Renders body h1 + closer paragraph + CTA + capacity pill
- Capacity readout reflects live D1 state
- CTA label flips for logged-in / waitlist / cold states
- Sessions link appears for logged-in visitors
- Bilingual at full parity (FR + EN body + closer + slot pill)
- One live fetch on mount
- Reduced-motion: ink-absorb skipped, paragraphs presented at rest
knownLimitations: One fetch on mount for capacity. If /api/capacity is down, the CTA defaults to the cold-state label (open), which is the right side of the fence for a hero that should never look broken on cold-start

statusHistory.reason: Component shipped with the offer + live capacity + greeting.
Code-first doc co-located. Live-capacity + clock-aware greeting stable.

Redesign: stripped 4-line display headline, clock-aware greeting, VÉRIFIÉ stamp, shipped counter, bilingual line, hand-drawn signature flourish, HeroShippedProject right-rail. Replaced with one empathy paragraph + closer + sage hairline + small-caps name. Anti-AI-tells pass on the copy (triadic noun list dropped).

features.title: /admin/email-outbox — pending email outbox viewer with manual retry
features.problem: Durable email sends (tier-assigned, refund-notice, installment-cleared, status-change, withdrawal-visitor) fail-and-enqueue into email_outbox. The daily digest sweepEmailOutbox retries with exponential backoff up to 5 attempts; rows stuck past the cap sit silently. Marc had no admin UI to see what's stuck or to force one more try. Closes prod-readiness gap #9.
features.analysis: Lazy-loaded route mounted under the existing Admin <Outlet>. Loads /api/admin/email-outbox on mount. Renders a single fleet-table with one row per pending outbox entry (to, kind+subject, attempts+stuck-tag, last_error, created_at, Retry button). Click Retry → POST { id } → on delivered, reload; on failure, surface the error inline next to the button. Per-row busy + result state kept in two records keyed by id; no global busy.

decisions.decision: Lazy-loaded route, not a panel under /admin/today
decisions.rationale: Operator-only diagnostic that's rarely visited but valuable during incidents. Keeping it off /admin/today avoids cluttering the dashboard with rows that 99% of the time are zero.
decisions.recommendation: If outbox volume becomes a chronic signal, add an outbox count widget to /admin/today's System health panel (already shows the count, just no link).

decisions.decision: Per-row inline result instead of a toast or modal
decisions.rationale: Retry is a low-frequency, high-context operation — Marc is troubleshooting one specific row. Inline result keeps the cause (last_error) and effect (new state) in the same eyeline. Toast/modal would obscure the row context.
decisions.recommendation: Same pattern in any other admin retry UI.

decisions.decision: No bulk retry button
decisions.rationale: Bulk retry on Resend incidents would either succeed (great) or hammer the API with retries we already know will fail. The daily cron's sweepEmailOutbox already serves the 'try them all on a schedule' use case; manual retry is for one-row triage.
decisions.recommendation: Reconsider only if Marc reports needing to retry 10+ rows in a single session. Bulk retry would also need rate-limit guards.

features.successCriteria:
- Loads pending outbox rows admin-only
- Click Retry → row delivers + drops off OR error shown inline
- Already-delivered row → 'Already sent' inline (idempotent)
- 403 → forbidden message; 5xx → error message + Reload button
- Empty outbox → 'Outbox is clear' empty state
- Both FR + EN copies (Québécois register for FR)
knownLimitations: List capped at 100 rows server-side; backlog past that is invisible
No delivered-history view; the sweep prunes delivered rows after 30 days
No bulk retry

statusHistory.reason: Shipped paired with /api/admin/email-outbox as Tier-A #9 of prod-readiness gap queue.

features.title: Admin today — one-glance operator dashboard
features.problem: Reconstructing 'what do I need to do today?' from the existing admin surfaces requires clicking through the hub, the inbox, the custodian board and the audit log — and even then the per-session 'next action' isn't surfaced anywhere; it lives in Marc's head. AdminToday collapses every signal the daily-digest email already aggregates into one page so the triage decision happens by eye, then links each row back to the focused surface (session page, custodian view) for the action itself.
features.analysis: Single round-trip: GET /api/admin/today returns sessions (with computed nextAction, last-message timestamps per author, build-leg payment counts, note snippet), overduePayments (build-kind pending >7d), slaBreaches (draft/triage >72h), unansweredMessages (visitor wrote, no operator reply, >24h), systemHealth (outbox stuck/pending, last-7d bounces+complaints, open admin_alerts, capacity), and custodianAlerts (past-due + recent-30d switches). Client renders one panel per section; capacity reads from the same payload (no second /api/capacity call). The nextAction inference (functions/_lib/nextAction.ts) is the pure-function brain — given session + payment + message timestamps, returns one of 13 NextActionCodes plus severity + FR/EN copy. The dashboard renders the label by code so a new branch flows through automatically.

decisions.decision: Single /api/admin/today endpoint, not five parallel calls
decisions.rationale: The dashboard is read-only and needs everything-or-nothing — a partial render (e.g. sessions loaded but health pending) is worse than the loading spinner. One endpoint also lets the server share the sessions query across the SLA/unanswered/per-session subsections instead of making the client recompute. The capacity cap keeps the dataset small enough that the multi-table aggregation is cheap.
decisions.recommendation: When a new dashboard signal lands, add it to /api/admin/today rather than minting a new endpoint. Resist the urge to refactor into per-section endpoints unless one section gets expensive enough to justify its own cache.

decisions.decision: nextAction is a pure function (functions/_lib/nextAction.ts), not inlined in the handler
decisions.rationale: The 'what should Marc do next?' inference will outgrow the dashboard — it's the natural input for a future digest summary line, a push notification, or an LLM context blurb. Pulling it out as a pure function with explicit NextActionContext means the dashboard handler stays a data-aggregation shell and the action logic gets unit-tested in isolation.
decisions.recommendation: When adding a new branch, extend NextActionCode + the inferNextAction precedence chain, then add FR/EN LABELS + HINTS. Don't fork into a parallel inference fn — precedence ordering is the contract.

decisions.decision: Operator-notes table read in the same query, with graceful pre-migration fallback
decisions.rationale: The note snippet (first 200 chars) ships in each TodaySessionEntry so the dashboard can show 'there's context to read here' without a second request. Migration 0028 may land in a separate deploy from the handler — wrapping the read in try/catch on /no such table/ matches the tenants pre-migration pattern in _middleware.ts and keeps /admin/today rendering during the window.
decisions.recommendation: Any new admin-data table read by /admin/today should follow the same fallback — a missing table degrades to 'no signal for this section', not a 5xx.

decisions.decision: Capacity panel reads from the dashboard payload, not /api/capacity
decisions.rationale: The dashboard already loads in one round-trip; calling /api/capacity in parallel would mean a second roundtrip + the risk of momentary disagreement between the two reads (e.g. an admission landed between them). Including the capacity numbers in the dashboard's systemHealth section keeps the page internally consistent.
decisions.recommendation: Don't add a /api/capacity call from this page. If the cap rule itself changes, edit the SQL in today.ts and the docs in this feature — the cap is structural (see CLAUDE.md).

features.successCriteria:
- One GET /api/admin/today returns the full payload; client renders in one pass
- Each panel has explicit empty state (the dashboard is *useful* when nothing's wrong)
- nextAction labels render in the visitor's lang (FR/EN parity)
- Per-row links land on the right focused surface (session page / custodian view)
- Pre-migration deploy of operator_notes degrades to no-snippet, no 500
- Capacity panel agrees with the home page's /api/capacity (they share a count source via shared SQL semantics — see decisions)
knownLimitations: No auto-refresh — the page reads on mount; if Marc leaves it open all day the numbers staling are on him. Adding a 60s poll would be one useState/useEffect away but the current operator routine is open-page → act → close.
Sort within sections is server-side; the client doesn't expose alternative sorts. Single-operator scale doesn't need them yet.
No filter UI — the dataset is small (capacity cap = 2 live sessions), so Ctrl+F suffices.
Email-event volume is a 7-day rolling count, not a trend chart. The /admin/audit page is the deeper view if signals look off.

statusHistory.reason: Dashboard shipped — handler + page + 38 backend tests passing.

features.title: GET/POST /api/admin/email-outbox — pending outbox view + manual retry (operator only)
features.problem: Durable email sends fail-and-enqueue into email_outbox. The daily digest sweepEmailOutbox retries them with exponential backoff up to OUTBOX_MAX_ATTEMPTS=5, after which a row sits silently — the daily digest alerts once but the row stays stuck. Marc had no admin UI to view the current outbox state or to manually retry a row past the cap. Closes prod-readiness gap #9.
features.analysis: Two handlers in one file. GET: currentEmail → unauthorized; isAdmin → forbidden; SELECT pending rows (sent_at IS NULL) ordered attempts DESC then created_at ASC, limit 100. Graceful degrade on 'no such table' (pre-migration). POST: same auth, JSON body { id }, SELECT row, sendRaw with stored payload, UPDATE sent_at on delivery or attempts+1 + last_error on failure. Manual retry intentionally bypasses the attempts ceiling (operator explicit intent) and the backoff window.

decisions.decision: Reuse exported sendRaw from _lib/email.ts
decisions.rationale: sweepEmailOutbox already uses sendRaw for the same shape (rendered payload, pure POST). Exporting it for the manual-retry path keeps the network primitive in one file and ensures parity with the automated sweep. Building a separate retryOutboxRow helper would be premature — one caller, sweeper has its own batched loop.
decisions.recommendation: If a third caller appears (e.g. an SQS-style worker queue), promote to a retryOutboxRow helper then. Don't pre-build the abstraction.

decisions.decision: Manual retry bypasses OUTBOX_MAX_ATTEMPTS and backoff window
decisions.rationale: The whole point of the manual endpoint is to give the operator an escape hatch. Honoring the cap would make the UI button useless on the exact rows that need it most.
decisions.recommendation: If retries-past-cap become a regular workflow (not occasional incident response), revisit whether the cap itself needs to be raised.

decisions.decision: POST gates on RESEND_API_KEY
decisions.rationale: Without Resend configured the retry can't possibly succeed. Returning 503 is more useful than pretending success and silently no-oping.
decisions.recommendation: Same shape as other Resend-dependent endpoints — graceful degrade with a clear message.

features.successCriteria:
- Operator gets list of pending rows (sent_at IS NULL)
- Non-operator → 403
- Anon → 401
- POST { id } retries the row and updates DB
- 404 on unknown id
- 200 + alreadySent:true on already-delivered row (idempotent)
- 503 when RESEND_API_KEY unset
- Graceful degrade on pre-migration env (returns empty list)
knownLimitations: List capped at 100 rows; backlog past that is invisible (acceptable — outbox never grows past ~10 at current scale)
No delivered-history view (sent_at IS NOT NULL rows hidden); the sweep prunes delivered rows after 30 days anyway
No bulk retry button — manual retry is one row at a time

statusHistory.reason: Endpoint + page shipped together as Tier-A #9 of prod-readiness gap queue.

features.title: Operator notes — admin-only per-session scratch pad
features.problem: The session message thread is visitor-facing — the operator can't write 'remember to push back on scope tomorrow' or 'visitor is travelling until Wed' there without the visitor seeing it. Marc's been keeping this kind of context in his head, which works at one-active-session scale until a few days off and the next-action isn't obvious anymore. The /admin/today dashboard surfaces *what* the next action is; this surface stores the *why* behind it.
features.analysis: One row per session (operator_notes.session_id is PK). GET returns null gracefully when no row exists. PUT upserts via INSERT … ON CONFLICT (session_id) DO UPDATE; empty/whitespace body folds into a DELETE so an emptied textarea tidies up instead of persisting nothing. DELETE removes the row outright. Body capped at 4 KB *bytes* (not chars — emoji can bloat past 4096 chars while staying under 4 KB, or vice-versa). Cascade-deleted with the parent session via FK ON DELETE CASCADE, so a /api/me erasure cleans this up for free.

decisions.decision: Single PUT endpoint, empty-body folds into delete
decisions.rationale: Two endpoints (PUT for content, DELETE for clear) is the strictly-correct REST split — but the UX is a textarea with a Save button. Most real 'clear this' moments will be 'select all → backspace → save', not a separate Clear-and-Save flow. Folding empty-string PUT into DELETE means the textarea-save path always Does The Right Thing. DELETE still exists for the explicit Clear button on the panel.
decisions.recommendation: Don't add a 'soft-clear' state. The note is either present or not; there's no preserved-but-hidden state.

decisions.decision: 4 KB byte ceiling enforced in the handler, not via SQLite CHECK
decisions.rationale: SQLite's CHECK on length() runs per write — the application layer is the right boundary because we want to return 413 cleanly with a JSON error, not surface a DB-constraint error. The number 4096 is generous enough that no realistic operator memo trips it (a 4 KB note is ~600 words) while bounding a paste-bomb.
decisions.recommendation: If a note routinely hits the cap, the real fix is decomposing — the note is becoming a doc and belongs in a different surface, not a bigger field.

decisions.decision: Pre-migration fallback on GET (try/catch 'no such table')
decisions.rationale: Same pattern as the tenants table in _middleware.ts. If migration 0028 lands a few minutes after the handler in a deploy, the SessionPage operator panel mounts cleanly with an empty textarea instead of throwing in the user's face.
decisions.recommendation: Mirror this fallback on any new admin-data table that the operator panel reads optimistically.

features.successCriteria:
- GET returns {note: null} when no row, never 404
- PUT with non-empty body upserts and returns the canonical note
- PUT with empty/whitespace body deletes the row and returns {note: null}
- DELETE always returns {note: null} (idempotent)
- Body >4 KB returns 413
- All three methods admin-gated (currentEmail → isAdmin), 401/403 otherwise
- PUT/DELETE flow through the standard CSRF gate (not in CSRF_EXEMPT_PATHS)
- Cascade-deletes with the parent session (/api/me erasure cleans it)
knownLimitations: No history — every save overwrites. If recovering a prior version matters, paste it into the visitor thread or store elsewhere. Single-operator scale, not worth a versioning layer today.
No formatting beyond plain text — no markdown rendering, no link auto-linking. Adding rich text would mean a sanitization story; the field stays plain to stay simple.
Single body per session — no thread of timestamped entries. If a session ages enough that the note becomes a journal, the right move is to summarize-in-place rather than expand the schema.

statusHistory.reason: Endpoint + panel + migration shipped; tests passing.

features.title: submitIntake — two-phase intake submit orchestrator (session POST + napkin upload)
features.problem: Pre-P1.8 the intake submit was one POST: intake_json carried the whole payload, including the napkin PNG as a base64 data URL. That worked while sketches were tiny but blew the intake size cap as soon as visitors drew busier napkins, and was the wrong shape regardless (a multi-MB blob doesn't belong in a JSON column). After P1.8 the PNG lives as a separate kind='napkin' R2-backed attachment, but the intake client now has TWO ordered POSTs to coordinate: POST /api/sessions to create the row, then POST /api/sessions/:id/attachments?kind=napkin to upload the snapshot. This helper hides that ordering from the call sites (Intake.tsx signed-in submit + MePortal.tsx post-magic-link finalize) and standardizes the failure shape — a napkin upload failure does NOT abort the submit (the session is the load-bearing part), it returns alongside the session row so the caller can log to Sentry and proceed.
features.analysis: Pure async helper. splitNapkinPng() walks the intake payload, finds napkin.png (a data URL), pulls it out into a separate variable, and returns the payload-without-png alongside it (the scene + caption + savedAt stay on the napkin object). When no napkin is present, or png is already a URL (resumed flow), the splitter returns the payload unchanged with napkinPng=null. The orchestrator: calls createSession with payloadSansPng; on success, if napkinPng is set, calls uploadNapkin (which converts the data URL to a Blob via fetch(dataUrl) and POSTs multipart). The return shape is { session, napkinUploadError? }: a session POST failure throws (caller handles), a napkin-upload failure resolves with the session + the error string. A 409 from the napkin upload is treated as silent success — the only way to hit that is a resumed submit where the napkin is already there.

decisions.decision: Napkin failure does not abort the submit
decisions.rationale: The session record is the load-bearing primitive. A visitor whose session was created but whose napkin upload failed (transient R2 hiccup, malformed PNG, per-session quota) is still in a recoverable state — they can re-draw and re-upload via a future session-view affordance, or via re-submission. Aborting the whole submit on a napkin failure would mean losing the session row + the visitor's typed answers for a cosmetic-tier asset.
decisions.recommendation: If a future product decision makes the napkin load-bearing (e.g. mandatory for certain problem types), the abort behavior moves here, not into the caller — keep the failure-shape decision in one place.

decisions.decision: Treat 409 from napkin upload as silent success
decisions.rationale: The only way to legitimately hit 409 (one-per-session) on the napkin endpoint is a resumed/double-clicked submit where the napkin is already attached. The session POST is idempotent at the visitor level (a fresh submit gets a new session id), so 409 here ALWAYS means 'this exact session already has its napkin' — not an error worth surfacing.
decisions.recommendation: Don't widen this to other 4xx codes. 415 (wrong content-type) or 413 (too big) ARE worth bubbling to the caller for a human-facing message.

decisions.decision: Strip png from the napkin object, leave scene + caption + savedAt
decisions.rationale: The editable Excalidraw scene is small (a few hundred KB max) and worth keeping inline in intake_json so SessionPage can re-open the canvas without a second round-trip. Only the PNG is heavyweight enough to deserve R2; the scene is the source of truth, the PNG is its snapshot.
decisions.recommendation: If the scene ever grows enough to bust the intake cap on its own, the same split-pattern applies: move it to R2 as kind='sketch-scene' and leave only the metadata inline.

features.successCriteria:
- Strips napkin.png from intakeJson before the session POST (the request never carries the data URL)
- Session POST failure throws ApiError, caller handles capacity 409 / network etc.
- Napkin upload failure resolves with { session, napkinUploadError } — does NOT throw
- 409 from napkin upload is silent success
- No-napkin and already-a-URL payloads round-trip unchanged
knownLimitations: No automatic retry on napkin upload failure — the failure modes that actually fire (R2 outage, magic-byte rejection, per-session quota) aren't helped by client-side retry. The redo-from-draft path is the recovery story
Visitor sees no in-app notice when the napkin upload silently fails; only Sentry sees it. A future affordance on the session view would close this
The data URL → Blob conversion uses fetch(dataUrl), which silently returns an empty blob for malformed data URLs. The server then 400s on the empty file; surfaced as napkinUploadError
splitNapkinPng is naive about deeply nested napkin shapes — assumes the top-level intakePayload.napkin.png path

statusHistory.reason: Shipped with AUDIT P1.8 — the two-phase submit pattern is in production from day 1. Stays active until the first wave of real intakes validates the failure-shape rationale; needs a re-freeze once the napkin retry UX (if any) is wired.

features.title: POST /api/admin/digest — daily-cron operator summary email + housekeeping piggyback
features.problem: Pages Functions don't have scheduled triggers natively. A free external cron (cron-job.org, a CF Workers scheduled trigger in a separate worker) hits this endpoint once a day to do two things: (1) compile a digest email for Marc — any triage rows over 48h old + any unresolved admin_alerts (Stripe-notification fallbacks from the webhook's maybeNotifyAdmin) — and (2) piggyback four housekeeping sweeps that need a daily trigger: prune expired magic-link tokens, cancel stale pending Stripe payments, GC orphan attachment uploads (kind != 'napkin'), and run the email outbox (AUDIT P1.3 — retry pending durable sends, alert on rows that JUST hit OUTBOX_MAX_ATTEMPTS, prune delivered rows past OUTBOX_DELIVERED_TTL_SECONDS). Marc's daily operational alarm without paying for a hosted scheduler.
features.analysis: POST only. Auth: requires X-Digest-Token header matching env.DIGEST_TOKEN — out-of-band so no human cookie is required (a cron job posts without a browser). CSRF-exempt in middleware. Idempotent — calling twice in the same hour just sends two emails (acceptable; the external cron typically fires once daily). Body: query D1 for triage sessions WHERE updated_at < now() - 48h AND deleted_at IS NULL; query admin_alerts WHERE resolved_at IS NULL. Build the email; send via Resend to primaryAdminEmail (functions/_lib/sessions.ts) in the right lang (getLang(adminEmail)). 200 ok on send, 500 on Resend failure.

decisions.decision: Out-of-band auth (X-Digest-Token) instead of cookie
decisions.rationale: The caller is a cron job, not a browser. No cookie context, so the standard auth doesn't apply. A shared-secret header is the simplest auth for server-to-server calls; the token lives in CF secrets and the cron job's config. Compromise on either side means rotating the token.
decisions.recommendation: Keep the token-header pattern. If a second cron-triggered endpoint is added, reuse the same secret name + header.

decisions.decision: admin_alerts are surfaced but have no UI to mark resolved
decisions.rationale: admin_alerts rows are inserted when the webhook's maybeNotifyAdmin can't reach Marc via the primary channel (Resend down, etc.) — rare but real. The digest re-surfaces them so they don't vanish. There's no UI to mark resolved because the volume is so low (single-digit per year). Marc manually clears resolved entries in D1 when fixing the underlying issue.
decisions.recommendation: Don't build a UI. If volume crosses ~10/year, evaluate.

features.successCriteria:
- X-Digest-Token validates against env.DIGEST_TOKEN; mismatch → 401
- Triage rows older than 48h are listed in the email
- Unresolved admin_alerts are listed in the email
- Idempotent — twice-fired produces two emails, no DB mutation
- Send via Resend; failures return 500 (cron should retry)
- 200 + summary on success
knownLimitations: External cron is a separate failure mode — if cron-job.org is down, no digest. Mitigation: receive emails for active sessions via /api/sessions's notification side effects
Digest content is hand-curated in the handler; a new alert type requires code change
Idempotency is a feature for the visitor's mailbox (no duplicate emails on cron retry); but it ALSO means we can't deduplicate alerts across multiple sends
No quiet hours; cron fires whenever cron-job.org fires

statusHistory.reason: Endpoint shipped with the daily-digest content + DIGEST_TOKEN auth.
Code-first doc co-located. External-cron + token-header pattern stable.

AUDIT P1.3: digest now also sweeps email_outbox (retry pending sends, alert on hard-failed rows, prune delivered ones past TTL). Touches sweepEmailOutbox in functions/_lib/email.ts; needs a re-freeze after the first production sweep validates the cadence.

features.title: POST /api/payments/checkout — mint a Stripe Checkout session
features.problem: When the client clicks 'pay' on a session in pricing status, this endpoint mints a Stripe Checkout URL the browser redirects to. Stripe takes it from there (card form, 3DS, etc.) and pings /api/payments/webhook on completion. Three kinds: 'build' (next unpaid installment of the session's tier 1–4), 'scoping' ($250 scoping report, credited to leg 1), 'custodian' (annual subscription, Watch or Care plan). The server owns all pricing math — the client never sends an amount or a leg index, only the kind. That's the load-bearing property: any pricing change is one server-side update.
features.analysis: POST handler taking { sessionId, kind, custodianPlan?, lang?, amountCadOverride? }. Auth: canAccessSession() (visitor self or admin). Pricing: buildInstallmentPlan() (functions/_lib/pricing.ts) reads the session's tier and the paid rows, computes the next unpaid leg + its amount with scoping credit applied. For 'custodian' kind, it picks the right Stripe Price ID from STRIPE_CUSTODIAN_WATCH_PRICE / _CARE_PRICE env vars. For 'scoping', uses CUSTODIAN_CENTS (the canonical $250 in CAD cents). amountCadOverride is admin-only and bypasses the plan computation for one-off cases. Inserts a payments row with status='pending' before calling Stripe; returns { url, paymentId } so the client knows which payment row the eventual webhook will resolve. STRIPE_SECRET_KEY guard: if unset, returns 503 with a clear message (graceful-degrade pattern). Same shape for missing custodian Price IDs.

decisions.decision: Server owns pricing math; client sends only the kind
decisions.rationale: A client-sent amount is a security and consistency liability — a malicious client could send $1 for a $5000 leg, or a buggy client could compute the leg index wrong and pay the wrong installment. The server reads the session's tier + paid rows, computes the next leg, and uses the canonical price. The client only signals intent ('I want to pay'); the math is server-only. AUDIT P1.7's same lesson applied here.
decisions.recommendation: Don't accept client amounts. amountCadOverride exists as an admin escape for one-off cases (Tier 4 custom quotes) but is strictly admin-gated.

decisions.decision: Scoping credit applies to leg 1 automatically
decisions.rationale: The $250 scoping report is a separate kind but its value is credited against the next build's leg 1. Without automatic crediting, the visitor would pay scoping then see their leg 1 unchanged — they'd need to remember to ask, or the admin would manually adjust. Crediting in buildInstallmentPlan removes this paper-shuffling completely; the math is consistent.
decisions.recommendation: Keep the credit in buildInstallmentPlan() — that's the canonical pricing module. Don't split scoping into its own column on payments; the link is by session + kind.

decisions.decision: 503 with a clear message if STRIPE_SECRET_KEY missing, not a generic 500
decisions.rationale: The Stripe binding is optional (graceful-degrade pattern across the codebase). If a dev environment doesn't have Stripe configured, the rest of the app works; only payments are unavailable. 503 'payments unavailable' is the truthful response shape — 500 would suggest a bug. Same pattern for missing custodian Price IDs (which would otherwise produce a confusing 'no such Price' from Stripe).
decisions.recommendation: When adding any new Stripe dependency, follow the same pattern: env check at handler entry, 503 with a useful message if missing.

decisions.decision: Community discount (20% off build tiers) is applied to the tier TOTAL before the installment split, not per-leg after
decisions.rationale: Applying per-leg-after means the 40/40/20 split's rounding remainder could shift cents between legs unpredictably. Applying to the total once means the legs continue to be exact proportions of the (discounted) total — visitor sees three evenly-discounted amounts on the Stripe receipt, not 60% / 60% / 30.07%. Same defense as the original 40/40/20 invariant: legs sum to exactly the total.
decisions.recommendation: If a second discount type ever ships (étudiant, OBNL fédéré, etc.), thread it through the same buildInstallmentPlan(community) signature rather than stacking discount math at the handler level.

decisions.decision: Community discount does NOT apply to scoping or custodian payments
decisions.rationale: Scoping is a fixed $250 acquisition product; the 20% off would dilute it below the cost of running the scoping conversation. Custodian is a Stripe-Price subscription, not an ad-hoc amount — discounting it would require a Stripe Coupon, new env vars, a separate Loi 25 PIA mention, and a more complex toggle UX. v1 scope is build tiers only; the boolean flag carries that contract.
decisions.recommendation: Don't widen the discount surface without an explicit user-facing reason. The CommunityDiscountToggle component's copy + the Pricing.tsx communityNote both say 'build tiers only' — that copy is the operator's contract with the visitor.

decisions.decision: Freeze the community flag once any build leg is paid (atomic NOT EXISTS guard in PATCH /api/sessions/:id)
decisions.rationale: A toggle after the first paid leg would mean leg N+1 is charged at a different rate than legs 1..N. The visitor sees an inconsistent receipt (or worse, retroactive disagreement). Same shape as AUDIT P1.7's capacity-cap fix: fold the precondition into the UPDATE's WHERE so two concurrent toggles can't race. Returns 409 with a 'frozen' message; the admin must refund the paid leg first if a correction is truly needed.
decisions.recommendation: Keep the atomic guard in the UPDATE. Don't add a read-then-write helper. The CommunityDiscountToggle catches the 409 and renders the frozen-state hint inline.

features.successCriteria:
- A signed-in client (or admin) can mint a Checkout URL for build / scoping / custodian
- Pricing comes from the server's buildInstallmentPlan; client cannot override (except admin via amountCadOverride)
- Scoping payments credit leg 1 automatically
- STRIPE_SECRET_KEY unset → 503 with a clear message
- Stripe Checkout URL is returned + a paymentId for the eventual webhook to resolve
- Tier 4 (custom-quote) sessions cannot mint a checkout without an admin amount override
- Tests exercise the happy path + the unset-key path
knownLimitations: Tier 4 always requires an admin amount override; visitors can't self-serve a Tier 4 checkout. By design — Tier 4 IS the custom-quote tier

If STRIPE_SECRET_KEY is set but the webhook secret is wrong, the checkout flow completes but the webhook can't verify the event signature. The webhook returns 200 (to prevent Stripe retry storms) but the payment row stays pending. RUNBOOK §Stripe covers this triage
Custodian Price IDs are env vars; if a Price ID changes in Stripe (e.g. price renamed), this handler will mint a checkout pointing at the stale ID until the env is updated
Scoping credit is applied at plan-computation time only; an admin manually creating a leg 1 payments row would not get the credit automatically
Stripe Checkout sessions expire 24h after creation; clicking a stale 'pay' link would 404 on Stripe's side. The SPA's PaymentActions re-mints on each click

Community discount toggling is frozen once any build leg is paid (server returns 409). For a correction after that, the admin must issue a Stripe refund first (which marks the row 'refunded'), then the freeze releases and the flag becomes toggleable again

statusHistory.reason: Endpoint shipped with all three kinds (build / scoping / custodian) and the scoping-credit logic.
Code-first doc co-located. Server-side-pricing and graceful-degrade patterns stable; e2e harness stub is the canonical test path.

Adding community-pricing flag (sessions.community_discount → 20% off build tiers 1-4). Touches buildInstallmentPlan signature + installmentLabel + payment summary response; needs re-freeze once the operator has verified the discount math against a real Stripe receipt.

features.title: GET /api/payments — per-session payment summary
features.problem: The /me dashboard and /session/:id both need a unified view of what's been paid, what's owed, what subscription state the session is in. Per-row Stripe queries would be slow and leak Stripe-specific concepts into the SPA. This endpoint returns a single summary per session: { rows (paid history), custodianStatus, stripeMode, build (next-unpaid-leg with amount), scoping (paid or unpaid status) } — all the math done server-side, the client just renders.
features.analysis: GET ?sessionId=X. Auth: canAccessSession(). loadSession() + a query against the payments table for this session's rows. buildInstallmentPlan() (functions/_lib/pricing.ts) computes the build leg + amount with scoping credit applied, OR signals 'Tier-4 quote pending' if the session is Tier 4 without an admin-set amount. stripeMode is derived from the secret key prefix ('sk_test_' → 'test', 'sk_live_' → 'live') and surfaces as a visible UI pill so visitors don't mistake test charges for real. custodianStatus is the session row's custodian_status column ('none' | 'active' | 'past_due' | 'canceled' | 'switched_to_tout_a_toi'). The whole payload is consistent with what the webhook writes; this endpoint is purely read-side.

decisions.decision: Server returns the next unpaid leg's amount; client never computes
decisions.rationale: Same lesson as /api/payments/checkout: client-computed amounts are a security and consistency liability. The summary returns the canonical amount the client should render; checkout uses the same buildInstallmentPlan() to compute when minting. Single source of truth for pricing math.
decisions.recommendation: Don't let the client compute 'days until renewal' or any other derived value here. If the UI needs it, surface it in this payload.

decisions.decision: Expose stripeMode so the UI can render a 'TEST MODE' pill
decisions.rationale: In dev, the Stripe secret is sk_test_… — charges are fake. Without a visible 'TEST MODE' indicator, a developer doing manual QA could think they were on prod. A visible mode pill prevents that confusion. The mode is derived from the key prefix; if the prefix is unknown (e.g. partial key during setup), report 'unknown' and the UI hides the pill.
decisions.recommendation: Keep the mode in the response. Don't compute it client-side from anything else — the key prefix is the truth.

decisions.decision: BuildSummary carries a `community: boolean` field mirroring sessions.community_discount
decisions.rationale: The next-leg amount in this summary is the server-computed, already-discounted figure when the flag is on. The client needs to render a 'tarif communautaire' tag + the regular-price anchor next to the discounted amount; without the flag in the summary, the UI would have to re-fetch the session row or compute the discount client-side. Carrying the boolean keeps the 'server is the single source of truth for pricing math' decision honest while letting the client describe what it shows.
decisions.recommendation: When a new operator-set pricing modifier ships (étudiant, OBNL fédéré, etc.), thread it through the same pattern: a boolean on the summary, server applies the math, client renders the explanatory chrome.

features.successCriteria:
- A signed-in visitor (or admin) gets a complete payment summary for any session they can access
- buildInstallmentPlan computes the next leg + amount; Tier 4 returns 'quote pending'
- stripeMode is derived from the secret key prefix
- custodianStatus reflects the latest webhook state
- The payload is suitable for direct render in /me's PaymentActions component
- 404 for missing or unauthorized session
knownLimitations: No pagination on rows — a long-running custodian with many invoices accumulates rows. At current scale (custodian is annual, max 1 invoice/year per sub), not a concern
stripeMode = 'unknown' fallback may surface during initial deploys before STRIPE_SECRET_KEY is set; the UI hides the pill in that case
Currency is implied (CAD); a future multi-currency change would require schema work + this payload's structure to carry currency per row
The summary doesn't include refund history detail — only the rolled-up refunded_amount_cents on each row. Stripe Dashboard has the granular history

statusHistory.reason: Endpoint shipped with the full summary structure used by /me's PaymentActions.
Code-first doc co-located. Summary shape is stable; server-side-math posture is consistent across the payments family.

BuildSummary gained a `community: boolean` field so the client can render the discount tag + the regular-price anchor without re-fetching the session row. Needs re-freeze once the operator has verified the end-to-end flow against a live Stripe receipt.

features.title: GET + POST /api/sessions/:id/attachments — upload file/voice/sketch/napkin
features.problem: Messages on a session can carry attachments — documents, voice notes (with auto-transcription), Excalidraw scenes. The intake step can attach a napkin (PNG snapshot of the visitor's sketch, session-scoped, one per session). POST handles upload (one per request, multipart/form-data), deciding the kind from content type by default OR from an explicit `?kind=napkin` query param: file (streamed to R2), voice (buffered, magic-byte checked, transcribed via Workers AI, stored), sketch (buffered, parsed + shape-checked, stored), napkin (image/png streamed to R2, one-per-session enforced, exempt from the orphan-attachment sweep). Thread attachments are uploaded BEFORE the message that references them; the messages POST then links them by id. GET lists unlinked uploads the current actor has made for this session — useful for resumed flows where the user navigated away mid-message.
features.analysis: POST: canAccessSession + parse multipart. Content type drives the branch — application/* → file kind, audio/* → voice, application/json with shape → sketch. Each branch validates: file streams to R2 (memory-thin to avoid bloating worker memory); voice buffers, magic-byte check via attachments _lib helper, optional Workers AI transcription (if env.AI binding present), store both bytes (R2) + transcript (D1 column); sketch buffers, parse JSON, shape-check Excalidraw scene structure, store as text in D1 (small, no R2 trip). All write an attachments row with message_id = NULL (pre-link state). Per-session storage quota enforced (MAX_ATTACHMENT_BYTES_PER_SESSION) — AUDIT P2.17 work. Per-attachment size limit too. GET: SELECT WHERE session_id = ? AND author = (currentEmail-derived) AND message_id IS NULL — only the actor's own unlinked uploads, so admin and visitor can't see each other's pre-message drafts.

decisions.decision: Two-phase upload: attachments uploaded separately, then linked at message send
decisions.rationale: Same lesson as the messages handler: multipart message+attachments fails on intermittent connections and large files. Separate uploads stream independently; retries don't lose message text. The message POST is small + fast once attachments are in place.
decisions.recommendation: Don't go back to single-shot multipart. Two-phase is the right scale-friendly shape.

decisions.decision: Magic-byte validation, not extension or content-type trust
decisions.rationale: An attacker uploading a 'document.pdf' that's actually JS could embed unsafe content. Magic-byte sniffing (the first few bytes of every well-known file format) is the real-content check. Files that don't match a known magic-byte signature are rejected. Coupled with R2's role as inert blob storage (we never execute these files server-side), the attack surface is minimal.
decisions.recommendation: When adding support for a new file type, add the magic-byte signature to the _lib/attachments helper. Don't grow the allow-list without it.

decisions.decision: Voice transcription is opportunistic, not required
decisions.rationale: Workers AI is an optional binding. If unset, voice attachments still upload + store — they just lack transcripts. The voice playback works fine; only the searchable text is missing. Graceful-degrade pattern. The transcript column can be backfilled later via a one-shot script if Workers AI becomes available.
decisions.recommendation: If transcription is ever required (not optional), the right answer is to fail the upload, not silently store without transcript. Today opportunistic is correct.

decisions.decision: Napkin kind requires explicit ?kind=napkin — the sniffer never picks it
decisions.rationale: A napkin PNG looks identical to any other image/png to the content-type sniffer. The kind value carries one-per-session + orphan-sweep-exempt semantics that don't belong on a generic thread attachment; if the sniffer could elect napkin, a visitor's screenshot-as-attachment would silently become THE napkin. Explicit opt-in from the intake client keeps the semantics honest.
decisions.recommendation: If a 'replace napkin' flow ever appears, layer it as a DELETE-then-POST (admin-gated) rather than allowing two simultaneous napkins or in-place overwrite.

features.successCriteria:
- Each kind (file / voice / sketch) validates its own content shape before storing
- Magic-byte sniffing rejects mislabeled files
- Per-session storage quota enforced; over-quota → 413
- R2 binding unset → 503 (graceful-degrade)
- Voice attachments upload even when AI binding unset (transcript = null)
- GET returns only the current actor's own unlinked uploads for this session
- Each upload is one row in attachments with message_id = NULL until linked
knownLimitations: One attachment per POST — clients must call N times for N attachments. Acceptable; rarely > 3 per message in practice
R2 deletion is best-effort on the DELETE path (see [attId] handler); orphan blobs are GC'd by the digest cron
Magic-byte allow-list is hand-curated; a legitimate file type not on the list is rejected. Adding one is a code change
Workers AI inference is metered per call; abuse via voice spam would cost. Rate limit on the messages POST is the upstream defense
Sketch JSON shape-check is structural, not adversarial — a malformed-but-valid-shape sketch could store unexpected data. Sketches are inert (we don't execute the JSON), so the risk is bounded

statusHistory.reason: Endpoint shipped with all three kinds; two-phase upload + magic-byte + size cap all in.
Per-session storage quota added (AUDIT P2.17).
Code-first doc co-located. Two-phase upload + magic-byte + quota patterns all stable.

AUDIT P1.8: napkin moved from intake_json into R2 as a new AttachmentKind. Handler now accepts ?kind=napkin override (one-per-session, image/png only); needs a re-freeze pass once the intake flow has shipped in production for a couple of submits.

features.title: POST /api/webhooks/resend — bounce/complaint events from Resend
features.problem: Resend fires webhooks when a recipient's mail server tells it something meaningful about a previously-delivered message: hard bounce, soft bounce, spam complaint, scheduled-delivery delayed, etc. Without ingesting these we keep sending magic-link / tier-assigned / refund emails to addresses the rest of the internet has decided are dead — burning sender reputation along the way. This handler is the ingest endpoint: it verifies Resend's Svix-style signature, dedupes via the shared webhook_events table, and writes a row per bounce/complaint/delivered/delayed/opened/clicked into email_events. Code-only landing: P1.1 (custom-domain DNS) gates real events flowing in, so the handler stays inert (503 while RESEND_WEBHOOK_SECRET is unset).
features.analysis: POST only. Auth: Svix-style HMAC-SHA-256 over `${svix-id}.${svix-timestamp}.${raw-body}` using RESEND_WEBHOOK_SECRET (whsec_ prefix stripped if present). 5-minute timestamp tolerance to prevent replay. Constant-time signature compare. Header `svix-signature` is a space-separated list of `<version>,<sig>` pairs — accept on any v1 match (rotated keys). CSRF-exempt in middleware. Idempotency: INSERT OR IGNORE into webhook_events keyed on the Svix event id; duplicate returns 200 + duplicate=true with no email_events row. Informational `email.sent` is accepted (dedupe row) but dropped (no email_events row). Anything in INGEST_TYPES (delivered, delivery_delayed, bounced, complained, opened, clicked) writes a row carrying the recipient + type + subtype (bounce_type / reason / subtype field extracted from the data payload) + raw payload TEXT (truncated to 16KB defensively). 503 when secret unset, 401 on signature mismatch, 200 on every other outcome so Resend doesn't retry into backoff.

decisions.decision: Land the handler before activation (DNS-blocked) instead of waiting
decisions.rationale: The handler is the longest part of the work; DNS records + dashboard secret deploy are 5 minutes total once DNS access is available. Shipping inert lets the activation be a single-sitting flip without a code change. The 503 shape while RESEND_WEBHOOK_SECRET is unset means a stray request in the meantime is a non-event.
decisions.recommendation: If Resend ever changes its webhook signing scheme away from Svix, the verifier is the only piece to swap — the dedupe + ingest + 503-graceful-degrade structure stays.

decisions.decision: Reuse webhook_events table for dedupe instead of a per-source table
decisions.rationale: Stripe events are evt_..., Resend events are msg_... — no namespace collision in practice. One table = one INSERT OR IGNORE pattern that future webhook sources (CF Email Routing, support inbox?) plug into without schema work.
decisions.recommendation: If a third webhook source ever shares an id namespace with one of these, add a 'source' column. Not worth doing pre-emptively.

decisions.decision: Persist the raw payload TEXT, not just a structured projection
decisions.rationale: Resend's payload shape varies per event type and per version; modeling every field at the schema level would mean migration on every Resend release-note bump. Keep `type`, `subtype`, `to_email`, `received_at` as queryable columns (the dimensions an operator actually filters on) and stash the rest as TEXT for forensics. ~1 KB per row.
decisions.recommendation: If a specific field becomes load-bearing (e.g. bounce category drives an automatic suppression-list update), promote it to a column then. Premature normalization is the trap.

decisions.decision: Drop email.sent events without an email_events row
decisions.rationale: email.sent is just Resend confirming the API call landed — which we already know from send()'s success return. Storing a row per outbound email would inflate the table for zero forensic value; the durable email_outbox table already records the rows that mattered to begin with.
decisions.recommendation: If we ever want a full outbound-mail audit trail, that's a separate table (mail_log), not this one.

features.successCriteria:
- 503 when RESEND_WEBHOOK_SECRET unset (graceful degrade)
- 401 on signature mismatch (Resend will not retry)
- 200 + dedupe on duplicate event ids (Resend retry storm absorbed)
- 200 + email_events row written on bounce/complaint/delivered/delayed/opened/clicked
- 200 + ignored=true for informational email.sent (no row)
- Payload TEXT truncated to 16KB defensively
- Svix timestamp tolerance window enforced (5 minutes) — replay attack rejected
- Rotated multi-sig header accepted via any-match
knownLimitations: Dormant until P1.1 (custom-domain DNS) is configured — no real events flow before then
No automatic suppression of bouncing addresses at send time yet; future work once real bounce data accumulates
Svix signature scheme is hard-coded; a future Resend rework to a different signer would need code change in resendWebhook.ts
Subtype extraction is naive — checks bounce_type / reason / subtype keys. New event types with different subtype fields land NULL until added
email.sent dedupes (occupies a webhook_events row) but doesn't write to email_events; minor wastage on the dedupe table

features.title: Handoff ritual — two ownership modes and a public checklist
features.problem: The portal promises that at the end of any engagement the client owns everything — the code repository, the accounts, the domain. That promise inverts the usual agency lock-in model and self-selects clients who value autonomy, so it is load-bearing positioning. But a promise is only as good as its operational backing. A skeptical buyer — a notary, an accountant, a tradesperson — cannot verify a one-line promise before signing, and the promise said nothing about a buyer who would rather not hold the keys at all, or about what happens if the consultant becomes unreachable. This feature turns the promise into something inspectable: two named ownership modes, a public page that explains them, a public checklist with the exact transfer procedure, and a documented dormancy path the client can execute alone.
features.analysis: The practice offers two ownership modes rather than one. The default, 'Tout à toi', means the client holds every key from day one. The optional 'Je m'en occupe' means the consultant stays custodian — handling the domain, certificates, and small upkeep — for an annual fee. The managed mode is only honest if it cannot become a new kind of lock-in, so two safeguards are built in: non-payment quietly reverts the client to 'Tout à toi' rather than cutting off service, and a documented dormancy procedure lets the client take ownership unilaterally if the consultant goes silent. The trust move is publishing the operational checklist — every transferable asset, with the exact steps — so a buyer can read the whole transfer procedure before signing rather than taking it on faith.

decisions.decision: Offer two modes instead of a single unconditional 'you own it'
decisions.rationale: Some non-technical buyers explicitly do not want to manage domain renewals, certificates, and security patches — forcing them to own everything just creates a different failure, where the deliverable rots from neglect. A managed mode lets the consultant honestly charge for that custodial labour, and because non-payment reverts to full ownership it does not break the no-lock-in promise.
alternativesConsidered: Single unconditional 'Tout à toi' mode — ignores buyers who want a managed offering
A multi-plan subscription — contradicts the boring-tech and single-operator constraints
Free managed mode — creates a silent backlog of dormant clients still owed renewals

decisions.decision: Publish the operational checklist publicly, not as an internal document
decisions.rationale: The target buyer is skeptical and wants to verify before signing. Publishing the exact transfer procedure makes the no-lock-in promise inspectable; hiding it behind 'ask for details' would make the promise look like marketing theatre.
alternativesConsidered: Internal-only document — the buyer cannot verify without asking
A public page behind a request-access gate — adds friction for no security gain

decisions.decision: Document a dormancy procedure the client can execute alone
decisions.rationale: Managed mode is only honest if the client has a written path to ownership when the consultant is unreachable. Without it, 'Je m'en occupe' is structurally a lock-in regardless of intent. The checklist names the dormancy threshold, the support tickets to open, and the order of operations, and the client holds a printed copy at delivery.
alternativesConsidered: Trust-based, no documented procedure — the client cannot recover ownership
A credential-escrow service — introduces a third party and contradicts the simplicity mandate

decisions.decision: Mode preference is a soft choice at intake, confirmed for real at handoff
decisions.rationale: Forcing the choice at intake friction-loads the form for a visitor who does not yet know what managed mode means. Confirming at handoff means the choice is made after the buyer has seen the deliverable and understood the trade-offs. The intake radio is optional so it never blocks submission.
alternativesConsidered: A required choice at intake — friction without information
No intake field at all — misses buyers who already have a firm preference

features.successCriteria:
- /handoff explains both ownership modes and reaches the switching and dormancy sections
- /handoff/checklist renders the full transfer procedure, including the dormancy path
- The footer carries a site-wide 'how it ends' link, the pricing table points to /handoff, and the About section mentions ownership
- The intake form shows the optional ownership-preference radio without blocking submission
- A skeptical buyer can verify the no-lock-in promise end to end before signing
knownLimitations: The public checklist is hand-mirrored to an offline copy in the repository — the two can drift until a build step generates one from the other
The managed-mode annual price is set without a signed managed client to calibrate against and may need adjustment after the first real engagement
The intake ownership-preference value shows as a friendly label on visitor-facing surfaces but as a raw code in the admin session view
No automated test exercises the dormancy recovery end to end — it depends on third-party support workflows that cannot be safely run in CI
The pricing pointer to /handoff renders only on the managed-eligible tier card; widening managed mode to other tiers would need a rendering change

statusHistory.reason: Code-first verification — feature.json co-located at src/pages/Handoff.feature.json. Re-checked both pages, the routes, the pricing pointer, and the intake radio against the shipped code.

features.title: Engagement runtime — magic-link sessions and the admin inbox
features.problem: On its own the portal is a brochure: a visitor who fills the intake form and leaves loses everything, the consultant has no inbox of work in flight, and there is no shared surface for the two parties to exchange messages. The engagement runtime is the layer that makes the rest of the portal real. A visitor signs in with a one-time email link, their intake becomes a persistent engagement they can return to from any device, the consultant gets an admin inbox of every engagement in flight, and both parties exchange messages on the same page. The one-active-plus-one-triage capacity rule becomes something the system can actually enforce, because there are now real engagement records to count.
features.analysis: The runtime turns the portal's static patterns into a live system. There are no passwords: a visitor enters an email, receives a one-time link, and clicking it establishes a signed session held in a cookie. Each engagement is one record — created from an intake submission, owned by the visitor's email, carrying its intake brief, a status, a message thread, and attachments. The consultant signs in with the same email-link mechanism; an allowlist of admin addresses is the only thing that distinguishes the admin view, so there is one authentication surface to secure rather than two. The admin inbox lists every engagement in flight; the session page is the shared surface where either party reads the brief, follows the status, and posts messages. Nothing is real-time by design — a page refreshes its data when it is opened or comes back into focus, never via push — because a real-time channel would create the always-available expectation the practice exists to refuse.

decisions.decision: Magic-link sign-in — no passwords, no third-party identity providers
decisions.rationale: It is the lowest-friction identity for a one-engagement-at-a-time practice: enter an email, click a link. No password to remember, no OAuth scopes, no vendor lock-in. It also fits the async-first contract — the visitor consents to email as the medium at the door.
decisions.recommendation: Keep links single-use and short-lived, and rate-limit how often they can be requested for an email or from an address.

decisions.decision: The admin role is an email allowlist, not a separate authentication flow
decisions.rationale: The consultant signs in exactly as a visitor does; the only difference is a server-side check of the address against an allowlist on admin routes. One authentication mechanism means one security surface to reason about.
decisions.recommendation: Treat the server-side allowlist check as the source of truth; any client-side admin hint is a convenience, never a control.

decisions.decision: One engagement per intake submission
decisions.rationale: Each engagement record corresponds to exactly one current or potential piece of work, which keeps the admin inbox a list of real engagements rather than a generic CRM and keeps the capacity cap cleanly countable.
decisions.recommendation: A visitor who wants to propose a second project starts a second intake; do not overload one record with multiple briefs.

decisions.decision: Polling on focus — never push, never real-time
decisions.rationale: A real-time channel is exactly the always-available expectation the practice is built to amputate. Refreshing data when a page is opened or refocused gives 'fresh on next visit' semantics, which matches the async-only contract for both parties.
decisions.recommendation: If anyone asks why updates are not live, point at the contract — live updates would oblige the other party to be available.

decisions.decision: A small, hand-rolled backend — no auth library, no ORM
decisions.rationale: Signed-cookie sessions and direct SQL keep the runtime small enough to read end to end. Adding a backend, authentication, and a database bends the boring-tech mandate, but each individual piece is kept to the smallest surface area that works.
decisions.recommendation: Resist adding an ORM, an auth library, or a state-management library until the runtime has been stable in production for a good while.

features.successCriteria:
- A visitor can save an intake, leave, and return on any device via a one-time email link with their engagement intact
- The session page shows the intake brief, the status, and a message thread, and both parties can post to it
- The admin inbox lists every engagement in flight with its triage deadline
- Admin-only routes refuse any signed-in address that is not on the allowlist
- One-time links are single-use, short-lived, and rate-limited
- The capacity cap is enforced from real engagement counts
- Nothing in the runtime creates a real-time or always-available expectation
knownLimitations: No real-time updates by design — a reply appears when the other party next opens or refocuses the page
Multi-admin support is only the email allowlist — adding a co-admin means editing the allowlist
Message bodies are plain text — there is no rich-text editor or markdown rendering
Engagements are never hard-deleted from the runtime — they are soft-statused and soft-deleted, which suits a text-and-attachment history
The whole backend runs on one hosting provider's platform — moving providers would be a non-trivial port, an accepted trade-off for the cost and the data-residency fit

statusHistory.reason: Code-first verification — feature.json co-located at src/pages/SessionPage.feature.json. The runtime has shipped: magic-link auth, the sessions and messages tables, the admin inbox, attachments, and the privacy-law data pages are all live.

features.title: About — home-page block introducing Marc
features.problem: The home page sells a HUMAN solo Québécois dev — the visitor needs to see who they're working with. About is the home-page introduction block: portrait + short bio + GitHub link. Portrait gracefully degrades to text-only if /marc.jpg ever 404s (onError handler hides the figure).
features.analysis: Renders DICT[lang].about copy. /marc.jpg is the portrait; hides the figure so a 404 doesn't render a broken-image icon. GITHUB_URL is hardcoded. CrossFeatureLink at the bottom routes to /parcours for more depth. Feature colour is HOME_SECTION_FEATURE['about'] = 'meta' (the backstage feature) — about-the-practice is meta-tinted.

decisions.decision: Portrait onError hides the figure instead of showing alt text
decisions.rationale: A broken image icon looks broken. Alt text on a hidden figure is gentler — the bio paragraph still reads. The cost is no portrait shown when 404'd; cheaper than rendering wrong.
decisions.recommendation: Keep the onError handler. The previous version showed a generic placeholder which AUDIT P3.18 corrected.

features.successCriteria:
- Portrait + bio + GitHub link render
- Portrait gracefully degrades on 404
- About section is meta-tinted (backstage feature colour)
- Bilingual at full parity
knownLimitations: Portrait is a single asset; no responsive srcset
GitHub URL is hardcoded; no link to other socials (no Twitter, LinkedIn) by design — Marc's professional surface is the portal itself
Bio length is short by intent; if the practice ever wants a deeper About page, fork into /about

statusHistory.reason: Component shipped with portrait + bio.
Code-first doc co-located. onError graceful-degrade pattern stable since AUDIT P3.18 cleanup.

features.title: Bring anything — neutralizes the vibe-filter for hesitant visitors
features.problem: The VibeFilter section ('je fais / je fais pas') answers the 'is this for me?' question with explicit do/don't lists. A side effect: a hesitant visitor who sees an item they're unsure about on the 'don't' side may self-filter out incorrectly. BringAnything sits IMMEDIATELY after VibeFilter to neutralize that self-filter — Marc's framing is 'I want every idea to come in; the triage is my job, not the visitor's.' The examples are aspirational, not historical, since the practice hasn't built a long history yet.
features.analysis: DICT[lang].bringAnything content block. Sits right after VibeFilter in Home.tsx render order, by design — its job is to undo the over-filter the vibe section can cause. Examples are aspirational ('things I'd happily take') not 'projects I have shipped'. Feature-coloured as 'intake' (HOME_SECTION_FEATURE['bring-anything']) since it pushes the visitor toward the intake form.

decisions.decision: Sits directly after VibeFilter, by render order
decisions.rationale: BringAnything's purpose is to neutralize VibeFilter's self-filter effect. The two sections must be adjacent and in order; reordering them breaks the funnel logic.
decisions.recommendation: Don't reorder these two. If a future audit suggests a different funnel structure, evaluate whether BringAnything still has its purpose; if so, keep adjacent.

decisions.decision: Aspirational examples, not 'projects I've shipped'
decisions.rationale: The practice hasn't built a multi-year client history yet. Falsely-historic examples would be dishonest; aspirational examples ('things I'd happily take') are structurally true.
decisions.recommendation: Once the showcase has 6+ shipped projects, revisit — maybe the section can use real anonymized examples. Until then, aspirational copy.

decisions.decision: Example cards grouped by pricing tier (0→4), not by life-domain theme
decisions.rationale: Originally one card per theme (everyday, gifts, work, hardware, weird). That structure showed range across life domains but left the set clustered in Tier 0-2 with no Tier 4 platform-scale idea, so the section quietly under-sold the high end.
