Regrouping by tier makes the examples reflect every pricing level: a visitor with a tiny idea and one with a multi-role platform both see a matching card. The example prices in each label mirror the Pricing section exactly (Niveau 0 free, 1 750$, 2 1800$, 3 3600$, 4 7500$+); hardware is placed in Tier 3 per the pricing hardwareNote. Ideas inside each card stay mixed (home/family/small-biz/hardware) so it still reads like a notepad, not a price sheet.
decisions.recommendation: If the Pricing tier amounts change, update these labels in lockstep. Keep at least one warm/personal idea per card so the tier ladder doesn't read as a cold price grid.

features.successCriteria:
- Renders directly after VibeFilter in home order
- Copy positions Marc as the triager, not the visitor
- Examples are aspirational (not falsely historic)
- Example cards span every pricing tier (0 free → 4 platform), labels matching the Pricing section
- Bilingual at full parity
- Feature-coloured as intake (pushes toward intake form)
knownLimitations: Example prices in the labels duplicate the Pricing section; they must be updated by hand if a tier amount changes
Examples may go stale; revisit periodically
No CTA button (intentional — the section is messaging, not conversion)

statusHistory.reason: Component shipped to neutralize VibeFilter's over-filter.
Code-first doc co-located. Adjacency-to-VibeFilter decision stable.

features.title: FAQ — disclosure accordion with URL-hash deep links
features.problem: The home page's FAQ section needs to handle the typical 'is this for me?' objections (price, timeline, ownership, idea-still-fuzzy, etc.) without a heavy interactive widget. The FAQ component is a native HTML <details>/<summary> accordion — no React state for open/closed — plus URL-hash sync so a /#faq-price link opens directly to that question. Each FAQ has a feature-colour dot tying it to the relevant feature.
features.analysis: Renders DICT[lang].faq.items as an array of <details> elements. open/closed is browser-native — react-hooks/setState doesn't drive it. URL-hash sync uses a useEffect that listens for hashchange + on mount: if hash matches an item id, set its open attribute. The 'open all' override is the only React state. FAQ_FEATURE map (src/lib/features.ts) ties each FAQ slug to a feature; FeatureDot renders the colour next to the question. Inline FAQPage JSON-LD is injected for SEO. Accessibility is good by default (native disclosure pattern).

decisions.decision: Native <details>/<summary>, not a custom React accordion
decisions.rationale: Native gets ARIA right, keyboard right, screen-reader right, with zero JS. A custom React accordion would re-implement all of that, less reliably. The only React state we need is the 'open all' override and the URL-hash sync — both well-suited to React; the open/closed state itself stays in the DOM.
decisions.recommendation: Don't switch to a custom accordion. If a future need wants per-item analytics on open, add an onclick listener but keep the native disclosure pattern.

decisions.decision: URL hash → open item, deep linkable
decisions.rationale: A /#faq-price link should land on the FAQ section AND open the price question. The hashchange listener + on-mount check handles both. The hash → slug map is keyed by FAQ slug (FAQ_FEATURE in features.ts), so the same id is the anchor + the colour + the deep link target.
decisions.recommendation: When adding a FAQ item, add it to DICT, FAQ_FEATURE, and FAQ_LABEL together. The features.test.ts test catches drift.

decisions.decision: Inject FAQPage JSON-LD
decisions.rationale: Google's rich-result FAQ snippet requires the JSON-LD structured data. Injecting it inline (vs. a hosted .jsonld file) keeps the FAQ + its structured data in one place. SEO benefit is real for the long-tail Quebec-local case-study traffic the practice targets.
decisions.recommendation: Update the JSON-LD when adding/changing FAQ items. The shape is hand-maintained; don't auto-generate (the human-readable answers and the JSON-LD answers can diverge slightly for SEO formatting).

features.successCriteria:
- FAQ items render as native disclosure accordion
- /#faq-<slug> deep links open the right item
- Each item shows its feature colour via FeatureDot
- 'Open all' button works
- FAQPage JSON-LD is injected for SEO
- Bilingual at full parity
knownLimitations: JSON-LD is hand-maintained alongside DICT; risk of divergence if updated in one place. Mitigation: SEO QA after major FAQ changes
Native <details> doesn't animate open/close uniformly across browsers; this is intentional (anti-AI-tells — uniform fade-in feels generic)
Hash sync fires on hashchange; programmatic state changes (e.g. router-driven) don't trip it. The home page's nav uses real hash anchors so this works

statusHistory.reason: Component shipped with the native disclosure + URL-hash sync + JSON-LD.
Code-first doc co-located. Native disclosure + hash deep links stable.

features.title: Featured projects — home-page shipped-work strip
features.problem: The most persuasive answer to 'is this a real practice?' is showing shipped work. FeaturedProjects is the home-page strip: top entries from the public showcase, each with a live iframe preview, drilling into /projects for the full gallery. Self-hides when the showcase is empty (early-practice state) so the home page doesn't render a half-built section.
features.analysis: GET /api/public/projects on mount. Renders the top N as ProjectCardPreview cards — each shows the showcase_title + tagline + an iframe of the current build URL (or a static placeholder if iframe_path is absent). HomeDrillCard at the bottom routes to /projects for the full gallery. useViewTransitionState from react-router-dom enables a smooth view transition into /projects on click. Returns null when the fetch returns zero rows — no empty-state copy, no skeleton; the section just doesn't exist for empty practices.

decisions.decision: Self-hide when empty (no rendering), not 'no projects yet' copy
decisions.rationale: An early-practice home page with 'no projects yet' would scream early-practice. Self-hiding lets the section appear only when there's something to show. The visitor experiences a coherent home page either way; once shipped, the section appears organically.
decisions.recommendation: Keep self-hide. If a future need wants a 'coming soon' affordance, add it elsewhere (e.g. a teaser at the section's anchor); don't dilute this section.

decisions.decision: iframe preview, not screenshot
decisions.rationale: An iframe shows the actual live site — proof that the build exists and works. A screenshot would be cheaper but stale. The iframe sandbox is permissive enough for self-hosted demos (AUDIT P1.9 deferred tightening). The cost is iframe load time; mitigated by lazy load + viewport-aware embedding.
decisions.recommendation: Don't switch to screenshots. If iframe load cost becomes a Lighthouse issue, evaluate intersection observer to defer until scrolled into view.

features.successCriteria:
- Renders top N showcased projects (currently 3)
- Each card embeds the live build via iframe
- Self-hides when zero projects
- HomeDrillCard routes to /projects
- View transition animates the navigation
- Bilingual at full parity
knownLimitations: Top N is hardcoded at 3; visible-on-home is fixed
iframe sandbox tightening (AUDIT P1.9 deferred) — currently permissive
If a build URL goes 404, the iframe renders the browser's broken-page state; no error handling
Self-hide branch means analytics can't distinguish 'no projects shown because empty' from 'visitor didn't scroll'

statusHistory.reason: Component shipped with the live iframe preview + self-hide + view transition.
Code-first doc co-located. Self-hide + iframe-preview decisions stable.

features.title: How it works — home-page primer with drill-card to /parcours
features.problem: Visitors arriving at the home page need a fast 'how does this work?' answer before they're willing to read pricing or send an intake. HowItWorks is the home-page primer — short, no specifics about workflow, just the practice's shape. A HomeDrillCard at the bottom links to /parcours (the full 12-step Journey page) for visitors who want more.
features.analysis: Short copy block + a HomeDrillCard pointing at /parcours. Feature colour is intentionally NEUTRAL (HOME_SECTION_FEATURE['how'] is undefined) because 'how it works' crosses every feature — it'd be wrong to dot it with one feature's colour. The neutral hollow dot is rendered via FeatureDot's undefined-handling. Copy lives in DICT[lang].how.

decisions.decision: Neutral feature colour (undefined), not a specific feature
decisions.rationale: How-it-works describes the WHOLE practice; pinning it to one feature would mislead the visitor about which colour represents what. The neutral hollow dot is the deliberate visual signal that this section bridges every feature.
decisions.recommendation: When adding a new home section, decide on its feature deliberately. Most sections should pick one; meta-explainer sections should pick undefined.

decisions.decision: Short primer + drill-card, not a full walkthrough on home
decisions.rationale: Home page is sequence; each section answers one visitor question. 'How does this work?' gets a short answer here + a deeper read available behind a click. Putting the full 12-step walkthrough on home would bury other sections.
decisions.recommendation: Keep the primer short. If new content emerges, add it to /parcours, not here.

features.successCriteria:
- Renders short primer + drill-card to /parcours
- Feature dot is neutral (undefined feature)
- Bilingual at full parity
knownLimitations: Primer copy is hardcoded; if the practice's shape changes, edit DICT
Drill-card style is shared with FeaturedProjects/Testimonials' HomeDrillCard; restyle there to change here

statusHistory.reason: Component shipped with primer + drill-card.
Code-first doc co-located. Neutral-colour decision stable.

features.title: Napkin replay — paced sketch playback for the engagement view
features.problem: An intake's napkin sketch (Excalidraw scene) is most affecting when seen drawn out, not static. NapkinReplay takes a saved scene and replays its elements in order over a fixed total wall-clock window — so a 6-stroke sketch and a 60-stroke sketch both finish in the same time. The total window is hand-tuned; element pacing adapts to fit. Lazy-loads Excalidraw (heavy ~600KB) only when the panel is opened.
features.analysis: Props: napkin scene JSON. Lazy-imports SketchCanvas (which itself lazy-imports Excalidraw). Total replay window is a constant; element count drives per-element pacing (totalMs / elementCount = perElement). On mount of the canvas, starts a setInterval that progressively reveals elements via Excalidraw's updateScene API. Pause/resume controls + replay-from-start. Replay runs once by default; user can re-replay.

decisions.decision: Fixed total window, not fixed per-element pacing
decisions.rationale: A 60-element sketch paced at 200ms/element takes 12 seconds — too long. A 6-element sketch at 200ms takes 1.2 seconds — over too fast. Fixed total window means both feel consistent. The trade-off: dense sketches reveal fast, sparse sketches reveal slowly, but both finish in the affecting-but-not-tedious window.
decisions.recommendation: Don't switch to fixed per-element pacing. The window length is tuned; adjust the constant if needed.

decisions.decision: Lazy-load SketchCanvas, not import statically
decisions.rationale: Excalidraw + its dependencies are ~600KB. Loading on every session view would bloat the chunk. Lazy via React.lazy means a session WITHOUT a napkin doesn't pay the cost.
decisions.recommendation: When using Excalidraw anywhere, route through SketchCanvas's lazy boundary.

features.successCriteria:
- Replay completes in the fixed window regardless of element count
- Excalidraw is loaded only when the panel opens
- Pause/resume/restart controls work
- Original scene state is restored after replay
- Replay is one-shot by default; can be replayed via UI
knownLimitations: Replay is element-by-element; doesn't reveal stroke-by-stroke within an element. Acceptable resolution
Pause keeps the partial state; restart wipes back to empty. No 'rewind' affordance
Excalidraw's API may change across versions — pin the version carefully (boring-tech mandate)
Replay window is per-component, not configurable per session

statusHistory.reason: Component shipped with the fixed-window replay + lazy load.
Code-first doc co-located. Fixed-window decision stable.

features.title: Sketch canvas — Excalidraw boundary with lazy chunk + CSS code-split
features.problem: The intake form's napkin sketch step needs Excalidraw (lightweight sketch tool). Excalidraw + its dependencies are ~600KB before assets. Importing statically would pay that cost on every intake load — most visitors don't open the sketch step. SketchCanvas is the boundary: a React.lazy-wrapped component that lazy-imports Excalidraw + its stylesheet. Callers route through React.lazy themselves too (e.g. NapkinReplay) so the chunk is doubly split.
features.analysis: Single component, mostly a wrapper around <Excalidraw />. Excalidraw imported via React.lazy. Excalidraw's stylesheet is imported inside this file (deliberate — the stylesheet is part of Excalidraw's package and would otherwise bundle eagerly). Callers should reach SketchCanvas via React.lazy too, so the lazy boundary actually code-splits. <Suspense> wrapping handles the chunk load. Forwards ref / API to caller via the ExcalidrawAPI type so callers (NapkinReplay, intake form) can drive the scene programmatically.

decisions.decision: Lazy-import via React.lazy at this boundary, callers do same
decisions.rationale: A single React.lazy upstream lets some code-paths reach Excalidraw eagerly via static imports. Double-lazy (this component + every caller) means EVERY reach is async, so the chunk is genuinely split.
decisions.recommendation: Every component that uses SketchCanvas should also be lazy-loaded by its caller. Static-import-then-lazy defeats the split.

decisions.decision: Excalidraw stylesheet imported in this file, not in styles.css
decisions.rationale: Importing in styles.css would bundle the CSS into the main bundle. Importing it here (inside the lazy boundary) means the CSS comes down with the JS chunk — same load-once-when-opened semantics as the component.
decisions.recommendation: Don't move the @import to styles.css. The boundary integrity depends on co-locating the CSS with the lazy component.

features.successCriteria:
- Excalidraw chunk loads only when SketchCanvas mounts (via caller-side React.lazy)
- Excalidraw CSS code-splits with the chunk
- ExcalidrawAPI is forwarded to caller via ref
- Scene state survives across renders within the component
- Heavy chunk never on the home/intake critical path
knownLimitations: Lazy load means a brief flicker on first open; mitigated by the <Suspense> fallback which renders a sketch-canvas placeholder
Excalidraw's API surface may change across versions — TypeScript types catch most breaks at compile time
Static imports anywhere in the call chain will defeat the split; CI bundle analyzer would catch but isn't currently wired up

statusHistory.reason: Component shipped as the Excalidraw lazy boundary.
Code-first doc co-located. Boundary + double-lazy pattern stable.

features.title: Testimonials — home-page vouches block (top 3)
features.problem: The home page needs social proof without overwhelming visitors with a wall of vouches. Testimonials shows the top 3 most recent approved vouches with a HomeDrillCard linking to /vouches for the full wall. Self-hides when no vouches are approved — keeps the home page honest for the early-practice state.
features.analysis: GET /api/public/vouches on mount; slice top 3 (HOME_LIMIT). Each rendered as a card with name + relationship pill + body. HomeDrillCard → /vouches at the bottom. Returns null when zero approved vouches. Feature-coloured as 'shipped' (HOME_SECTION_FEATURE['testimonials']) since vouches are the social-proof leg of the shipped feature.

decisions.decision: Top 3 only on home; full wall at /vouches
decisions.rationale: Three is enough to signal 'this is real'; more is noise on a home page. The drill-card is the affordance for visitors who want more.
decisions.recommendation: Don't change HOME_LIMIT. The number was tuned for visual density.

decisions.decision: Self-hide when zero vouches, no 'no testimonials yet' copy
decisions.rationale: Same logic as FeaturedProjects: early-practice self-hide is more honest than skeleton state copy.
decisions.recommendation: Keep self-hide.

features.successCriteria:
- Top 3 approved vouches on home
- Self-hide when empty
- HomeDrillCard routes to /vouches
- Bilingual at full parity
knownLimitations: Top 3 is hardcoded; not configurable per visitor / locale
No filter by relationship — newest 3 regardless of type
Self-hide hides analytics signal of 'empty practice'

statusHistory.reason: Component shipped paired with the vouches moderation flow.
Code-first doc co-located. Top-3 + self-hide stable.

features.title: Time-travel scrubber — keyboard-navigable build-history player
features.problem: On /share/:id, the recipient should be able to step through a session's build advancements — not just see the latest. TimeTravelScrubber takes an ordered array of public advancements and presents play/pause + step controls + an iframe embedding each step's build URL. Arrow keys step, Space toggles play. ?step=N URL param deep-links to a specific moment, so a shareable URL can point at 'the state right after the napkin sketch landed'.
features.analysis: Props: items (array of PublicAdvancementRow). Renders the iframe of the current step's build URL with metadata (label + date + step N of M). Controls: prev/next/play/pause buttons + arrow-key + Space handler. PLAY_INTERVAL_MS = 3500 (per-step pacing on play). STEP_PARAM = 'step' for URL deep-linking; readStepFromUrl clamps to [0, max] so a malformed ?step= falls to a valid index. Replaces idx in the URL via history.replaceState on every change so a shared URL captures the current scrub position. Keyboard handler is window-level and gates on focus-inside-scrubber-root (no global capture). aria-describedby ties a visible mono-pill keyboard hint to the section.

decisions.decision: Window-level keyboard handler with focus-inside gate
decisions.rationale: Capturing arrows globally would steal them from the rest of the page. A window listener that checks 'is focus inside .scrubber-root?' lets arrows work when the scrubber is the visitor's locus, and stays out of the way otherwise. Buttons are real <button>s so Tab+Enter+Space all work natively.
decisions.recommendation: Don't switch to document.addEventListener with always-on capture. The focus-inside gate IS the affordance.

decisions.decision: ?step is 1-indexed for humans
decisions.rationale: ?step=3 reads as 'step 3 of 5' to a human; ?step=2 (0-indexed) would be confusing. Internally the component uses 0-indexed idx; readStepFromUrl converts. AUDIT P3.3 documented this decision.
decisions.recommendation: Keep 1-indexed. External code that links to ?step= must use the 1-indexed convention.

decisions.decision: Replace state on idx change, don't push
decisions.rationale: history.replaceState means the back button doesn't navigate through scrub positions — it leaves the page. Push would create N entries for N scrub positions, polluting back-button history. Replace keeps the URL shareable without inflating history.
decisions.recommendation: Don't switch to push. Shareable + non-polluting is the right balance.

features.successCriteria:
- Step controls work via buttons, arrow keys, Space
- ?step=N deep-links to that 1-indexed step
- idx changes update URL via replaceState
- Iframe loads the current step's build URL
- Keyboard handler only fires when focus is inside the scrubber root
- Visible keyboard hint with aria-describedby
knownLimitations: Iframe load time depends on the build URL; no per-step preloading
Play interval is hardcoded (3500ms); not configurable per session
If items[].build_url is missing (unstamped), the iframe falls back to about:blank
Single global keyboard listener; multiple scrubbers on one page would conflict (not a current case)

statusHistory.reason: Component shipped with the iframe + controls.
AUDIT P3.1 + P3.2 + P3.3 work landed: visible keyboard hint, window-level handler with focus gate, ?step deep link.
Code-first doc co-located. Keyboard nav + deep-link patterns stable.

features.title: Voice recorder — shared mic capture for intake and session thread
features.problem: Both the intake form and the session thread want voice-note capture: tradespeople prefer speaking to typing. The two contexts have different consent wording (intake clips are transcribed-and-discarded; session clips are retained) and different confirm-button labels — so the recorder is context-agnostic. Caller supplies the copy; this component only knows how to capture audio, show a preview, and hand the blob back.
features.analysis: MediaRecorder API; navigator.mediaDevices.getUserMedia for stream. State machine: idle → recording → preview → confirmed (or cancelled back to idle). Records to a Blob via MediaRecorder's dataavailable event. Preview offers playback before confirm. onConfirm(blob) handed to caller. Cleanup releases the mic track on unmount or cancel. Permission prompt the FIRST time; subsequent uses skip the prompt on the same origin.

decisions.decision: Context-agnostic; caller supplies consent line and confirm label
decisions.rationale: Intake's recording is transcribed-and-discarded (no retention question). A session message's recording is retained (different Loi 25 footnote). The consent copy + button label must reflect WHICH context — but the recording mechanics are the same. Letting the caller supply the copy keeps the recorder reusable.
decisions.recommendation: When adding a third context (e.g. admin voice note for moderator), supply its own copy. Don't fork the recorder.

decisions.decision: Preview before confirm, not auto-submit
decisions.rationale: A voice note is often a re-do — first attempt is too long, second attempt is right. Forcing review-before-submit catches the 'didn't mean to record' case and gives the user a chance to delete a bad take without sending it anywhere.
decisions.recommendation: Keep the preview step. If a future UX wants 'one-shot quick voice', evaluate as a separate component.

features.successCriteria:
- Captures audio via MediaRecorder + getUserMedia
- Preview-before-confirm workflow
- Mic stream released on unmount + cancel
- Caller controls consent copy + confirm label
- Bilingual at full parity (DICT[lang])
- Graceful handling of permission denial
knownLimitations: MediaRecorder produces audio/webm in Chrome/Firefox, audio/mp4 in Safari — different mimetype; consumer handles
No auto-stop at a duration cap; user must hit stop. Could add a max-duration prop
Permission denial state is a polite 'tap to retry'; some browsers permanently deny on first refusal and require user to re-grant via browser settings
Single-channel mono assumed; multi-mic setups use the default

statusHistory.reason: Component shipped as the shared mic-capture surface for intake + session.
Code-first doc co-located. Context-agnostic + preview pattern stable.

features.title: Admin shell — chrome + auth gate for the /admin tree
features.problem: Every /admin/* surface needs the same wrapper: sidebar nav, language toggle, signed-in-as indicator, and the auth gate that bounces anon visitors to /login. Centralizing this in Admin.tsx as a layout component means each admin route is one focused screen, not a copy-pasted shell. Sub-routes render through <Outlet /> with the chrome already in place.
features.analysis: Layout component. useAuth() → if no email, <Navigate to='/login' replace />. Otherwise renders sidebar + header + <Outlet /> for sub-routes. useTenant() supplies tenant context for operator/buyer distinction; sub-routes decide what to show based on role. Mounted at /admin and /en/admin in router.tsx with index = AdminHub and children for /audit, /custodians, /inbox, /trash, /vouches, /showcase, /runbook. Marketplace-shaped surfaces (AdminFleet, AdminBilling, AdminTeam, AdminAppearance) are still reachable by direct URL per the root feature.json's 2026-05-09 note but no longer surfaced in the sidebar.

decisions.decision: Centralize the auth gate at the layout, not per-sub-route
decisions.rationale: Each /admin/* sub-route would otherwise need its own useAuth() guard. Centralizing means the gate is one component; missing it on a new sub-route is structurally impossible because the sub-route is under <Outlet />.
decisions.recommendation: When adding a new /admin sub-route, mount it as a child of Admin in router.tsx. Don't bypass the layout.

decisions.decision: Marketplace-shaped surfaces kept direct-URL reachable
decisions.rationale: Per the root feature.json 2026-05-09 note: the marketplace surfaces (Fleet, Billing, Team, Appearance) cover a hypothetical buyer scenario. Removing them entirely would mean rewriting later if that scenario reopens. Hiding from sidebar removes the cost of carrying without losing the option.
decisions.recommendation: If the buyer scenario stays off the table for ~12 months, evaluate deleting these surfaces; until then, keep them parked.

features.successCriteria:
- Anon visitor on /admin/* is redirected to /login
- Signed-in operator sees the sidebar + sub-route content
- Sidebar reflects the active route
- Language toggle works in both /admin and /en/admin trees
- Sub-routes render via <Outlet /> with chrome already in place
knownLimitations: No role-based sidebar (every signed-in admin sees everything); fine for single-operator practice
Auth gate uses client-side redirect — there's a brief flash before /login renders. Acceptable
Marketplace-shaped surfaces (AdminFleet etc.) aren't lazy-removed; they ship in the bundle (~few KB)

statusHistory.reason: Layout shipped with the auth gate + sidebar.
Code-first doc co-located. Layout + auth gate stable; marketplace-shaped surface posture decision documented.

features.title: Admin audit — reverse-chronological feed of operator actions
features.problem: Operator-significant actions (tenant provisioning, theme edits, status freezes, etc.) need a trail. AdminAudit reads /api/admin/audit and renders the audit_log table as a reverse-chronological feed. Read-only — append-only is the contract; nothing here can edit history. Client-side actor + action substring filters help narrow down without server-side date-range queries (deferred — small log size).
features.analysis: GET /api/admin/audit on mount; returns recent audit_log entries (server-side limit; client paginates if more). Each row has id + ts + actor_email + tenant_id + action string + optional payload JSON. Renders newest first. Two filter inputs: actor substring + action substring; match-count badge updates as user types. Clear-filters button. No edit affordance — history is append-only.

decisions.decision: Client-side filters, server-side date-range deferred
decisions.rationale: audit_log is small (operator actions are rare). Fetching everything + filtering client-side is fine at this scale. Server-side date-range would add a /api/admin/audit?from=...&to=... param with its own pagination — deferred until log size makes client-filter slow.
decisions.recommendation: Don't pre-emptively add server-side filter. Revisit when audit_log crosses ~5000 rows or response payload approaches 1MB.

decisions.decision: No edit, no delete affordance
decisions.rationale: An audit log that can be edited isn't an audit log. The contract is append-only; the SQL has no DELETE/UPDATE path exposed via any handler. Edit would also confuse compliance review.
decisions.recommendation: Don't add edit. If a wrong entry needs context, append a new entry referring to it.

features.successCriteria:
- Lists audit_log entries newest first
- Two filter inputs (actor, action) work client-side with match-count badge
- Clear-filters resets to full list
- Operator-only (gated at the API)
- No edit, no delete affordance
knownLimitations: Server-side limit on initial fetch; very old entries require pagination (not surfaced today)
No date range filter UI; client-side substring is the only narrowing today
Payload JSON is shown verbatim — could be long. No truncation/preview UI
audit_log inserts are best-effort from the handlers that emit them; a failure to insert isn't fatal but is silent

statusHistory.reason: Page shipped with the feed + client-side filters (AUDIT P3.17).
Code-first doc co-located. Append-only contract + client-filter posture stable.

features.title: Admin custodians — every subscription state at a glance
features.problem: Custodian subscriptions live in sessions.custodian_status with state transitions driven by /api/payments/webhook. Marc needs one place to see all subs at once: who's active (MRR + next renewal), who's past-due (Stripe failed — needs operator attention), and the history of switched/canceled. AdminCustodians is that page. No new endpoint required — reuses /api/sessions (admin scope) and buckets client-side.
features.analysis: GET /api/sessions on mount. Bucket sessions by custodian_status: active (renders with MRR sum + next-renewal dates), past_due (top of page, needs attention), switched_to_tout_a_toi + canceled (history strip). Empty buckets render nothing — page doesn't look half-built for a young practice with few subs. No /api/admin/subscriptions endpoint; deriving from session rows keeps the schema lean.

decisions.decision: Reuse /api/sessions, no dedicated subscriptions endpoint
decisions.rationale: custodian_status is a column on the session row, set by the webhook. The list endpoint already returns it. Adding /api/admin/subscriptions would duplicate auth + projection work for zero schema benefit.
decisions.recommendation: If subscription queries get complex (e.g. churn analysis), add a /api/admin/subscriptions/analytics that returns aggregate-only data; keep this page reading from /api/sessions.

decisions.decision: Past-due bucket renders at the TOP, not in status order
decisions.rationale: Past-due means Stripe's payment retry has failed enough that admin needs to step in — usually a card update or a cancel. Surfacing it first puts the operator-urgent work at the top. Active subs (no work needed) sit below.
decisions.recommendation: Keep past-due at the top. The page is for operator work, not balance-sheet review.

features.successCriteria:
- All active subs visible with their MRR contribution and next renewal
- Past-due subs surface at top with the session link for triage
- Switched / canceled history kept browsable
- Empty buckets render nothing (no half-built page in young practice)
- One fetch on mount; client-side bucketing
knownLimitations: Past-due investigation is operator manual — no automated re-charge attempt beyond Stripe's built-in retry. Stripe retries 4 times over 4 weeks by default
MRR sum is computed client-side and only over current active subs; refund history isn't surfaced here (see /admin/inbox/:id for that)
No CSV export; SQL the customers table or use the Stripe Dashboard for accounting

statusHistory.reason: Page shipped with the three-bucket layout.
Code-first doc co-located. Bucketing pattern stable.

features.title: Admin hub — operator console index
features.problem: An operator hitting /admin needs to see what state the practice is in at a glance — pending triage count, active session, due-soon checks, recent audit activity — and then click through to the right tool. AdminHub is that index page. Mounted as the /admin/index route, it groups every admin surface + external dashboard (Stripe, Resend, CF) into scannable tiles, with live counters where possible. The same grouping definitions feed the /carte map's Admin layer so the two views stay in sync.
features.analysis: Reads /api/sessions (admin sees all) on mount to compute live counters: pending triage count, active session title + age, custodian past-due count. Tiles are grouped by purpose (Sessions, Vouches, Payments, Observability, External) using HUB_SECTIONS defined in src/lib/admin/hubSections.ts — single source so /carte's Admin layer can render the same grouping. Each tile is a labeled link + optional badge (e.g. red dot for past-due, count for pending queue). External tiles link out to Stripe Dashboard, Resend, CF Pages — not embedded, deliberately, since each surface has its own auth.

decisions.decision: Group definitions in src/lib/admin/hubSections.ts, not inline
decisions.rationale: Two consumers (this page + /carte's Admin layer) need the same grouped tile structure. Centralizing means one place to add a new admin surface; both consumers pick it up.
decisions.recommendation: When adding a new admin surface, add it to hubSections.ts. Don't add inline tile defs here.

decisions.decision: External dashboards are linked out, never embedded
decisions.rationale: Embedding Stripe / Resend / CF dashboards would either require their iframe support (not always available) or proxy their auth (huge security surface). Linking out is simple and respects each tool's native auth.
decisions.recommendation: Don't try to embed external dashboards.

features.successCriteria:
- Land on /admin and see live counters for pending triage, active session, past-due custodians
- Tiles are grouped by purpose, not alphabetically
- External tools open in new tab
- /carte's Admin layer mirrors the same grouping
- Single fetch on mount; no per-tile waterfall
knownLimitations: Single fetch on mount; counters can drift if a transition happens elsewhere while the page is open. Acceptable — the page is a hub, not a real-time dashboard
External tile links assume the operator is already signed into Stripe / Resend / CF; no SSO chain
Tile badges are computed client-side; a buggy listSessions response could miscount

statusHistory.reason: Page shipped as /admin index; replaced the legacy redirect to the now-hidden /admin/apparence.
Code-first doc co-located. Tile model + grouping centralization stable.

features.title: Admin inbox — every session in one scannable queue
features.problem: Marc needs a single page that lists every session (every status, every visitor) sorted by what needs attention first. AdminInbox is that. Reads /api/sessions (admin scope), groups by status, sorts within groups by SLA pressure (oldest triage at the top), and surfaces enough metadata per row to triage without opening each session. Clicking a row navigates to /admin/inbox/:id which is the SessionPage in admin mode.
features.analysis: GET /api/sessions on mount; admin scope returns every non-deleted session. Group client-side by status (triage, active, etc.), sort within each group by SLA pressure (computeSla from src/lib/format.ts — hours since creation vs. the 72h SLA constant). Each row shows: visitor email + first-name, problem type label, status pill, time since creation, SLA badge (green/amber/red). Click → navigate to /admin/inbox/:id which reuses SessionPage in operator mode.

decisions.decision: Reuse /api/sessions for inbox view, not a dedicated /api/admin/inbox endpoint
decisions.rationale: The list endpoint already returns admin-scope data when called with admin cookie. Adding a parallel endpoint would duplicate auth gating and projection logic. The /me dashboard and /admin/inbox call the same endpoint with different rendering — SQL is the projection.
decisions.recommendation: When adding a new admin-facing view of sessions, render from the same endpoint. If a new view needs fundamentally different shape, evaluate a new endpoint, not the inbox shortcut.

decisions.decision: SLA pressure sort within status groups, not status-then-time
decisions.rationale: An operator scanning the inbox should see the most-urgent triage at the top — a 70h-old triage is more important than a 5h-old triage. Status grouping keeps similar work together; SLA pressure within ranks them. Pure time-sort would mix freshly-created and aged across statuses.
decisions.recommendation: Don't reorder. The grouping is the affordance.

features.successCriteria:
- Lists every non-deleted session in admin scope
- Groups by status with most-urgent first within each group
- SLA badge color reflects pressure (green / amber / red)
- Click navigates to /admin/inbox/:id which is SessionPage in operator mode
- Single fetch on mount; no per-row request
knownLimitations: No filter UI (status, problem type, email search) — single-operator scale, Ctrl+F is enough
No bulk operations — every status flip is per-session via the session page
Sort is client-side; a very large inbox (~1000+ rows) would be slow. Current scale: <100

statusHistory.reason: Page shipped with the grouped + SLA-sorted inbox view.
Code-first doc co-located. Reuse-/api/sessions pattern stable.

features.title: Admin runbook — two-track operator runbook with handoff-gap detection
features.problem: Marc's operating-the-portal knowledge lives in two tracks: dev handoff (what infra is set up, what credentials exist, what monitoring) and user journey (what a visitor experience from intake to ship). Solo, the two are easy to keep in sync; under pressure, gaps appear (Track B says 'visitor receives email', Track A says 'Resend not yet configured' — silent handoff gap). AdminRunbook renders both tracks side by side; Track B steps with a `dependsOn` link to Track A get a red flag when the prerequisite is unchecked.
features.analysis: Renders via <RunbookParallel /> (src/components/RunbookParallel.tsx) which takes the two tracks as props. Track A = dev handoff steps (defined in src/lib/runbook.ts); Track B = user journey steps with `dependsOn: 'track-a-step-id'` references. Per-step progress (checked/unchecked) is in localStorage, browser-local, no server sync. Reset button wipes all checks in one shot. The dependency check is real-time: changing a Track A check flips any Track B step depending on it (red flag if prereq unchecked).

decisions.decision: Per-step progress in localStorage, not server-side
decisions.rationale: Runbook progress is per-operator, per-device. Server-syncing would create a shared mutable list that two operators could trip over. localStorage keeps it private + per-device, which matches the 'this is my checklist' mental model.
decisions.recommendation: Don't add server sync. If multi-device sync becomes a real ask, evaluate whether a shared runbook is even what you want (probably not).

decisions.decision: dependsOn red flag, not greyed-out unavailable
decisions.rationale: Greying out 'visitor receives email' until Resend is configured would HIDE the dependency. The point of the runbook is to SHOW the gaps. Red flag + checkbox-still-clickable lets admin proceed anyway (knowing the gap exists) or fix the prereq first.
decisions.recommendation: Keep red flag, not disable. Visibility is the value.

features.successCriteria:
- Track A + Track B render side by side
- Per-step checkbox state persists in localStorage
- Reset wipes all checks
- Track B steps with `dependsOn` flag when prereq is unchecked
- Bilingual support
knownLimitations: Browser-local progress; switching device or clearing storage loses state
Step definitions are hardcoded in src/lib/runbook.ts; adding a step requires a code change + deploy
dependsOn supports one prereq per step; a step needing two prereqs would need multi-dep support (not implemented)
Track B's 'visitor experience' isn't auto-derived from real data; it's a curated list

statusHistory.reason: Page shipped with the two-track layout + dependency check.
Code-first doc co-located. Two-track + red-flag pattern stable.

features.title: Admin showcase — operator-only OG card grid + brand-check
features.problem: Each showcased project gets a dynamic OG card (functions/og/share/[id].ts) used as the social-unfurl preview. Card content drifts as session metadata changes; bad data (title overflow, missing tagline, un-tiered session) silently produces ugly cards that scrapers cache for days. AdminShowcase is the brand-check surface — a grid of every showcased project's OG card, with warning chips overlaid for each known issue. Operator-only.
features.analysis: GET /api/public/projects on mount (same data the public gallery uses; safe to fetch as admin too). Each project is rendered as a card with its actual OG image embedded via . WARNINGS are computed per-project: title length over OG safe area, missing tagline, no tier set, deploy URL missing. Warning chips overlay the card top; click chip to navigate to /admin/inbox/:id for the fix. Grid layout, no list mode.

decisions.decision: Render the actual OG image, not a placeholder
decisions.rationale: Brand-check means seeing what social scrapers actually see. A placeholder would mask render bugs in workers-og. Rendering the live OG endpoint via means font issues, layout breaks, etc. all surface here.
decisions.recommendation: Don't switch to a placeholder. If requests stress the OG endpoint, add CF Pages edge caching — but the real OG renders are what we need to eyeball.

decisions.decision: Warnings as overlay chips, not a separate audit page
decisions.rationale: Seeing the warning next to the card it applies to lets admin diagnose visually — 'oh, the title is overflowing' makes sense when you SEE the overflow + the warning together. A separate audit list would force admin to map warning → card mentally.
decisions.recommendation: Add new warning types as needed; keep them visual + adjacent.

features.successCriteria:
- Every showcased project renders its real OG card
- Warning chips overlay each card for any detected issue
- Click a chip → navigate to /admin/inbox/:id for the fix
- Operator-only (anon visitors redirect)
- Brand-check at a glance
knownLimitations: OG cards render via tag; some browsers cache aggressively. Hard refresh if needed
Warning list is static (predefined types); new types require a code change
If /og/share/:id returns the static fallback (rendering failure), this page shows the fallback too — operator can't distinguish 'no warning' from 'render broke'. RUNBOOK § OG triage covers

statusHistory.reason: Page shipped with the grid + warning chip overlay.
Code-first doc co-located. Render-real-OG + adjacent-warnings decisions stable.

features.title: Admin trash — soft-deleted sessions, restorable
features.problem: Sessions soft-delete via DELETE /api/sessions/:id; the row stays in D1 with deleted_at set. AdminTrash is the recovery surface — lists soft-deleted sessions, shows when each was deleted and by whom, and offers a one-click restore. Without this, recovering an accidentally-deleted session would require SQL access.
features.analysis: GET /api/sessions?deleted=true (admin-only opt-in flag) on mount; returns ONLY rows with deleted_at IS NOT NULL. Each row shows: visitor email, problem type, original status (last value before delete), when deleted, who deleted (self vs admin). Restore button → PATCH /api/sessions/:id with { restore: true } which clears deleted_at and resets status to 'triage' (the safest restoration target — admin decides next move from there).

decisions.decision: Restore lands at status='triage', not the pre-delete status
decisions.rationale: Restoring directly to whatever status the session held before delete could put it back into active capacity (breaking the cap rule). Landing at 'triage' is safe — admin reviews and transitions explicitly. Worst case: a restored session needs one PATCH to active. Best case: the deletion was correct and the restoration is a clean re-triage.
decisions.recommendation: Keep the triage-restore. If an admin wants to restore to exact prior state, they can do that via two PATCHes (restore + transition).

features.successCriteria:
- Lists only soft-deleted sessions
- Restore button works in one click
- Restored session lands at status='triage'
- Visitors never see soft-deleted sessions on /me regardless of admin actions
- Forbidden message for non-admin visitors who somehow reach the page
knownLimitations: No bulk restore — one click per row
No filter / search; current scale doesn't warrant
Restored sessions go to triage even if originally far past that — admin re-evaluates

statusHistory.reason: Page shipped with the trash list + one-click restore.
Code-first doc co-located. Restore-to-triage decision stable.

features.title: Admin vouches — moderation surface for testimonials
features.problem: Vouches submitted via the open /vouch form land in status='pending'. AdminVouches is where Marc moderates: approve to surface on /vouches, reject for spam, edit visitor copy (typos, length, voice), or soft-delete. Four sections stacked (pending / approved / rejected / trash) — each rendered only if non-empty so the page doesn't look half-built early on. Inline edit (no modal) keeps moderation fast.
features.analysis: GET /api/admin/vouches on mount; returns every vouch including pending/rejected/deleted with email attached. Sorted pending-first (newest) then everything else by created_at DESC. Client-side bucket by status into the four sections. Edit row expands inline with name/relationship/body/link fields; save PATCHes via PATCH /api/admin/vouches/:id. Status flip is the same endpoint with { status: 'approved' | 'rejected' }; delete via DELETE /api/admin/vouches/:id. Restore from trash is PATCH with { undelete: true }. Author email is shown (operator-only context) but never editable.

decisions.decision: Inline edit row, not a modal
decisions.rationale: Moderation is fast work — read, edit, approve, next. A modal interrupts the flow with a focus shift + close click. An inline-expanding row keeps the operator on the same scroll position and lets them approve-then-edit-the-next without modal whiplash.
decisions.recommendation: Don't switch to a modal. If two operators ever need to moderate concurrently, the race is on the SAVE, not the edit UX.

decisions.decision: Edit is intentionally broad (name + body + link + relationship)
decisions.rationale: Marc routinely tightens visitor copy before approving — typos, length over the comfortable limit, voice that doesn't match the practice. A narrow edit ('only fix typos') would force separate workflows. Broad edit makes the moderation surface feel like a real editor, not a triage console.
decisions.recommendation: Keep edit broad. If a future need wants 'preserve original' as a feature, surface a 'show original' affordance, don't restrict editing.

decisions.decision: Author email immutable
decisions.rationale: Email is the contact channel for moderator follow-up + the dedup key for rate-limit. Changing it would break either or both. If a vouch comes in with a wrong email, reject + ask the submitter to resubmit.
decisions.recommendation: Don't add email edit. The submission flow is the right place to fix that.

features.successCriteria:
- Lists pending / approved / rejected / trash sections; empty sections don't render
- Inline edit row supports name + relationship + body + link
- Status flips work; soft-delete + restore work
- Author email is shown (operator context) but not editable
- Single fetch on mount
- Visitor never sees pending/rejected/trash from this page
knownLimitations: No bulk operations; each row is its own click
Edit doesn't versioning — overwrite is permanent. Original copy is gone after save
No filter / search UI; current scale doesn't warrant
Sort is sticky (pending first, then created_at desc); no custom sort

statusHistory.reason: Page shipped with the four-section layout + inline edit.
Code-first doc co-located. Inline-edit + broad-edit decisions stable.

features.title: L'atelier — the portal's design system as a public exhibit
features.problem: A consultant claiming visual range and testing rigor in 2026 is making an unverifiable promise — everyone says it. Most portfolios show a curated five-screen highlight reel and call it done. /atelier inverts that: every visual baseline this project ships, across every viewport and theme, is rendered publicly. The gallery IS the proof — if a screen is in the e2e regression suite, it's in the atelier; if it isn't in either, it doesn't exist as a claim. Pair that with the portal's own design tokens displayed as an exhibit (feature glyphs, colour scale, type ramp) and a visitor can verify both 'they have a design system' and 'they actually keep it on rails' in one page.
features.analysis: Two exhibits stacked. Exhibit A — visual language: the seven feature glyphs render as live <FeatureGlyph /> components (each a link into /carte filtered by that feature), the colour tokens display the actual CSS custom properties resolved at render time, the type scale shows the Source Serif 4 / JetBrains Mono ramp in use. This is the design system as a code-first artifact, not a Figma export. Exhibit B — the Atelier Gallery: every committed Playwright screenshot in e2e/__screenshots__/ becomes a clickable thumbnail. Click one and an inspector opens — switch viewport (phone / wide / etc.), step through related captures of the same route, jump to the live page. The gallery manifest is generated at prebuild by scripts/build-atelier-gallery.mjs from the PNG filenames + dimensions; full-resolution PNGs are also surfaced (clicking the thumb opens the full). The page is deliberately excluded from the screenshot suite itself (see e2e/routes.ts) — capturing /atelier would recurse the whole corpus into one image.

decisions.decision: The gallery IS the e2e baselines, not a curated subset
decisions.rationale: If the atelier showed cherry-picked screens, the implicit claim ('we visually-regress every screen') would be unprovable — the visitor would have to take the consultant's word. By rendering every committed baseline, the page becomes its own verification: any visual regression the team has actually caught is on display. A cherry-pick would be a marketing artifact; this is structurally honest.
decisions.recommendation: Don't add curation filters or 'featured' tags. If a baseline is too ugly to ship, fix the baseline, don't hide it from the gallery.

decisions.decision: Generate the manifest at prebuild, never fetch at runtime
decisions.rationale: Loi 25 + the boring-tech mandate: no runtime API call means no server log of who browsed the atelier, no 503 path to handle, no third-party CDN to negotiate. The manifest is a static JSON written by scripts/build-atelier-gallery.mjs from disk inspection of e2e/__screenshots__/. A fresh clone needs to run a build (or the script directly) before public/atelier/ thumbnails exist; the README points at this.
decisions.recommendation: If a thumbnail rendering optimization is ever needed (e.g. WebP), do it in build-atelier-gallery.mjs — keep the runtime a static manifest read.

decisions.decision: Exclude /atelier from the e2e screenshot suite
decisions.rationale: Capturing /atelier would screenshot a page that itself displays every other screenshot, creating a recursive corpus where regenerating one baseline cascades into hundreds of pixel-diff failures. The page is checked manually for visual regressions instead. e2e/routes.ts has the exclusion comment so a future contributor doesn't re-add it.
decisions.recommendation: Keep the exclusion. If a separate 'atelier integrity' test is needed (e.g. 'every PNG in __screenshots__/ has a manifest entry'), write it as a Vitest unit against the manifest JSON, not a Playwright run against the live page.

features.successCriteria:
- Every committed PNG in e2e/__screenshots__/ has a thumbnail + inspector entry in the gallery
- The seven feature glyphs render with live colours resolved from CSS custom properties (not hardcoded values)
- The inspector supports keyboard navigation (arrow keys to step) and viewport filters
- /atelier itself is NOT captured by the e2e suite (preserves the recursion guard)
- The page renders zero runtime fetches — gallery manifest + tokens both come from static sources
- A visitor can land on /atelier and verify, in one page, the portal's visual range and the testing rigor that backs it
knownLimitations: Gallery thumbnails live in public/atelier/ which is gitignored — a fresh clone shows a broken-image gallery until `npm run build` (or just build-atelier-gallery.mjs) runs
The viewport filter only knows the viewports declared in playwright.config.ts; a new viewport in the suite requires updating the filter list
/atelier is excluded from the screenshot suite, so a visual regression on this page itself wouldn't be caught by the gate — it's checked manually before a release
The inspector's 'live page' link assumes the route is publicly reachable; an auth-gated baseline (admin surfaces, signed-in /me) shows a sign-in page when followed by an anon visitor

statusHistory.reason: Page shipped with the atelier-gallery prebuild script; gallery manifest + inspector both live.
Code-first doc co-located; recursion exclusion, no-fetch design, and gallery-as-proof framing all stable since 2026-05-21.

features.title: Au revoir — the post-erasure ritual
features.problem: DELETE /api/me is a structurally honest action — the row goes, the attachments go, the magic-link tokens go — but it lands the visitor on /me a half-second later with the dashboard half-disappeared and no acknowledgement that anything happened. The act needs a closing frame: a quiet page that says 'it is done' and dissolves the categories of data that just left. /au-revoir is that closing frame. It runs only when arrived-at via the erasure flow; a direct visit shows a shorter 'you're already gone' branch instead so the URL isn't a dead end.
features.analysis: The page has two branches separated by a one-shot sessionStorage flag (mp_just_erased). The /me dashboard sets the flag immediately before navigating to /au-revoir after a successful DELETE; this page reads + clears the flag at mount. Branch A (flag present) renders six category labels (email, sessions, messages, attachments, drafts, preferences) that fade from ink to paper over ~3 seconds, staggered per item, then settles on a quiet 'c'est fait' / 'you are no longer in the ledger' line. Branch B (flag absent) renders the direct-visit copy ('your account is already gone, or you never had one') with a home link — no animation, no tokens. Reduced-motion users get Branch A's tokens rendered at zero opacity from frame zero so the title still stands alone; the emotional weight stays intact without the flicker. The ritual branch also clears the in-tab visit log via clearVisits() in a useEffect — a freshly-erased visitor shouldn't carry a /passage trail from before the deletion. No FeatureContinue: a 'keep browsing the backstage tour' pointer would defeat the entire framing of the page.

decisions.decision: Two branches gated by a one-shot sessionStorage flag, not a URL param
decisions.rationale: A URL param (?just=erased) would be tampering-prone — anyone could craft the ritual URL and play the animation for no real reason. sessionStorage is set server-coordinated by /me's onDelete handler IMMEDIATELY before the navigate; the flag is consumed on first render and never re-set without another real deletion. Tab-close clears the flag automatically.
decisions.recommendation: If a future flow needs to land the visitor on /au-revoir for a non-deletion reason (e.g. logout), introduce a separate flag — don't re-use mp_just_erased. The ritual frame is reserved for actual erasure.

decisions.decision: Cache the first claim of the erasure flag in a module-level slot
decisions.rationale: React's lazy useState initializer can be invoked multiple times during a single mount cycle — Concurrent Mode in prod, StrictMode in dev. A side-effecting initializer that *consumes* sessionStorage would read '1' on the first call and null on the second, silently defaulting to the direct branch and skipping the ritual in BOTH environments. The module-level claimSlotInUse / claimSlotValue pair caches the first read so all subsequent invocations within the same mount return the same value. useEffect cleanup resets the slot for the next mount so a navigate-away-and-back still gets a fresh claim against (now-empty) sessionStorage.
decisions.recommendation: Don't move the consume call back into a lazy useState initializer thinking 'the initializer only runs once' — that assumption is false in React 18+. If you need a similar 'read external state once at mount' pattern elsewhere, copy this claim-slot scaffold or use a useEffect-then-setState (with an accepted single-frame flash to the default branch).

decisions.decision: Reduced-motion users skip the animation but keep the page
decisions.rationale: The token-fade is the affective payload of the page; honoring prefers-reduced-motion means rendering the labels at zero opacity from the start, so the title and body line still anchor the moment. Some users would experience the staggered fade as nauseating — silencing the animation while preserving the framing is the right trade. Hiding the labels entirely would feel like an incomplete page; rendering them invisibly keeps the layout's emotional weight.
decisions.recommendation: Keep the CSS rule that opacity-zeros the tokens at zero delay for reduced-motion. Don't replace it with a 'reduced-motion alternative animation' — the point is no animation, not a different one.

decisions.decision: No 'keep browsing' pointer on either branch
decisions.rationale: FeatureContinue or a related-aside would convert the page into a tour stop, defeating its terminal framing. The only link is 'back to home' — minimal, available for the visitor who chooses to keep using the site, but never suggesting they should. This is also why /au-revoir does NOT appear in META_PAGE_NEXT (see src/lib/features.ts comment).
decisions.recommendation: Resist the pull to add a 'next steps' block here. The page is a goodbye; goodbyes don't include cross-sells.

features.successCriteria:
- A visitor arriving via successful DELETE /api/me sees the token-fade ritual + 'c'est fait' line
- A direct-URL visitor sees the 'your account is already gone' branch with a home link, no animation
- prefers-reduced-motion users see the ritual branch with tokens at zero opacity from frame zero
- React StrictMode's double-mount does NOT replay the ritual — flag is consumed on first read
- The /passage walked-pages log is cleared after the ritual fires (no stale trail from before erasure)
- The page is reachable at /au-revoir + /en/goodbye; no public site link points at it (only the /me dashboard's post-DELETE navigation)
knownLimitations: sessionStorage in private/incognito modes may be unavailable — in that case the flag can't be set and the ritual branch never fires, even after a real deletion; the direct branch is the safer fallback so visitors aren't trapped on a broken animation

The token list is hardcoded (six categories) — the actual row count of what just got deleted is unknown by design (querying the DB for a now-deleted user is impossible), so the labels are honest in shape but not in count

The reduced-motion CSS path keeps the labels in the DOM at zero opacity — screen readers may still announce them; aria-hidden='true' on the <ol> mitigates but doesn't eliminate this in every AT/browser combo
If a future migration adds a new data category (e.g. voice transcripts), the six token labels here will silently understate what was erased until someone bumps the list

statusHistory.reason: Page shipped in f3ef8f1 with src/lib/erasureFlag.ts. /au-revoir and /en/goodbye render the two branches; e2e baselines committed on a298255 (au-revoir.png, en-goodbye.png — direct branch, since sessionStorage isn't pre-set in e2e).
Code-first doc co-located post-ship. Branch behaviour, reduced-motion handling, and the no-cross-sell decision all documented. Required fields complete.

features.title: Le carnet — what I hold vs. what Meta collects
features.problem: Saying 'I keep almost nothing about you' is a claim nobody has to believe. Saying it next to a real, sourced enumeration of what Meta/Facebook collects on the same axes makes the gap visible and verifiable. The page is auth-gated because it's framed as the visitor's own ledger — anonymous browsers see a sign-in invitation, not the comparator. Drawing the contrast against Meta's documented practices (citing Meta's own data policy, help centre, off-Facebook activity, and pixel pages) turns the receipt-version page (/passage) into a felt 'so what'.
features.analysis: Three transparency surfaces, each addressing a different question: /confidentialite (legal disclosure — 'what could you collect?'), /passage (receipt — 'what does THIS tab carry right now?'), /me/dossier (ledger — 'how does what you keep compare to a mainstream service?'). The ledger is the most opinionated of the three: it doesn't just enumerate what's kept, it juxtaposes that row-for-row against Meta's documented collection on the same category. Each row carries a public source URL (Meta data policy, help centre, off-Facebook activity, pixel docs) so the reader isn't asked to take Marc's word for the comparator side. The 'you are here' pointer on the collection axis is pinned at the leftmost notch — it's a state, not a slider. Keeping the comparator factually current is a recurring maintenance cost (~quarterly grep against the cited URLs); worth it because the moment the comparator goes stale, the page reads as a self-serving caricature.

decisions.decision: Auth-gated, with a quiet sign-in panel for anon visitors
decisions.rationale: Framing the page as 'your ledger' requires an account; without one, the contrast is hypothetical and the 'you are here' pointer has nowhere to anchor. The anon branch shows a one-paragraph hook with the eyebrow tag and a sign-in CTA — enough to convey what's behind the wall without leaking the full ledger to crawlers.
decisions.recommendation: Don't let the anon branch grow — it's a teaser, not a lite version. If the comparator content needs to be SEO-discoverable, fork a public marketing variant; don't merge it into this page.

decisions.decision: Cite Meta's own public docs, not third-party privacy reports
decisions.rationale: A row that says 'Meta collects X' has to be defensible at face value. Sourcing from Meta's own data policy, help centre, off-Facebook activity page, and pixel docs means the comparator can't be argued away as activist framing. The source URLs sit inline at the end of each 'theirs' cell so the reader can verify in one click.
decisions.recommendation: When refreshing the comparator, only update a row if you can find the new wording in a Meta-owned URL. Third-party investigative reports (FTC filings, news articles) are useful context but don't make it into the ledger.

decisions.decision: The 'you are here' pointer is a state, not a slider
decisions.rationale: An interactive slider implies the visitor could move it — they can't. The portal collects what it collects; that's not configurable. A fixed pointer at the leftmost notch reads as a claim of fact, which is the right register. Hover/keyboard interaction would also pull the page toward dashboard-feel and away from the felt-version aesthetic.
decisions.recommendation: Resist any 'gamification' suggestion that would turn the axis into a configurator (e.g. 'show me what it'd look like if I opted into X'). The page makes a point; configurability dilutes it.

features.successCriteria:
- A signed-in visitor sees the full ledger with sourced comparator rows side by side
- An anon visitor sees a one-paragraph hook + sign-in CTA, no comparator content
- Every comparator row carries at least one Meta-owned source URL the visitor can click through
- The 'as of' date is bumped after each comparator refresh; a stale date is a tell that the page needs a review
- The page is reachable from /passage's related-aside, from /me, and from /me/data — but not from the public site navigation
- Visual register matches /passage and /confidentialite, not /admin (dashboard chrome would defeat the felt-version framing)
knownLimitations: Comparator data ages — Meta's policies change without notice, so a row can become stale between quarterly refreshes; the asOf date is the only signal that a refresh is overdue
The collection axis is illustrative, not calibrated — 'minimal' vs. 'uncomfortable' is a qualitative read, not a measurable scale
Source URLs sometimes get re-routed by Meta to broader index pages over time; a 404 on a cited URL means refresh, not 'Meta retracted'

The page deliberately does NOT compare against every social platform (Google, TikTok, X) — adding more comparators dilutes the focused contrast and multiplies the refresh burden; if a buyer ever needs a generic 'minimal vs. mainstream' comparator, fork the pattern rather than expanding this page

Auth-gating means the ledger isn't visible to search engines or to a visitor weighing whether to sign up — that's the trade-off the framing demands; the public sibling /passage carries the lighter version

statusHistory.reason: Page shipped in f3ef8f1. /me/dossier and /en/me/dossier render the auth-gated ledger; e2e baselines committed on a298255 (me-dossier.png, en-me-dossier.png).
Code-first doc co-located post-ship. Ledger rendered, comparator sourced against Meta's own docs, asOf stamp present. Required fields complete.

features.title: Handoff checklist — operational procedure, two audiences
features.problem: /handoff explains WHAT a handoff is (two ownership modes — keys returned vs. custodian). /handoff/checklist is the companion procedural document — WHAT the buyer should expect on the day, in their own language, plus the buried technical details a future dev would need if the buyer ever migrates. The page exists because asking a Quebec tradesperson 'do you have a Cloudflare account?' requires preparation — they need to know what they're going to be asked, in plain words, ahead of time. Burying the technical details in a collapsed section keeps the page legible for the primary audience without losing the secondary audience.
features.analysis: Two-audience structure layered top-to-bottom. Sections 1–4 are buyer-facing in plain Québécois — what gets handed over (the live URL + DNS, the GitHub repo invitation, the D1 + R2 + Pages account access in 1Password format, the credentials), what they need to do in the first week (test the live URL from a fresh device, change a price in Stripe if applicable), what dormancy looks like (a single 30-day email check-in, not a 90-day-with-3-emails escalation). Section 5 is a collapsed 'Détails techniques' that a developer would open if the client migrated — asset-by-asset commands, account-level instructions, the GPG-encrypted D1 dump procedure as an opt-in (default share is the 1Password file, encrypted dumps are for buyers with their own crypto setup). The procedure was simplified from an earlier 9-section version; the README at /handoff documents the upgrade path. Page is feature-tinted as 'keys' (PAGE_FEATURE['page.handoff-checklist']) — handoff is the keys-feature, and this is its operational artifact.

decisions.decision: Two-audience structure: buyer-friendly sections 1–4, technical section 5 collapsed
decisions.rationale: A single audience document either bores the buyer with command-line instructions or leaves the future-dev guessing about account-level access. Splitting by audience and collapsing the technical side keeps the buyer page legible while the dev side stays one click away. The two audiences are real and distinct — the buyer reads this once before signing off; the future dev opens it months later when migrating.
decisions.recommendation: If a third audience emerges (e.g. compliance / Loi 25 inspectors), add a section but consider whether a separate page is cleaner. Two collapsed sections on one page is fine; three is a hint the page should be split.

decisions.decision: GPG-encrypted D1 dumps are opt-in, not the default share format
decisions.rationale: Most buyers don't have a GPG setup and don't need one — the default share is a 1Password vault with credentials + the DB password, which works for 95% of buyer profiles. The encrypted dump is for the 5% who explicitly want it (security-conscious buyers, future devs taking over). Defaulting to encrypted would block every handoff on a 30-minute GPG onboarding for the buyer; opt-in handles the case without imposing the cost.
decisions.recommendation: Keep the opt-in default. If a buyer requests the encrypted dump, walk them through gpg --import once; don't switch the default.

decisions.decision: Dormancy threshold: 30 days × 1 email, not 90 days × 3 emails
decisions.rationale: An earlier version followed the corporate-pattern 90/60/30 escalation but felt punitive for a small-practice handoff — the buyer isn't a SaaS subscriber missing payments, they're a one-off client who agreed to take the keys. A single 30-day check-in is cordial, signals 'I noticed you might want help', and exits cleanly if they don't reply. The 90-day version was creating an artificial relationship the practice doesn't want.
decisions.recommendation: Stick with 30 × 1. If a buyer regularly hits the threshold without responding, take it as a signal the handoff was the right call — the relationship has ended, that's expected.

features.successCriteria:
- A buyer reading sections 1–4 can prepare for the handoff day without opening section 5
- A future dev opening section 5 finds the commands and account-level instructions needed to migrate
- The procedure matches the actual handoff Marc performs (no drift between page and practice)
- The dormancy line is consistent with /handoff's two-mode explanation
- Page renders bilingual at full parity (FR canonical, EN mirror)
- Page is reachable from /handoff with a 'voir la check-list' CTA
knownLimitations: Section 5 (technical details) is hand-curated — adding a new asset (e.g. a new Cloudflare binding) requires editing both the buyer side ('and here's a new thing') and the technical side (the command list)
The 30-day dormancy threshold is hardcoded in the copy; if Marc ever changes practice (e.g. to 60 days for buyers in seasonal businesses), the page must be edited
The GPG dump procedure references a specific gpg version's command syntax; if the dev community migrates to a newer GPG with different defaults, the commands may need updating

The 1Password format reference assumes the buyer or future dev has a 1Password account; an OnePass-allergic buyer requires a one-off alternative (encrypted ZIP, hand-delivered USB), not documented here

statusHistory.reason: Page shipped with the simplified 5-section structure; replaced an earlier 9-section version that had ageing-out detail.
Code-first doc co-located. Two-audience structure, opt-in GPG, and 30-day dormancy decisions are stable since the 2026-05-20 simplification.

features.title: Le parcours — twelve-step walkthrough from stranger to ship
features.problem: The portal's pricing page shows the destination; /carte shows the geography; the home page shows the headline. None of those answer 'what actually happens between landing on the site and shipping a build?' /parcours is that page — twelve steps across four phases (Discover, Decide, Build, Ship) on a center-spine timeline. Each step is tagged with an actor (you / me / both) so the visitor sees at a glance that they carry half the steps, not all of them. It's the asynchronous-friendly equivalent of a 'how it works' deck, and it's what a busy prospect reads before deciding whether to send an intake.
features.analysis: Twelve steps total: Discover (you land, you read pricing, you read vibe-filter, you read showcase) → Decide (you write intake, I triage, we agree) → Build (I build, you watch live, we exchange messages) → Ship (you keep the keys, you optionally share, you optionally vouch). Each step's copy lives in DICT[lang].journey — the i18n.ts dictionary, structured as an array of { phase, actor, title, body, anchor } so the timeline structure mirrors the data shape. Alternates left/right along a center spine on desktop (~960px+) for visual rhythm; on mobile, collapses to a single column with the same actor tags. Actor tags (you / me / both) are colour-coded — you=intake hue, me=ochre, both=blended — so a quick scan answers 'whose work is this step?'. The CTA at the bottom drops the visitor at /intake. Page renders entirely from the dictionary at compile time; no fetch.

decisions.decision: Twelve steps, not three or twenty
decisions.rationale: Three is the marketing-deck default ('Discover, Decide, Done') and tells the visitor nothing useful — every consultancy says that. Twenty is too long; the visitor stops reading at step seven. Twelve is the right granularity to make each step concrete (one verb, one actor, one paragraph) without dropping below the fold. The four-phase grouping (Discover / Decide / Build / Ship) provides the mental scaffold for navigating the twelve.
decisions.recommendation: Resist both compressing to a 'short summary' (you lose the actor breakdown) and expanding to add sub-steps. Twelve is calibrated.

decisions.decision: Actor tag per step (you / me / both), not just narrative copy
decisions.rationale: A 'how it works' page that describes work as 'we do X' silently lumps the prospect's labour with the consultant's, and a busy prospect reads the page and exits thinking the workload is symmetric. Tagging each step's actor makes the asymmetry visible — most steps are 'me', the prospect's work is bounded and explicit. Same goal as the capacity-cap public counter: structural honesty about how much of the work is whose.
decisions.recommendation: When editing copy, keep the actor tag tied to the verb in the title. 'I build, you watch' is two steps, not one — split if mixing actors in one card.

decisions.decision: Center-spine timeline, not a numbered list
decisions.rationale: A numbered list reads as instructions ('do this, then this'); a timeline reads as a story ('this happens, then this happens'). The latter matches the asynchronous-first framing — the visitor is reading what unfolds, not following commands. Visual rhythm (alternating left/right) also makes scanning faster than a wall of bullet points.
decisions.recommendation: Don't 'modernize' to a vertical card list. The spine is doing visual work — scanability + story framing both come from it.

features.successCriteria:
- A visitor reading /parcours can answer 'what's MY work in this engagement?' without scrolling back
- The asymmetry (most work is Marc's) is structurally visible via actor tags, not buried in copy
- The page is 100% static at build time — no fetch, no third-party assets
- Mobile breakpoint collapses cleanly; the actor tags remain legible
- FR and EN parity at the dictionary level (typeof FR enforced via i18n.ts)
- The bottom CTA lands the visitor at /intake — the funnel-conversion point
- Copy is Québécois-register on the FR side (per project memory project_avoid_ai_tells)
knownLimitations: Mobile layout (≤720px) collapses to a single column and drops the spine ornament — the alternating rhythm only exists on wider viewports
Twelve is hardcoded as the step count; an eleven-step or thirteen-step version would require minor renderer adjustments since the :nth-child rules assume even/odd parity
Actor tag colour tokens are defined in styles.css alongside the feature hues; a new actor (e.g. 'their team' for multi-stakeholder projects) would need a new colour token

The page does NOT link to specific feature.json docs per step — a step about 'build' doesn't reference the engagement-runtime feature.json explicitly. A future enhancement could add deep links, but the current copy stands on its own

statusHistory.reason: Page shipped with the twelve-step center-spine layout; actor tags and bilingual copy verified.
Code-first doc co-located. Step count, layout, and actor-tag design all stable; the funnel role (intake CTA at bottom) is consistent with FEATURE_PRIMARY_PAGE['intake'].

features.title: Connexion — magic-link sign-in (no passwords)
features.problem: Passwords are a security AND UX liability for a low-frequency consulting portal — a visitor signs in maybe once a month, forgets the password every time, and now there's a password-reset surface to maintain, a credentials DB to leak, and the visitor's day is ruined. Magic-link sign-in skips all of that: the visitor enters an email, gets a one-time link, clicks it, lands signed-in. The portal never stores a password hash; the email account IS the credential. Resend handles delivery; the token lives in D1 with an expiry (30 min) and a single-use flag.
features.analysis: Single email field, single submit button. On submit, POST /api/auth/request-link with the email. Server validates format, looks up or creates a magic_link_tokens row with a 30-min expiry + single-use flag, and queues a Resend email containing the verification URL (/login/verify?token=...). The client doesn't see the token — it goes only to the email — so submission redirects to /login/sent which shows the 'check your email' state. If the user is already signed in (useAuth().email is set), Login.tsx short-circuits to a 'you're already signed in' panel with a 'go to /me' link instead of showing the form again. ?reason=token-... query params show specific error copy for the four magic-link verification failure modes (missing, unknown, used, expired) — those come from /api/auth/verify redirecting back here on rejection. The page is rate-limited at the API layer (per-IP, in functions/_lib/ratelimit.ts) so the form can't be used to flood somebody's inbox.

decisions.decision: Magic-link instead of password
decisions.rationale: The visitor's session is the email account anyway — owning the email means receiving the link. Adding a password layer doesn't add security (an attacker with email access bypasses it), only friction. For a portal where most visitors return once a month, the friction is high; for the consultant, eliminating the password DB removes a class of operational risk.
decisions.recommendation: Don't add a 'set a password' option even as a power-user feature. The single-credential model (email + magic link) is structurally simpler; adding a second credential creates the same security analysis as before.

decisions.decision: Always respond 200 on request-link, even for unknown emails
decisions.rationale: If the API said '404, no such account' for unknown emails, the form becomes a public account-enumeration tool. Returning a uniform 200 + 'check your email' regardless means an attacker can't tell whether an email is registered. The cost is a slightly confusing UX for someone who mistyped their email (they'll wait for a link that never arrives) — RUNBOOK §Email covers the deliverability triage.
decisions.recommendation: Keep the uniform response. If a future UX improvement is wanted, send a 'this email isn't registered' email TO the unknown address — that gives the real owner a clue without leaking the negative to an enumerator.

decisions.decision: ?reason= query params surface verify-failure modes back on /login
decisions.rationale: When /api/auth/verify rejects a token (missing, unknown, used, expired), redirecting to a dedicated 'sorry your link didn't work' page is more clicks than necessary. Sending the visitor back to /login with ?reason=X and showing a tailored line above the form is one redirect closer to recovery. The four reason codes map to the four real failure modes; an unrecognized reason value falls back to 'request a new link.'
decisions.recommendation: Don't add reason codes for new failure modes — instead, fix the underlying issue (e.g. a 'too many tokens for this email' would point at rate-limit tuning, not a new UI state).
