features.successCriteria:
- A visitor enters an email and receives a magic link within 60 seconds (delivery SLA, monitored via /admin/digest)
- The link expires after 30 min and is single-use (D1 enforces both)
- Repeated requests are per-IP rate-limited (functions/_lib/ratelimit.ts thresholds)
- An already-signed-in visitor sees a short-circuit state with a /me link, not the form
- ?reason=... query params show tailored error copy for the four verify failure modes
- The form responds uniformly 200 even for unknown emails (no enumeration)
- Page is bilingual at full parity
knownLimitations: Email deliverability depends on Resend + the recipient's spam policies — Marc's monitored DKIM/SPF/DMARC setup is correct, but transient delivery delays still happen; RUNBOOK §Email is the triage doc
Current Resend sender domain isn't yet customized to a marc-portal.com address (AUDIT P1.1 — DNS-blocked); senders may see a default Resend address in some clients
Rate limits are per-IP, not per-email — an attacker behind a proxy could request links for many emails; the cap on token count per email (server-enforced) limits the real damage
The mp_session cookie is signed (HMAC-SHA-256) but not encrypted; an attacker reading network traffic can read the email — TLS is the protection there
No 'remember this device' option; every session lives 7 days from issue, then re-magic-link

statusHistory.reason: Sign-in flow shipped end-to-end: request-link → email → verify → mp_session cookie.
Code-first doc co-located. The magic-link flow has been stable since launch; current open issue is AUDIT P1.1 (custom sender domain) which is DNS-blocked and tracked separately.

features.title: Vérifie ton courriel — magic-link sent confirmation
features.problem: After /login submits the magic-link request, the visitor needs a clear destination that explains what just happened, what they should do, and what to do if the email doesn't arrive. A bare 'thank you' line leaves them hanging; a complex page invites confusion. /login/sent is the deliberate plain-text confirmation — one line that says 'we sent it to X, expires in 30 min', one line of reassurance ('you can always request another'), one fallback ('check spam or try a different email'). The email address comes via ?email= query param so the visitor sees their own input echoed back.
features.analysis: Single landing page after POST /api/auth/request-link succeeds. /login navigates here with ?email=<typed-value>; this page reads that param to render 'we sent a link to <email>'. If the param is missing (direct hit, reload, manual navigation), the copy falls back to a generic 'your link is on its way' — same information without the personalization that would otherwise render as 'we sent a link to .' (broken empty case). The page also offers a 'send another link' affordance that navigates back to /login. There's no auto-refresh, no polling for verification — clicking the link in the email takes the visitor to /api/auth/verify which redirects to /me on success. The 30-minute expiry is mentioned explicitly so the visitor doesn't sit on the page expecting magic.

decisions.decision: Read email from query param, fall back gracefully when missing
decisions.rationale: Reading from query param means /login can do a navigate(/login/sent?email=...) without coordinating state with this page. A direct hit (reload, bookmark, manual URL) loses the param — the fallback copy keeps the page readable as a generic 'your link was sent' rather than degenerating to 'we sent a link to .' Lossy state recovery via redo is the right pattern here; the empty case is rare and benign.
decisions.recommendation: Don't store the email in sessionStorage to 'recover' it across reloads — that adds a small data footprint (email in browser state) for almost no UX gain. The fallback copy is enough.

decisions.decision: No auto-refresh, no polling for verification status
decisions.rationale: Polling would require the page to know whether the user clicked the link in their email, which would either require a server endpoint exposing token-verification status (leak) or polling the user's own /me endpoint (which would 401 until verified). Both add complexity for a flow that already works — the visitor clicks the link in the email and lands on /me directly, this page is irrelevant once they click.
decisions.recommendation: Resist 'add a real-time refresh' UX requests. The flow is asynchronous by design; the email is the signal, not the page.

features.successCriteria:
- A visitor arriving via /login submit sees 'we sent a link to <their-email>' with the 30-minute expiry mentioned
- A direct hit (no email param) shows a graceful generic 'link sent' message — not a broken empty case
- The 'send another' affordance navigates back to /login
- No polling, no auto-refresh; the email click is the next event
- Page is bilingual at full parity
knownLimitations: ?email is plain text in the URL — not a security issue (it's an email the visitor just typed), but it's logged in browser history and could appear in shoulder-surfing scenarios
The 30-minute expiry copy is hardcoded; if the server-side TTL is ever changed (functions/_lib/auth.ts magic_link_tokens schema), this page must be edited to match

No analytics on this page; we don't know what fraction of visitors stall here vs. complete the magic-link click — manual instrumentation in /admin/digest only counts deliveries, not user-side completion

statusHistory.reason: Page shipped as part of the magic-link flow; /login navigates here on submit, fallback copy handles direct-hit case.
Code-first doc co-located. Behaviour is minimal and stable; no plans to add polling or refresh.

features.title: La carte du site — layered atlas of every page and data flow
features.problem: A solo consulting portal accumulates surfaces fast — public marketing, auth-gated dashboard, admin operator hub, the showcase gallery, transparency pages. Without a map, a curious visitor doesn't know what's here, and a returning client can't orient. A traditional sitemap link list is a 200-entry text dump that nobody reads. /carte renders the same information as a graph the visitor can actually navigate — layered by intent (pages / data flow / journeys / admin), filterable by feature colour, with admin surfaces hidden from public viewers.
features.analysis: Four layered views on the same MapData (defined in src/lib/map/data.ts): Pages (every reachable route + the home-section anchors), Data flow (which features read/write which D1 tables and R2 buckets), Journeys (visitor walk-throughs — intake-to-engagement, browsing-the-showcase, etc.), Admin (the operator surfaces). MapCanvas wraps Cytoscape.js — chosen because the layout is non-trivial (forces, edge routing) and the boring-tech mandate rules out a hand-rolled SVG renderer. Public viewers (signed-out OR admin-with-previewAsUser-active) see admin nodes filtered out via filterForViewer; admin viewers see everything. The visitor-preview flag is the same sessionStorage key the rest of the chrome uses (mp_preview_as_user), so toggling here matches toggling from the header — one preview state across the whole site. ?feature=X URL param filters the graph to one feature's surfaces; FeatureFolioLink components inside the legend hand off to it.

decisions.decision: Cytoscape.js for layout, not D3 or a hand-rolled SVG
decisions.rationale: Force-directed layouts with edge routing are hard. D3 makes you build the layout pipeline; a hand-rolled SVG would mean writing collision detection and zoom/pan from scratch. Cytoscape ships both with a stable API and reasonable bundle size. The boring-tech mandate isn't 'no libraries' — it's 'no libraries you'll have to babysit' — and cytoscape has been on the same major version for years.
decisions.recommendation: If a future requirement forces a layout cytoscape can't do (e.g. hand-authored spatial positions), reach for the cytoscape preset layout, not a rewrite.

decisions.decision: Filter admin nodes via the same previewAsUser flag the chrome uses
decisions.rationale: Two sources of truth for 'should I see admin surfaces?' would silently diverge. Reusing mp_preview_as_user means a session-wide preview toggle in the header dims admin everywhere consistently — including on /carte. The flag is sessionStorage-scoped so it doesn't persist across tabs (a fresh admin tab sees admin surfaces).
decisions.recommendation: Don't add a /carte-local preview toggle. If the header's preview controls grow (e.g. a 'preview as different role'), thread it through filterForViewer rather than duplicating the predicate.

decisions.decision: MAP_DATA is hand-curated, not derived from the route table
decisions.rationale: A derived map would only know what react-router knows — paths, components. It wouldn't know which surfaces belong to which feature, what the data-flow edges are, or what journey a visitor follows from intake to ship. The hand-curated MapData encodes intent; map.test.ts guards consistency with the auto-discoverable route skeleton so they can't silently drift apart.
decisions.recommendation: When adding a new route, add a node to MAP_DATA in the same PR. The test will fail loudly if you forget. Adding a node without a feature assignment will also fail — every node needs a feature.

features.successCriteria:
- A visitor opens /carte and can see every public surface of the site as a graph, coloured by feature, with admin nodes hidden
- An admin viewer sees the full graph including admin surfaces
- The visitor-preview toggle in the header dims admin nodes here too (one preview state across the site)
- ?feature=X URL param filters the graph to one feature's surfaces; deep-linkable from anywhere on the site
- Four layer toggles (Pages / Data flow / Journeys / Admin) work independently — the visitor can stack journeys on top of pages or view just data flow
- The page makes zero runtime fetches (Loi 25 + no-analytics posture)
- map.test.ts catches any drift between MAP_DATA and the route skeleton at PR time
knownLimitations: Cytoscape's layout is force-directed, so node positions are NOT stable across page loads — a screenshot taken today won't pixel-match tomorrow's render; the e2e suite captures /carte but with a generous diff threshold for this reason
MAP_DATA is hand-curated — a route added without a corresponding node is invisible on the graph until someone notices, though map.test.ts catches the drift in CI
Cytoscape adds ~350KB to the lazy chunk; the map page is intentionally lazy-loaded so this never affects the home/intake/login critical path
The Data flow layer doesn't auto-derive edges from code analysis — it's curated. A new D1 table read/write requires a manual MapData edge or it won't show
Screen-reader navigation of a force-directed graph is limited; the FeatureIndex panel is the AT-friendly counterpart and lists everything the graph shows

statusHistory.reason: Page shipped with MAP_DATA + Cytoscape canvas + four layer toggles. Public + admin viewers verified.
Code-first doc co-located. Filtering, layering, feature index, and preview-toggle behaviours all stable; map.test.ts continues to guard MAP_DATA invariants.

features.title: Mon espace — signed-in client dashboard
features.problem: After magic-link sign-in, the client lands on /me — the single canonical place where their sessions, their data, and their account live. Without one canonical destination, a returning client has to remember 'where do I check my session?' across multiple URLs. /me consolidates: a tile of their sessions (with status + unread counts), a tile for starting a new intake, payment actions if a session is in pricing, language preference, optional display-name (first name), and the data-rights surface (download my data / delete my account). The page IS the client-side console; everything else (intake form, individual session views, /me/data) is reachable from it.
features.analysis: Auth-gated via useAuth() — a signed-out visitor is redirected to /login. Once authenticated, the page loads the session list via listSessions() (functions/api/sessions/index.ts via src/lib/sessionsApi.ts), an array of SessionRow records: id, problem_type, status, created_at, last_message_at, has_unread (computed via src/lib/unread.ts against per-session last-seen markers in localStorage). Each session renders as a card linking to /session/:id; unread sessions show a dot. Below the list: tiles for new intake (links into /intake with a fresh PendingIntake set up via PENDING_INTAKE_KEY localStorage handoff), payment actions (if any session is in pricing — PaymentActions component renders Stripe Checkout button), language preference (LangPrefCard), display name (FirstNameCard). At the bottom: data-rights — a 'download my data' link to /me/data and a destructive 'delete my account' action that calls deleteMyAccount(), sets markJustErased() (sessionStorage flag), then navigates to /au-revoir for the post-erasure ritual. Bilingual at full parity. The 'console' eyebrow framing signals this is operational, not marketing.

decisions.decision: Tile layout instead of a vertical list of sections
decisions.rationale: The dashboard has 5–6 concerns (sessions, new intake, payments, language, name, data rights). A vertical list buries data rights at the bottom; a tile layout makes each concern equally visible and accessible. Mobile collapses tiles to a single column; the visual rhythm holds.
decisions.recommendation: Keep tiles. If a seventh concern emerges, consider whether it belongs on /me at all or under a sub-route — adding tiles indefinitely turns the dashboard into a control panel.

decisions.decision: Delete-my-account hands off to /au-revoir via sessionStorage flag, not a confirmation modal
decisions.rationale: A modal-based 'are you sure?' flow followed by a server call is the dashboard-default but feels jarring for an erasure ritual. The actual confirmation lives at the API layer (DELETE /api/me requires CSRF, requires sign-in); the UX flow is: click delete → action fires → navigate to /au-revoir which carries the emotional weight of the moment. The user has time to see the post-erasure ritual instead of being dumped back on a now-empty dashboard.
decisions.recommendation: Don't add a confirm-via-typing pattern ('type DELETE to confirm') — the magic-link sign-in is itself the confirmation that this is the account owner. The /au-revoir ritual is the right amount of weight for the action.

decisions.decision: Unread tracking is per-device via localStorage, not per-account via D1
decisions.rationale: A client signing in from their phone after seeing a message on desktop shouldn't have to wade through unread-on-the-phone artifacts of what they already read. Per-device unread is the right UX even at the cost of cross-device unread state not syncing. The cost: a client clearing their browser data loses their unread state; a clean session shows everything as unread until first visit. Both are acceptable.
decisions.recommendation: Don't sync unread state via the server. If multi-device sync becomes a real complaint, consider a per-session 'last opened' timestamp on the server — but it complicates the model significantly.

features.successCriteria:
- A signed-in visitor lands on /me and sees their sessions, with unread indicators that match what they've read
- A signed-out visitor is redirected to /login (not shown a 401 page)
- 'New intake' navigates into /intake with the pending-intake state primed so /intake doesn't ask 'who are you?' again
- Delete-my-account triggers the full DELETE /api/me + /au-revoir ritual flow
- Language and display-name changes persist via PATCH /api/me/prefs
- Payment actions render only for sessions in pricing status
- Bilingual at full parity
knownLimitations: Per-device unread state isn't synced across devices — a session read on desktop shows as unread on mobile until opened there
If localStorage is cleared, all sessions appear as unread on next visit until each is opened (acceptable trade-off)

PaymentActions only renders for sessions currently in 'pricing' status — if a session is paid and then moves to building, the action disappears (which is correct), but a visitor coming back to /me later won't see history of past payments here; /session/:id is the per-session payment history
FirstNameCard / LangPrefCard each PATCH /api/me/prefs — there's no batch save; rapid edits could cause minor server load (unbatched)

The dashboard fetches the full session list at mount; for a client with many sessions, this could grow — capacity cap means the count stays bounded, but a future infinite-session model would need pagination

statusHistory.reason: Page shipped with tile layout, session list, intake handoff, and delete-my-account flow.
Added markJustErased() handoff so the delete-my-account action navigates to /au-revoir with the ritual flag set; integrates with the ton-passage arc.
Code-first doc co-located. Erasure flow now end-to-end with the /au-revoir ritual; behaviour stable.

features.title: Sous le capot — the portal documents itself, publicly
features.problem: Most consulting portfolios show the polished result and hide the construction. That asymmetry feels dishonest to a buyer who's been burned before — they want to know HOW the work gets done, not just what shipped. /meta inverts the default: every feature documented in the codebase is rendered as a public card. Click one to expand the full record — problem, implementation, decisions (with rationales), success criteria, known limitations, status history. The same document the dev reads when modifying the code is the document the visitor reads to evaluate the dev. Build-in-public, structurally: no separate marketing artifact, no curated 'about how we work' page — just the feature.jsons that already drive the code.
features.analysis: Reads src/data/lac-features.json — the manifest written at prebuild by scripts/build-lac-meta.mjs from every *.feature.json co-located in the codebase. Each feature renders as a card with the title + status pill + tags; clicking expands it in place to show the full record. Status history is rendered chronologically (draft → active → frozen, with reasons + dates). Decisions display with their rationale + recommendation, not just the headline — that's what makes the page useful to a reader trying to evaluate the practice. The Scorecard component up top tallies coverage: how many features are frozen vs. active vs. draft, mean staleness in days, total decisions captured. A 'voir en direct' link on each card jumps to the documented surface so the visitor can read the doc and the artifact side by side. The page is static after prebuild — no runtime API call, the manifest is fixed at deploy time.

decisions.decision: The page renders directly from the same feature.json files the code uses — no separate marketing copy
decisions.rationale: If /meta read from a hand-curated 'public-facing version' of the docs, the public version would silently drift from reality the first time someone changed a feature.json without remembering to update the mirror. By rendering the same files, drift is structurally impossible — there's one source of truth.
decisions.recommendation: If a particular field needs to read differently for a public audience (e.g. trim the implementation prose), edit the feature.json itself. The internal LAC reader should be reading the public-style copy too — that's the point.

decisions.decision: Manifest is static at prebuild, not fetched at runtime
decisions.rationale: Loi 25 + boring-tech: no runtime fetch means no server log of who reads the meta page, no 503 path, no API to maintain. The manifest is also tiny (~50KB) so bundling it has zero performance cost. A future ten-fold growth in feature count would still be sub-megabyte.
decisions.recommendation: If the manifest ever crosses ~500KB, code-split per-feature: keep the index static, lazy-load individual feature docs. Don't introduce a runtime API.

decisions.decision: Show the full decision record — rationale and recommendation, not just headlines
decisions.rationale: A bulleted list of 'we decided X' reads like a marketing artifact and tells the reader nothing about why. A list with 'we decided X because Y, recommendation Z' reads like an engineering record and lets a buyer evaluate the judgment, not just the outcome. The cost is page length; the payoff is signal.
decisions.recommendation: When authoring a feature.json's decisions, write the rationale to a stranger. Don't shorthand internal context — assume the /meta reader is reading this as their first encounter with the project.

features.successCriteria:
- Every feature.json in the codebase appears on /meta at next deploy, with no additional manual step
- Status pills, status history, decisions, success criteria, and known limitations are all surfaced verbatim from the source files
- The Scorecard reflects current coverage stats at every deploy
- The 'voir en direct' link on each card resolves to the documented surface, working in both FR and EN
- The page makes zero runtime fetches
- A reader using /meta to evaluate the practice can see the same record an internal contributor reads
knownLimitations: lac-features.json is regenerated at prebuild — a feature.json edited but not committed before deploy won't appear until the next build runs

Schema validation in build-lac-meta.mjs is lenient (warn but don't fail) — a malformed feature.json shows up here with a 'flagged' marker rather than blocking the build; the rationale is that one bad feature.json shouldn't break the deploy
Decision records grow over time and never auto-collapse — a feature with 12 decisions reads as a long expanded card; readers are expected to scroll or skim by tags

/meta itself does not have a Scorecard entry for 'how many features.json are missing' — FEATURE-DOCS.md is the companion audit; the two are intentionally separate (public-facing showcase vs. internal gap tracker)

statusHistory.reason: Page shipped reading the LAC manifest; cards expand in place with full decision records.
Code-first doc co-located. Public showcase of the lifecycle continues to mirror the internal LAC reader's view 1:1.

features.title: Mes données — Loi 25 right-of-access, as a readable document
features.problem: Loi 25's right-of-access obligates the practice to give the client a copy of everything held about them, in a portable form. The default interpretation — a machine-readable JSON download — satisfies the law but reads as dismissive. A client asking 'what do you have on me?' deserves an answer they can actually read. /me/data renders the same export the JSON download contains, but as a designed plain-language document — everything held about you, on one page, in clear words, printable. The JSON download still exists for portability; this page is the trust statement built on the same data.
features.analysis: Auth-gated via useAuth(). On mount, calls exportMyData() (src/lib/export.ts) which hits /api/me with an Accept-bundle header and returns an ExportBundle: account info, every session's intake JSON (parsed via getSchemaForType), every message in chronological order, attachment metadata (filenames + sizes, not the blobs themselves — those are linked separately via attachment URLs). The page then renders this as a structured document with one section per session — eyebrow, IntakeSummary (the same component /admin/inbox uses, reused on the public side), message thread, attachment list. Above all that: an account section showing the email + display name + lang preference + the JSON download button. Sibling page /me/dossier is the comparator version ('vs. Meta'); /passage is the receipt version (cookies + localStorage + walked routes). Three angles on the same Loi 25 obligation, pitched at different curiosity levels.

decisions.decision: Render the export as a readable document, NOT just offer the JSON
decisions.rationale: Loi 25 doesn't say the export must be human-readable — only portable. But a JSON download is the bare-minimum compliance posture and reads as 'we did the legal minimum.' Rendering the same data as a designed page converts the obligation into a trust statement: 'here it is, in clear words.' The JSON button is still there for portability (e.g. the client wants to load into a personal archive); the readable page is the default.
decisions.recommendation: Keep both. If pressure ever comes to drop the JSON button to 'save bytes', resist — the right-of-access flow benefits from offering both forms.

decisions.decision: Reuse IntakeSummary from /admin/inbox to render each session's intake
decisions.rationale: The same intake form is rendered in three places: the admin sees it on /admin/inbox/:id, the client sees it on /session/:id (when in triage), and again here on /me/data. Reusing IntakeSummary means all three views show the same record; a refactor to intake schemas propagates to all three at once. This is one of the things the LAC primitive 'engagement-runtime' captures.
decisions.recommendation: When adding new intake schemas, plug them into IntakeSummary; don't add a /me/data-only renderer. Same display = no drift.

decisions.decision: Print-friendly via @media print, not a separate /me/data?format=print
decisions.rationale: A separate print URL is two surfaces to maintain; CSS @media print is one stylesheet. The chrome (header, footer, page mast) drops; the document structure remains; the result is a clean paper artifact. A client wanting to keep a physical copy of 'everything they hold on me' has a one-click path (browser print → save as PDF or print to paper).
decisions.recommendation: Test print preview after structural changes to the page — the print stylesheet can silently break when new sections are added that need their own print rules.

features.successCriteria:
- A signed-in client opens /me/data and sees every piece of data the portal holds about them, in plain language
- The JSON download produces a portable bundle that matches the page content (no fields hidden)
- Attachment metadata is shown; attachment blobs are reachable via separate per-attachment URLs (not bundled in the JSON)
- IntakeSummary renders each session's intake exactly as /admin/inbox would render the same intake
- Print preview produces a clean paper-ready document
- A signed-out visitor is redirected to /login (not shown a 401 or empty state)
- The page is bilingual at full parity
knownLimitations: Attachment blobs are NOT inlined in the JSON download — only filenames + URLs; a full archive would require zipping R2 objects which is deferred (no real complaint yet)

The page does NOT include past, soft-deleted sessions — by design, soft-delete is 'gone from the client view'; if a client wants to see soft-deleted sessions, they're past the right-of-access window and we shouldn't pretend otherwise
Print stylesheet is hand-tuned for the current layout; a major redesign of the page requires re-tuning the print rules
exportMyData makes one network call that fetches everything; for a client with many sessions this could grow large — capacity cap and per-session size limits keep the bundle well under 1MB today
There's no 'export to CSV' option — JSON is the only machine-readable format on offer; if a future client requests CSV, the JSON-to-CSV is one client-side script away

statusHistory.reason: Page shipped with the readable-document layout + JSON download; satisfies Loi 25 right-of-access for the practice.
Code-first doc co-located. Behaviour is stable; sibling /me/dossier + /passage extensions of the same Loi 25 surface shipped in f3ef8f1.

features.title: Ton passage — café receipt of the current visit
features.problem: A Loi 25 privacy policy and a PIA tell a visitor what data the site MIGHT keep about them in general. They don't tell the visitor what their own tab is carrying right now. The legal documents are correct but abstract; an honest practice should also offer a concrete reading the visitor can verify themselves. /passage is the felt-version of /confidentialite — same truth, different register — rendered as a café receipt the visitor can print and keep.
features.analysis: Two registers for the same data: /confidentialite is the legal disclosure (what the practice could collect under what conditions), /passage is the receipt (what THIS tab carries right at this moment). The legal page proves intent; the receipt proves practice. A visitor reading both sees that the legal page isn't an aspirational stub — the receipt confirms cookies, localStorage, and the walked-pages log are exactly what the policy says they are. Café-bill aesthetic is the load-bearing choice: a receipt is a familiar artifact that frames data exposure as a small, mundane, totalled-at-the-bottom thing rather than a privacy crisis. Total: $0.00. The print button hands the receipt off to the browser's print dialog so the visitor can keep a physical copy — that's the receipt metaphor pulled all the way through, and a quiet anti-AI-tell move (an AI-generated transparency page would render a dashboard, not a thermal-printer receipt).

decisions.decision: Browser-only reads — nothing leaves the page
decisions.rationale: The whole point is for the visitor to verify their own tab; routing through a server would defeat the felt-version framing AND introduce a server log of who read their receipt. document.cookie + window.localStorage + sessionStorage give a complete picture without a network call.
decisions.recommendation: Never add a server roundtrip here. If a future field requires server data (e.g. 'sessions on this account'), make it a separate signed-in surface and link to it from the related-aside — don't sneak it into the receipt.

decisions.decision: Café-receipt aesthetic, not a dashboard
decisions.rationale: A privacy dashboard reads as ChatGPT output — gradient cards, status pills, big numbers. A thermal-printer receipt reads as a small business owing you nothing. The aesthetic IS the message: data exposure framed as mundane, totalled, paid. The same content rendered as a dashboard would undermine the practice's anti-AI-tells stance documented in CLAUDE.md.
decisions.recommendation: Keep the mono font, the rule-divided blocks, the 'Total: 0,00 $' line, and the print button. If a designer tempts you toward 'modernizing' the look, the felt version disappears.

decisions.decision: Anon visitors get a sparser receipt, not a degraded one
decisions.rationale: A logged-out tab has no mp_session cookie, no /me draft in localStorage, no walked-pages from past sessions. Showing 'aucun cookie servi' for that visitor is honest — it's not a less-good version of the receipt, it's the correct receipt for a visitor that gave us nothing.
decisions.recommendation: Don't conditionally render a 'sign in to see your real receipt' nudge. The empty receipt IS the win — proves the site doesn't quietly load state behind the visitor's back.

features.successCriteria:
- A visitor opens /passage and reads, in plain words, what cookies + localStorage their tab is carrying right now
- The page makes zero network calls beyond the SPA's normal data fetches (which the receipt itself lists)
- A logged-out visitor sees an empty-but-honest receipt with the same affective frame as the populated one
- Print produces a readable physical receipt (single column, mono font, no decorative chrome)
- The aesthetic distinguishes it from /confidentialite at a glance — café receipt, not legal disclosure
- The related-aside lands a curious visitor on the deeper docs (/me/dossier, /confidentialite, /pia) without trapping them on /passage
knownLimitations: The receipt is a render-time snapshot — navigating away and back gets a fresh snapshot, not a persisted history
Cookies are filtered to mp_* prefix; the page does NOT show third-party or generic cookies (there shouldn't be any, but the filter would hide them if they appeared)
Cookie descriptions are hard-coded; a new mp_* cookie added to the codebase will show as 'portal-internal' until a description row is added to COPY.cookieDescriptions
sessionStorage in private/incognito modes may be unavailable — the walked-pages block shows an honest empty state in that case

The receipt's 'Demandes en cuisine (API)' block is static prose, not a live log — accurate to the SPA's actual fetches but not auto-derived; a new public route added to the boot path requires editing the apisBody string

statusHistory.reason: Page shipped in f3ef8f1 alongside src/lib/visitTracker.ts. /passage and /en/passage render the receipt for FR/EN visitors; rendered, prerendered, and e2e-screenshotted at baselines/passage.png + baselines/en-passage.png.
Code-first doc co-located post-ship. All required fields present; behavior confirmed in dev + e2e baselines regenerated on a298255.

features.title: EFVP — Loi 25 Privacy Impact Assessments, in public
features.problem: Loi 25 art. 3.3 requires a Privacy Impact Assessment (EFVP in French) before adopting any third-party processing of personal data. The portal uses two: Sentry (cross-border, US) for error monitoring and Stripe (Canadian entity) for payments. The Loi 25 article doesn't require these PIAs to be public, but keeping them at /pia — instead of 'available on request' — converts a compliance artifact into a transparency move that a buyer in due diligence can read without scheduling a call. The canonical markdown sources live at docs/loi-25-pia.md and docs/loi-25-pia-stripe.md; this page mirrors them faithfully.
features.analysis: Two PIA records on one page, anchored. Each follows the Loi 25 art. 3.3 template: purpose of the transfer, data categories, recipient + their jurisdiction, retention period, mitigations applied, residual risk acceptance, and annual review schedule. Sentry PIA: error monitoring; the receiving entity is Functional Software Inc. (US); mitigations include the four-part bundle documented in the project's memory (PIA + privacy-page §6 + code minimization + operator checklist). Stripe PIA: payments; the receiving entity is Stripe Payments Canada Ltd. (Canadian processing entity, no cross-border transfer at the legal level even though the parent is US). Each PIA section has its own anchor (#sentry, #stripe) so /confidentialite §6 and §11 deep-link in. The page is bilingual but the technical PIA text is intentionally only in English — that's the language a juriste or the Commission d'accès à l'information would expect for a compliance artifact. The FR intro explains why and what the reader is about to see.

decisions.decision: Publish PIAs at /pia rather than 'available on request'
decisions.rationale: Loi 25 doesn't require publication, but 'available on request' adds a step that buyers' legal teams routinely skip during evaluation. Publishing converts the compliance artifact into a trust signal — a competitor running cross-border without a PIA looks worse by comparison, and the buyer can verify our claims in 60 seconds instead of an email round-trip. The cost is keeping the page current (annual review per Loi 25, plus on every processor change).
decisions.recommendation: Don't soften the publication to 'available on request' just because the law allows. Keep the page; it earns its keep as a trust signal more than as a compliance artifact.

decisions.decision: PIA technical body in English only, bilingual intro
decisions.rationale: PIAs are read by lawyers, by the Commission d'accès à l'information, and by buyers' diligence teams. All three groups operate in English for technical-legal vocabulary; translating the body would risk introducing legal-meaning drift between FR and EN versions. The intro is bilingual so any FR-only visitor understands what they're looking at and why the body switches language; the canonical markdown sources at docs/ are also English-only.
decisions.recommendation: Don't translate the PIA bodies. If a Loi 25 enforcement action ever requires a French-language version, generate it at that moment with a legal translator, don't maintain two languages in parallel forever.

decisions.decision: Stripe PIA treats Stripe Payments Canada Ltd. as the recipient, not Stripe Inc.
decisions.rationale: Stripe operates a Canadian processing entity that handles all merchant operations originating in Canada; Stripe Inc. (US parent) doesn't receive the personal data at the legal level. Treating the Canadian entity as the recipient is structurally accurate and removes the 'cross-border' framing the buyer would otherwise need to evaluate. The PIA still discloses the parent-subsidiary relationship for full disclosure.
decisions.recommendation: If Stripe ever consolidates Canadian operations into the US entity (publicly announced via Stripe's contracts page), update the PIA to reflect the new flow and re-evaluate the cross-border mitigations.

features.successCriteria:
- Both PIAs (Sentry, Stripe) are fully rendered and reachable at /pia
- /confidentialite §6 anchors deep-link into the Sentry PIA; §11 into the Stripe PIA
- PIA bodies match the canonical sources in docs/loi-25-pia*.md exactly (no copy-edit drift)
- 'Dernière mise à jour' date reflects the most recent PIA edit
- A buyer in due diligence can answer 'what processors does this site use, what data goes to each, where, and for how long?' without leaving the page
- The page is bilingual at the intro layer; the technical bodies are English-only by design
knownLimitations: The PIA bodies are duplicated between Pia.tsx and docs/loi-25-pia*.md — there's no build step that asserts they match; drift is caught only on manual review
Anchors (#sentry, #stripe) are hardcoded — adding a third processor requires extending both Pia.tsx and /confidentialite's link list
Annual review isn't automated; a Loi 25 audit could find an out-of-date PIA if Marc forgets the calendar reminder — known and accepted, the cost-benefit of an automation here is low
Technical body is English-only by design — a French-only Loi 25 inspector would need to read English; the intro explains why this trade-off was made

statusHistory.reason: Page shipped with Sentry PIA; followed by Stripe PIA addition on 2026-05-17.
Code-first doc co-located. Both PIAs live, deep-link from /confidentialite, and mirror their docs/ sources. The publication-as-trust-signal decision held.

features.title: Politique de confidentialité — Loi 25 disclosure
features.problem: The footer claim 'Hébergé au Canada · Loi 25' is structurally dishonest without a published policy with a designated person to write to. /confidentialite is that page. Loi 25 (the Quebec adaptation of Canada's PIPEDA) mandates eight specific disclosures — designated person, data collected and purposes, consent + how to withdraw, right of access, right of rectification, right of erasure/portability, retention period, breach notification process. The page lays those out plainly because the practice is small (one human, one DB, transactional emails) and a 200-page boilerplate would itself be a lie. /pia is the companion page covering the two third-party processors (Sentry, Stripe) under Loi 25 art. 3.3.
features.analysis: Eight numbered sections, each addressing one Loi 25 obligation. The sections are deliberately short — Marc is the designated person (one human, no DPO consultant intermediary), data collected is minimal (email + intake + messages + attachments), consent is given by submitting the intake form, retention is bounded (12 months post-engagement OR immediate on DELETE /api/me), and breach notification follows the 72-hour standard. Sections 6 and 11 link to /pia anchors for the two third-party processors. Section 8 (right of erasure) is paired with /me's delete action — the legal text and the operational surface are consistent. The page uses the same PageMast + SectionEyebrow + FeatureFolioLink chrome as other content surfaces, in the meta-page colour, with a folio-mark in the footer that links back to /carte. Updates to the policy bump the 'En vigueur' date at the top — copy is deliberately not generated, this is a hand-authored compliance artifact.

decisions.decision: Marc is the designated person, no DPO consultant in the chain
decisions.rationale: Loi 25 requires a named individual responsible for privacy. For a one-person solo practice, putting a separate DPO consultant in the chain adds latency to access requests and complicates the breach-notification flow. Marc IS the practice; making him the DPO is structurally honest and operationally simpler. The 30-day response window required by Loi 25 is achievable for a solo dev only if the chain is short.
decisions.recommendation: If the practice ever takes on contractors who touch personal data, the role can remain Marc — contractors don't become DPOs automatically. Re-evaluate only if a buyer takes over the platform (then the buyer becomes the named person).

decisions.decision: Plain language, not boilerplate
decisions.rationale: A 200-section policy on a one-human practice is itself a misrepresentation — it implies a corporate structure that doesn't exist. Loi 25 doesn't require length; it requires the eight disclosures. Writing them plainly (and in Québécois register for the FR side) is more truthful and easier to enforce internally.
decisions.recommendation: When updating the policy, resist the urge to add 'just to be safe' boilerplate. If a new clause is needed, it's needed because something operationally changed. Document the operational change in a related feature.json too.

decisions.decision: Sentry and Stripe each get their own PIA at /pia rather than inline here
decisions.rationale: Loi 25 art. 3.3 requires a PIA before adopting any third-party processor. /confidentialite §6 and §11 reference Sentry and Stripe respectively but the full PIA documents live at /pia. Reasons: keeps the policy short and readable, the PIA template (purpose, data, retention, mitigations, residual risk) doesn't naturally fit inside the policy's sectioned flow, and a buyer's legal team doing due diligence wants the PIAs together as one artifact.
decisions.recommendation: When adding a new third-party processor (e.g. Resend for transactional email), author a PIA at /pia and reference it from the relevant /confidentialite section. Don't inline the PIA text.

features.successCriteria:
- The page covers all eight Loi 25 obligations explicitly
- The designated person (Marc) is named with a contact email
- The right-of-erasure language matches /me's DELETE behaviour exactly (immediate, no 30-day retention)
- /pia anchors are reachable from §6 (Sentry) and §11 (Stripe)
- The page is bilingual with parity (FR canonical, EN mirror); register is Québécois on the FR side
- The 'En vigueur' date is bumped on every policy change
- A reader doing due diligence can answer 'what data do they collect, why, and how do I withdraw consent?' without leaving the page
knownLimitations: The 'En vigueur' date is hardcoded — there's no automation that forces it to be bumped when COPY changes; reviewing the date is part of the manual release ritual

Inline HTML in section paragraphs is rendered via dangerouslySetInnerHTML to support anchor links and mailto: — the content is hand-written so injection isn't a vector, but a future contributor should NOT generalize this to user-controlled data

The page lists the third-party processors explicitly; adding/removing a processor requires editing both /confidentialite and /pia — there's no shared source. A future refactor could lift the processor list to a manifest, but two-file edits are tractable at this scale
Bilingual parity is enforced by reading and editing both sides during a policy update — no compile-time check ties FR and EN sections to each other

statusHistory.reason: Page shipped covering all eight Loi 25 obligations with Marc named as designated person; first version in production.
Code-first doc co-located. Policy text has been updated twice since launch (Sentry PIA addition 2026-05-16, Stripe PIA reference 2026-05-17); the page structure and decisions are stable.

features.title: Share view — public advancements with time-travel scrubber
features.problem: A client wants to share a work-in-progress with a stakeholder (their accountant, a peer reviewer, their own client) without giving the recipient a portal account. /share/:id is the URL-as-capability — anyone holding it can see the advancements an admin has flagged allowedForPublic for that session. Nothing else about the session leaks. The session id is a 72-bit base64url token; guessing one is astronomically unlikely, so the URL is the access control. Pair with the time-travel scrubber (a play/pause + step-through interface) so the recipient can see how the build evolved over its arc, not just the current snapshot. This is how 'show, don't tell' becomes a deployable URL.
features.analysis: Unauthenticated read-only view. Renders /api/public/sessions/:id/advancements (functions/api/public/sessions/[id]/advancements.ts) which queries D1 for advancements where session_id=:id AND allowed_for_public=1 AND session.deleted_at IS NULL. The session id is the capability — no other auth check beyond 'does the id exist and have public advancements'. Each advancement carries an iframe-embeddable build URL (the per-build preview deploy from a feature.json revision); the TimeTravelScrubber component (src/components/TimeTravelScrubber.tsx) takes the array and provides play/pause + arrow-key stepping + a ?step=N URL param so any scrub position is shareable. Vouches scoped to this session are loaded in parallel (one round-trip) so the recipient sees both the work and the social proof on the same page. When zero vouches exist for the session, the block falls back to a CTA-only block — that's the steady state for fresh projects, not a degraded view. Page is meta-tinted as 'iterative' (PAGE_FEATURE['page.public-advancements']) since it's the iterative builds feature surfaced publicly.

decisions.decision: URL-as-capability with 72-bit base64url ids, not separate share tokens
decisions.rationale: A separate share-token table doubles the schema and the operational complexity (who issued the token, when, can it be revoked?). The session id itself is already a 72-bit secret — guessing one is ~10^-22 per attempt. The trade-off: there's no way to revoke a leaked URL short of soft-deleting the whole session. That's acceptable because the public projection only includes advancements explicitly flagged allowedForPublic; revoking access means clearing those flags in /admin/inbox/:id, which works without changing the URL.
decisions.recommendation: If a future requirement demands per-recipient revocable links, add a share_tokens table without changing the existing /share/:id semantics. Don't try to make session ids revocable — they're load-bearing in too many other places.

decisions.decision: 404 the recipient if no public advancements exist — don't differentiate 'no such session' from 'session has nothing public'
decisions.rationale: Differentiating would leak the existence of sessions whose admin hasn't flagged anything public yet. A consistent 404 across both cases preserves zero-knowledge about session existence to anyone holding only an id. The admin who SHIPPED the session knows whether to expect content; the public reader doesn't need that signal.
decisions.recommendation: Keep the 404 generic. The admin error path (their session shouldn't be 404'ing the public link) is caught in /admin/inbox/:id which shows the public-flag count.

decisions.decision: Embed builds via iframe; ShareModal exposes a 'open in new tab' option
decisions.rationale: Iframe lets the recipient see the build in context (chrome, layout, surrounding scrubber affordances) without leaving the share page. But some builds (Stripe pages, third-party widgets, builds with strict X-Frame-Options) refuse to embed; the new-tab fallback handles those cases. The sandbox attributes on the iframe are tracked in AUDIT.md P1.9 (deferred — current set is permissive enough for self-hosted demos but too lax for untrusted third-party content; defense-in-depth already in place via session id scoping).
decisions.recommendation: When AUDIT P1.9 lands, the iframe sandbox tightening affects this page too — keep the new-tab fallback so a strict sandbox doesn't break the share UX.

features.successCriteria:
- A recipient with the URL sees only advancements flagged allowedForPublic for that session
- Other session data (messages, intake form, attachments, internal advancements) never reaches this page
- The scrubber works with keyboard (arrows + space) and supports ?step=N for sharing a specific moment
- Vouches scoped to the session render in parallel with advancements (one round-trip)
- 404 is consistent across 'session doesn't exist' and 'session has no public advancements' (zero-knowledge)
- ShareModal copies a canonical URL to clipboard and offers a new-tab fallback
- Page works for anon visitors with no cookies set
knownLimitations: URL-as-capability means leaking the URL is unrevokable short of soft-deleting the whole session; partial revoke (this advancement only) works by clearing its allowed_for_public flag

Some iframe-hosted builds set X-Frame-Options=DENY and refuse to embed — the new-tab fallback handles them but the in-page scrubber appears broken on those builds until the recipient notices the open-in-new-tab affordance
iframe sandbox is permissive (AUDIT P1.9 deferred); a future tightening could break embeds for builds that depend on same-origin-ish behaviour. Self-hosted preview deploys are the safe baseline
Vouches scoped to the session require the vouch's body to manually reference the session — there's no session_id FK on vouches today; an enhancement could link them automatically
?step=N is 1-indexed for human readability ('Step 3 of 5'); a 0-indexed link from external code would be off-by-one

statusHistory.reason: Page shipped with public-advancements API + time-travel scrubber + vouches block.
Code-first doc co-located. URL-as-capability, 404-consistency, and iframe-with-fallback all stable.

features.title: Écrire un témoignage — open vouch submission form
features.problem: Asking past clients and peers for testimonials by email is high-friction and unreliable — the email gets buried, the testimonial gets dashed off without context, and the back-and-forth to publish takes a week. /vouch is an open form anyone can use without signing in: name, email, relationship, body, optional outbound link. The submission goes into the moderation queue (pre-moderation) so Marc reviews before anything appears publicly. The page exists to lower the friction to near zero — a peer who thought 'I should send Marc a recommendation' can do it in two minutes from any device.
features.analysis: Open form — no auth required, no account creation. The visitor types name + email + relationship (from VOUCH_RELATIONSHIPS: client, past-employer, peer, mentor, friend) + body + optional outbound link, plus a name-display opt-in checkbox (default: first name only). Client-side validation mirrors the server's VOUCH_LIMITS so the visitor sees inline errors before submitting; the server re-validates on POST. On submit, fetches POST /api/vouches (CSRF-exempt because there's no cookie context for an anon submitter — see _middleware.ts's CSRF_EXEMPT_PATHS list). On success, the page swaps to a thank-you state. On 429 (rate-limited), it shows the rate-limit copy. On other 4xx, it maps the server's error string to a friendly localized message. Per-IP rate limit on POST /api/vouches keeps the moderation queue from being flooded; the spec is 5 submissions per IP per 24h, enforced in functions/_lib/ratelimit.ts.

decisions.decision: Open form, no sign-in required
decisions.rationale: A vouch from someone willing to log in is a vouch from someone who already has an account — that's the wrong audience. The people most useful to hear from (past clients, peers, mentors) don't have accounts on this portal. Forcing sign-in would filter them out and leave only the inner-circle audience. Open submission + pre-moderation is the structurally-honest compromise: low friction in, high quality out.
decisions.recommendation: Don't gate /vouch behind anything that's not anti-spam. If volume forces a CAPTCHA, prefer hCaptcha (privacy-friendlier than reCAPTCHA) and document it in /pia.

decisions.decision: POST /api/vouches is CSRF-exempt and IP-rate-limited
decisions.rationale: CSRF protection requires a cookie context to defend against. An anon submitter has no session, so CSRF double-submit can't work — the threat model the gate defends against (a logged-in user being tricked into POSTing) doesn't apply here. The replacement defense is a per-IP rate limit (5/24h) which doesn't depend on a cookie and prevents the realistic bad outcome (queue flood). The exemption is explicit in _middleware.ts's CSRF_EXEMPT_PATHS so it's not accidentally lost.
decisions.recommendation: If the CSRF gate's exemption list ever grows past a handful of entries, audit it — every exempt path is a potential opening. /vouches and the other current exemptions all have a justification documented in CLAUDE.md.

decisions.decision: Default name display to first-name-only, opt-in to full or anonymous
decisions.rationale: First name + relationship tag is enough signal; full name maximizes googleability of the voucher which they may not want. Defaulting to first-only respects Loi 25 minimization and the voucher's likely preference; opt-in upward (to full name) and downward (to anonymous) covers both ends. Anonymous still requires email so the moderator can verify the submitter is real, but the email never reaches the public wire.
decisions.recommendation: Keep the default at first-only. If submission UX feedback ever suggests the default surprises submitters, surface the choice more clearly in the form rather than changing the default.

features.successCriteria:
- An anon visitor can submit a vouch without signing in or creating an account
- Server enforces VOUCH_LIMITS (validation re-runs even if client lies)
- Per-IP rate limit caps submissions at 5/24h; a 429 response shows the rate-limit copy gracefully
- Successful submission shows a thank-you state and clears the form
- Errors are friendly and bilingual; the form preserves user input on error
- ?ref=engagement-slug pre-fills the body with a soft prompt
- Email never appears in the public projection of the vouch row
knownLimitations: No CAPTCHA — the rate limit + manual moderation is the anti-spam layer. If automated submissions ever leak past the rate limit, adding hCaptcha is the next escalation
Email is collected for moderator verification but isn't itself verified (no double opt-in) — a determined spammer could use throwaway emails, the moderation step catches the output regardless
?ref pre-fill is soft (just text in the body) — a future enhancement could link the vouch to the session_id of the referenced engagement, but the schema would need work
VOUCH_LIMITS is duplicated client-side and server-side; they could drift if updated in one place. The server enforces the truth, the client is UX
Per-IP rate limit reads X-Forwarded-For headers from Cloudflare; a misconfigured proxy upstream could mis-attribute requests — the production deploy is direct CF Pages, so this is theoretical

statusHistory.reason: Open form + rate-limited POST + moderation pipeline all live; first real-world submissions flowed.
Code-first doc co-located. Behaviour is stable; CSRF-exemption is justified and documented.

features.title: Témoignages — public vouches wall, post-moderation
features.problem: A consulting site without testimonials is a structural liability — buyers assume there's nothing to show. But anonymous reviews on a self-hosted page also fail the trust test (anyone could write them). /vouches threads the needle: anyone can submit a vouch via the open /vouch form (no auth required), but Marc moderates every submission before it appears publicly. Approved vouches are rendered as cards on /vouches with the author's first name, relationship tag (client, peer, mentor, etc.), and body — but never the author's email. The wall is small by intent — a real practice has a handful, not hundreds.
features.analysis: Reads GET /api/public/vouches (functions/api/public/vouches.ts) which queries the vouches D1 table filtered to status='approved' AND deleted_at IS NULL, projected through PublicVouchRow which strips author_email. The page renders the array as a flat card list — no infinite scroll, no pagination (the moderation throttle keeps the count bounded). Each card shows the author's display name (first name only, opt-in for full name), the relationship tag (client / past-employer / peer / mentor / friend), the body, and an optional link they provided (LinkedIn, project URL, etc.). The vouch submission flow is /vouch (a separate page); approved-vouch lifecycle: submitted (status='pending') → moderated via /admin/vouches → approved or deleted. The list is fetched on mount; an error state shows a quiet 'unavailable' message and a /vouch CTA so the visitor can still contribute. Vouches are scoped at the tenant level; tenant resolution happens in _middleware.ts before this handler runs.

decisions.decision: Pre-moderation (admin approves before public), not post-moderation (public-then-takedown)
decisions.rationale: Post-moderation means a moment exists where a malicious or AI-generated vouch is publicly visible — and that moment is also when SEO and screenshot caches catch it. Pre-moderation costs Marc time (it's a manual review per submission) but eliminates the bad-vouch-in-the-wild scenario entirely. At the practice's scale, manual review is tractable; if volume ever forced post-moderation, an automation step (e.g. require email verification + manual approve only for borderline cases) would be safer than 'public until removed'.
decisions.recommendation: Keep pre-moderation. If volume grows, automate the obvious-spam filter, but don't flip the default to public-by-default.

decisions.decision: Strip author_email at the SQL projection, not in the handler
decisions.rationale: Defense in depth: a future handler refactor that accidentally returns the full row should still not leak email because the projection PublicVouchRow in functions/_lib/vouches.ts doesn't include the column. Doing it at the projection means the client-bound type literally doesn't have the field; TypeScript catches anyone reaching for it.
decisions.recommendation: When adding a new field that should be public, add it to PublicVouchRow explicitly. When adding a private field (e.g. moderator notes), add it to the schema but NOT to PublicVouchRow.

decisions.decision: Display first name only by default, full name opt-in
decisions.rationale: A vouch's signal is the substance + the relationship tag, not the surname. Letting submitters opt into full name keeps the page friendly for vouchers who'd rather not be googleable by their endorsement. Quebec's Loi 25 also leans toward data minimization — collecting full name when first name suffices wouldn't pass a PIA review.
decisions.recommendation: If a vouch comes in from a public figure who specifically WANTS the full name displayed, the submission form already supports an opt-in; don't hand-edit the DB row, route them through the form.

features.successCriteria:
- Only approved + not-deleted vouches appear on /vouches
- author_email never reaches the wire (projection-level strip)
- The page renders gracefully even when zero vouches are approved (CTA to /vouch)
- A submitter who declined name display shows as 'Anonyme'; opt-in to full name works
- Relationship tags display consistently with their colour tokens
- /vouches loads on mount with a single fetch — no waterfall, no pagination
- The wall reads as small-and-real, not gamed
knownLimitations: No pagination — if the moderation queue ever lets ~50+ vouches through, the page becomes a long scroll. The throttle is intentional and the count is bounded; revisit if/when scale changes
The relationship tag colour tokens are hardcoded in the cssVars helper; a new relationship category requires a new colour token

Vouches do NOT auto-link to the specific engagement they reference — the body sometimes does so manually. A future enhancement could link by session_id, but it'd require schema work and may not be worth the cost
An approved vouch can be edited after publication (via admin), but the edit isn't versioned — the audit log captures who edited, not what changed

GET /api/public/vouches is unauthenticated; tenant resolution comes from _middleware.ts. A malformed Host that resolves to no tenant returns 404 (not an empty list) so the page renders the error state cleanly

statusHistory.reason: Page shipped with the public vouches list backed by GET /api/public/vouches; moderation flow live via /admin/vouches.
Code-first doc co-located. Projection strip, pre-moderation, and opt-in name display all stable.

features.title: GET /api/admin/audit — recent audit_log feed (operator only)
features.problem: AdminAudit needs a feed of recent audit_log entries; this endpoint serves that. Operator-only, returns the most recent entries ordered ts DESC. Tenant-scoped via requireTenant — audit_log is per-tenant so a fleet admin doesn't see another tenant's actions.
features.analysis: GET only. currentEmail → unauthorized if missing. isAdmin → forbidden if not operator. requireTenant → ctx.data.tenant or 500. SELECT FROM audit_log WHERE tenant_id = ? ORDER BY ts DESC LIMIT N (N defined at top of file, currently 200). Returns array of AuditRow. No filter params today; AdminAudit does the filtering client-side.

decisions.decision: Server-side LIMIT, no pagination
decisions.rationale: audit_log is small. Returning the most-recent 200 covers every realistic operator query. A LIMIT-without-pagination is simpler than a paginated API; AdminAudit's client-side filtering handles search.
decisions.recommendation: When the log crosses ~1000 entries per tenant, add a /api/admin/audit?cursor=... param. Don't over-engineer earlier.

decisions.decision: Tenant-scoped at the WHERE clause
decisions.rationale: audit_log entries are per-tenant. Filtering at the SQL level (not in app code) means even a misconfigured admin can't accidentally see another tenant's log. requireTenant guarantees tenant_id is non-null.
decisions.recommendation: When adding new admin endpoints that touch audit_log, thread tenant the same way.

features.successCriteria:
- Operator gets recent audit_log (tenant-scoped)
- Non-operator → 403
- Anon → 401
- Tenant lookup failure → 500 (requireTenant)
- Newest-first ordering
- Server-side LIMIT prevents giant responses
knownLimitations: No pagination; LIMIT is fixed server-side
No date range / actor filter at the API; client filters
Payload column is opaque JSON; structured search would require JSON1 or a dedicated index
Insertion into audit_log is best-effort from emitting handlers; a silent miss has no detection

statusHistory.reason: Endpoint shipped paired with AdminAudit page.
Code-first doc co-located. Tenant scoping + LIMIT pattern stable.

features.title: POST /api/admin/test-emails — operator deliverability + voice check
features.problem: The portal sends ~12 distinct email types (magic link, status changes, message notifications, payment confirmations, digest, etc.). Each has its own template, voice, lang variant. Without a fire-everything diagnostic, the operator only sees an email type by actually walking the matching user flow — slow, hard to QA changes, easy to miss a regression. /api/admin/test-emails fires one of every type at the signed-in admin's address with synthetic sample data. Zero production rows read or touched.
features.analysis: POST only. Operator gate (currentEmail + isAdmin). CSRF enforced by middleware. Iterates a defined list of email types, calls each one with synthetic sample data hardcoded in the handler. Pacing: ~600ms sleep between sends to stay under Resend's 2 req/s free-tier rate. Total: 12 sends × 600ms = ~7s, well under Pages Functions' 30s wall-time budget. Returns 200 { sent: [...types] } on success.

decisions.decision: Sequential pacing instead of Promise.all, stay under Resend's 2 req/s
decisions.rationale: Resend's free tier rate-limits at ~2 req/s. Promise.all would burst all 12 at once and hit the limit, getting 429s on the latter half. Sequential with ~600ms gap pacing keeps every send under the rate limit + gets all 12 in under 10 seconds.
decisions.recommendation: Don't refactor to parallel. If Resend's rate ever increases, evaluate; until then, sequential is correct.

decisions.decision: Synthetic sample data, never read prod rows
decisions.rationale: Reading real visitor data to test an email template would leak that data into the test email — even to the operator, that's a privacy footnote. Hardcoded synthetic data ('Test Visitor', 'sample@example.com', a fixture intake JSON) makes the test deterministic + privacy-safe.
decisions.recommendation: Don't make the sample data dynamic. If a future need wants 'render this email type for THIS real session', that's a different endpoint with different gating.

features.successCriteria:
- Operator gets ~12 emails (one per type) at their address
- Each email uses synthetic data; no prod rows read
- Sequential pacing stays under Resend rate limit
- Total send completes well under 30s wall-time
- 200 + sent list on success
knownLimitations: Sends to the signed-in admin's address only — can't target another address (avoids accidental spam vector)
Synthetic data is hardcoded; a new email type requires updating both EMAIL_TYPES and possibly the sample fixture
If Resend hits a temporary outage during the send loop, latter emails fail — handler returns 200 with the partial sent list (best-effort)
No dry-run mode; sending IS the test

statusHistory.reason: Endpoint shipped with all 12 email types + rate-paced sequential sends.
Code-first doc co-located. Synthetic-data + pacing decisions stable.

features.title: PATCH + DELETE /api/admin/vouches/:id — moderation actions
features.problem: AdminVouches' per-row actions go through here. PATCH supports status flips (pending → approved / rejected), copy edits (body, name, link, relationship), and undelete (restore from trash). DELETE soft-removes (sets deleted_at). Author email and submission timestamp are immutable — those are dedup + audit keys.
features.analysis: PATCH onRequest. currentEmail + isAdmin. Body fields all optional partial-update; if `status` provided, validate against VOUCH_STATUSES enum; if `relationship`, validate via isValidRelationship. Body / name / link length-checked against the same VOUCH_LIMITS used by the open /vouches submission. `undelete: true` clears deleted_at + sets status back to 'pending' (consistent with sessions' trash-restore pattern). DELETE onRequest: soft-delete only (sets deleted_at = now); hard-delete is SQL-only.

decisions.decision: undelete returns to status='pending', not original status
decisions.rationale: Same logic as session restore: a soft-deleted vouch might have been deleted as spam, then later it turns out to be legitimate. Restoring to 'pending' forces a fresh moderation decision rather than auto-publishing potentially-spam content.
decisions.recommendation: Keep the pending-restore. Trusting the original status would auto-publish spam on a restore.

decisions.decision: Author email + timestamp immutable
decisions.rationale: Email is the rate-limit dedup key and the moderator's follow-up channel. Timestamp is the audit anchor. Both unchangeable maintains the integrity of those uses.
decisions.recommendation: Don't add edit. If a wrong email needs fixing, reject + ask the submitter to resubmit.

features.successCriteria:
- PATCH supports status / body / name / link / relationship / undelete in partial shape
- DELETE soft-removes (sets deleted_at)
- Validation rejects bad statuses / relationships / overlength bodies
- Email + timestamp can't be changed
- 403 for non-operator, 401 for anon, 404 for unknown id
knownLimitations: No edit history — overwrite is permanent for the affected fields
Hard-delete only via direct SQL; the soft-delete pattern preserves the row for recovery
Undelete → pending means re-moderation is required; an admin wanting to restore exact prior state needs two PATCHes
No bulk PATCH — one row per call

statusHistory.reason: Endpoint shipped paired with the index handler + AdminVouches page.
Code-first doc co-located. Edit + undelete-to-pending decisions stable.

features.title: GET /api/admin/vouches — moderation list including pending + trash
features.problem: /api/public/vouches returns only approved + not-deleted vouches with email stripped. The admin moderation page needs the OTHER half: pending + rejected + soft-deleted, WITH email attached for follow-up. /api/admin/vouches is that. Sorted pending-first (newest) then everything else by created_at DESC — puts the moderation queue at the top of the response so the UI doesn't have to bucket the wrong way.
features.analysis: GET only. currentEmail + isAdmin gates. Optional ?status=pending|approved|rejected narrows. Otherwise returns every vouch (including soft-deleted). Full VouchRow shape — email, IP, rate-limit metadata, all of it. Sort: pending first by created_at DESC, then status='approved'/'rejected' by created_at DESC, then soft-deleted last. AdminVouches client-side buckets by status.

decisions.decision: Sort puts pending at top of response, not status-then-time
decisions.rationale: The moderation queue is what admin opens this page to handle. Returning everything sorted by created_at puts ancient approved rows at the top, burying the queue. Pending-first ordering shaves a client-side sort step and makes the API itself reflect the operator's priority.
decisions.recommendation: Don't reorder. If a future admin view wants different ordering, add a ?sort= param; keep this default.

decisions.decision: ?status= filter is OPTIONAL, default is everything
decisions.rationale: AdminVouches renders all four buckets at once — needing everything is the common case. ?status= is for occasional needs (e.g. 'just show me trash' for cleanup). Default-everything matches the page; filter is the special case.
decisions.recommendation: Keep optional. If a use case needs default-narrowed (e.g. mobile UI shows only pending), thread that through the client.

features.successCriteria:
- Operator gets every vouch including pending + rejected + soft-deleted
- Author email + IP + rate-limit metadata included (operator context)
- ?status= narrows when present
- Pending-first ordering with newest-first within
- 403 for non-operator, 401 for anon
knownLimitations: Returns every vouch — no pagination. Fine at current scale; revisit at ~500+ rows
No date range filter at the API; admin's client-side filter handles narrowing
Sort is server-side; can't be changed via query param today

statusHistory.reason: Endpoint shipped with the moderation-queue ordering.
Code-first doc co-located. Pending-first sort + optional filter stable.

features.title: POST /api/auth/logout — clear the session cookie
features.problem: The sign-out side of the auth flow. mp_session and mp_csrf cookies are cleared via Set-Cookie max-age=0; the response carries both clears + a JSON ok. Stateless because the cookie IS the session — there's no server-side session table to revoke. CSRF-exempt because a forced logout is a nuisance, not an account compromise, and gating it would create chicken-and-egg problems if the SPA's mp_csrf has drifted.
features.analysis: Single POST handler that writes Set-Cookie headers clearing mp_session and mp_csrf (max-age=0, same path/domain attributes as the original). Returns 200 { ok: true }. No D1 reads, no D1 writes — there's nothing to revoke server-side. The session is the cookie; clearing the cookie ends the session. CSRF is intentionally exempt — see the in-handler comment for why (forced logout is benign, gating it creates failure modes when CSRF cookie has drifted). Sentry doesn't wrap this handler specifically; the middleware does for any uncaught exceptions.

decisions.decision: Stateless logout — no session-table revocation
decisions.rationale: Server-side session tables exist for systems where you need 'log out everywhere' or 'revoke this device' as features. This portal's sessions are short-lived (7 days), low-stakes, and the user can already invalidate them by clearing browser cookies. Adding a session table for the sake of API symmetry would add a table to maintain, a revocation list to query on every authenticated request, and zero real-world security benefit at this scale.
decisions.recommendation: Don't add a session table. If 'log out from all devices' ever becomes a real ask, the right answer is to rotate SESSION_SECRET (which invalidates all existing signed cookies in one operation, server-side, instantly).

decisions.decision: CSRF-exempt — forced logout is allowed
decisions.rationale: The CSRF gate exists to prevent a logged-in user from being tricked into making a state-changing request. Forced logout via cross-site POST is a nuisance (the user has to re-sign-in) but doesn't compromise their account. Gating /api/auth/logout would create a real failure mode: a user whose mp_csrf has drifted (cookie expired but mp_session valid) cannot sign out — they're stuck. The exemption is documented in _middleware.ts CSRF_EXEMPT_PATHS.
decisions.recommendation: Keep the exemption. If a future review wants to gate it, the better fix is to surface 'your CSRF is stale, get a new one' as an explicit re-mint endpoint — not to require it for logout.

features.successCriteria:
- A POST clears mp_session and mp_csrf via Set-Cookie max-age=0
- Response is 200 { ok: true } with no body data leaks
- No D1 reads or writes
- CSRF-exempt status is preserved in CSRF_EXEMPT_PATHS
- After this call, the next authenticated request to /api/me returns { email: null }
knownLimitations: No 'log out from all devices' — clearing the cookie only ends THIS browser's session; another device with a valid mp_session keeps working. Acceptable for this scale; SESSION_SECRET rotation is the nuclear option
Stateless means we have no record of 'when did this user last sign out' — useful for audit logs but absent today

If a session has been hijacked, clearing the cookie on the victim's browser doesn't invalidate the attacker's copy of the cookie — the attacker keeps their session until expiry. Mitigation: rotate SESSION_SECRET

statusHistory.reason: Endpoint shipped with both cookie clears + 200 response.
Code-first doc co-located. Stateless model and CSRF exemption are stable.

features.title: POST /api/auth/request-link — issue and email a magic-link token
features.problem: The server side of the magic-link sign-in flow. Receives an email, issues a single-use random token, sends it via Resend in a URL, and returns 200 uniformly — including for unknown emails — so the endpoint can't be used to enumerate registered addresses. Token storage is hashed (SHA-256) so a DB snapshot or admin SELECT doesn't leak usable tokens.
features.analysis: Two rate limits in parallel: per-email (5 requests / hour) catches the targeted-flood case, per-IP (20 / hour) catches the rotating-email-same-IP attacker. Both hit before any D1 write, so a quota exhausted by an attacker doesn't pollute the magic_link_tokens table. Token generation: randomTokenB64url() produces a 24-byte URL-safe random string; sha256B64url() hashes it. Only the hash lives in D1; the plaintext goes ONCE in the email URL. TTL = 30 minutes. sendMagicLink() (functions/_lib/email.ts) calls Resend with the link URL; if Resend's API returns non-200 the function returns 200 anyway (no enumeration of which emails the system failed to deliver to). setLangIfAbsent writes mp_lang cookie if the request body included a lang preference. CSRF-exempt because the visitor doesn't yet have an mp_session — there's no cookie context to defend, so per-IP rate limit + uniform-200 are the threat model.

decisions.decision: Store the SHA-256 hash of the token, not the plaintext
decisions.rationale: A DB snapshot or careless admin SELECT shouldn't leak usable tokens. Hashing means even a full read of magic_link_tokens gives an attacker nothing actionable — the plaintext only exists in the email URL we sent. Verification flow hashes the URL token and looks up by hash. Cost: one extra SHA-256 per verify (negligible).
decisions.recommendation: If a future need wants to display 'your active sign-in links' to a user (unlikely), the architecture stays: surface the hash + the email + expiry; never resurface the plaintext.

decisions.decision: Two-axis rate limit: per-email AND per-IP, with stricter IP cap
decisions.rationale: Per-email alone lets an attacker rotate emails from one IP and drain Resend's free tier (100/day). Per-IP alone lets a distributed attack from a botnet flood one inbox. Both running in parallel covers both directions. Per-IP is 4x the per-email cap (20 vs 5) because a household (one NAT IP, multiple humans) can legitimately request several links.
decisions.recommendation: If Resend quota tightens or pricing changes, the IP cap is the right lever — start there before lowering the per-email limit which would also annoy legitimate users.

decisions.decision: Uniform 200 response regardless of email validity or delivery success
decisions.rationale: A 404 for unknown email is enumeration-as-a-service. A 503 for Resend hiccup is enumeration-as-an-error-channel. Returning 200 always means the response carries no signal about whether the email is registered or whether delivery worked — the user finds out by checking their inbox (or by RUNBOOK triage if they reach out). The cost: a user who mistyped their email waits for an email that never arrives.
decisions.recommendation: Don't add a 'we couldn't reach that email' response code. If observability is needed, log the failure server-side and surface it in /admin/digest — don't leak it to the requester.

features.successCriteria:
- Valid emails receive a magic link via Resend within ~60 seconds
- Unknown / mistyped emails receive a uniform 200; no enumeration signal in the response
- Token plaintext appears only in the email URL — D1 stores only the SHA-256 hash
- Per-email and per-IP rate limits are both enforced; either trips returns 200 (still no enumeration)
- TTL of 30 minutes is enforced; expired tokens cannot be consumed
- If a lang preference is supplied in the body, mp_lang cookie is set before response
- CSRF gate is correctly exempt (verified in functions/_middleware.test.ts)
knownLimitations: Resend free tier is 100 emails/day; the per-IP rate limit (20/h) was tuned to keep this safe but a distributed attack from many IPs could still exhaust the quota. Mitigation would be Cloudflare Turnstile (CAPTCHA) on /login submit; not done today

If Resend is down, requests return 200 with no email sent — the user has no idea their link didn't arrive. The /admin/digest synthetic monitor catches this within a day; in the meantime, the visitor's confusion is real

Token plaintext is in the URL Resend sees during delivery — Resend's logs theoretically contain valid tokens. Mitigation is the short TTL (30 min) and Resend's SOC2 posture documented in the PIA at /pia

Per-email rate limit means a user who genuinely needs five+ links in an hour (e.g. forgot which inbox they used and is retrying) gets blocked. Telling them to wait an hour is acceptable; the alternative is an enumeration channel
isPlausibleEmail does syntactic validation only; addresses with valid syntax but no MX record will get a 'we sent it' response and silently never deliver

statusHistory.reason: Endpoint shipped with token hashing + per-email + per-IP rate limits + Resend integration.
