Code-first doc co-located. The flow has been stable since launch; AUDIT P1.1 (custom sender domain) is the only outstanding work and tracked separately.

features.title: GET /api/auth/verify — consume a magic-link token, set session cookie
features.problem: The other half of the magic-link flow. The verification URL the user clicks lands here with ?token=<plaintext>; this handler hashes it, looks up the matching row in magic_link_tokens, validates expiry + single-use, sets the mp_session + mp_csrf cookies, then redirects into the SPA. Failure modes (missing / unknown / used / expired token) redirect back to /login with a ?reason=... so the SPA can render a friendly explainer.
features.analysis: GET handler taking ?token. sha256B64url(token) → hash lookup against magic_link_tokens. Validation order: missing token → ?reason=missing-token; row not found → ?reason=unknown-token; row.used_at != null → ?reason=token-used; row.expires_at < now → ?reason=token-expired. On success: mark used_at, sign an mp_session cookie via signSessionCookie() (HMAC-SHA-256 over { email, exp }, see functions/_lib/auth.ts), mint a fresh CSRF token via newCsrfToken() and set both via Set-Cookie headers. Detects admin via isAdmin(env, email) (controlled by ADMIN_EMAILS env var); admin gets the same cookies, the admin gate is per-handler. Reads mp_lang preference (getLang from userPrefs.ts) and sets the cookie on the response too so the SPA inherits the user's saved language. 303 redirect to /me (or /en/me) based on lang. The 30-min TTL on the magic link is enforced here, not on the request side — a token issued just before TTL drift still works if used within window.

decisions.decision: Redirect to /login?reason=... on failure modes, not a generic error page
decisions.rationale: A user clicking an expired link gets a worse experience if they see a sorry-page than if they get a tailored message right above the form they need to use anyway. Four reason codes (missing / unknown / used / expired) map to the four real failure modes; the /login page (src/pages/Login.tsx) renders specific copy for each. Recovery is one form re-submit away.
decisions.recommendation: Don't add new reason codes for new failure modes — if a new mode emerges, it usually points at a config or rate-limit issue that should be fixed at the source.

decisions.decision: Set both mp_session AND mp_csrf cookies on verify
decisions.rationale: mp_csrf is the double-submit token the SPA echoes as X-CSRF-Token on state-changing requests. Issuing it at sign-in means the SPA's first authenticated request already has it — no extra round trip to mint one later. Both cookies are sibling-scoped to the same path/domain; mp_session is HttpOnly (server-only), mp_csrf is NOT (the SPA reads it via document.cookie and echoes it).
decisions.recommendation: Don't make mp_csrf HttpOnly — the SPA must read it to echo. The 'NOT HttpOnly by design' is documented in src/lib/api.ts. If a future security review flags this, point at the CLAUDE.md auth section explaining the double-submit pattern.

decisions.decision: 303 redirect (not 302) on success
decisions.rationale: 303 'See Other' is the semantically correct redirect for a POST-PRG-pattern equivalent; even though this is GET, using 303 ensures the browser follows with a GET request to /me regardless of the original method semantics. 302 may be silently rewritten to a POST by older clients in edge cases. 303 is unambiguous.
decisions.recommendation: Keep 303. Don't switch to 307 (which preserves the original method); that would re-POST the request body if the original wasn't a GET, which is not the intent here.

features.successCriteria:
- A valid, unused, non-expired token consumes successfully and lands the user on /me signed in
- Each of the four failure modes redirects to /login with the right ?reason code
- Single-use: a verified token can't be replayed (used_at marks consumed)
- mp_session is HttpOnly + signed; mp_csrf is set but readable to the SPA
- Admin status is detected here (isAdmin via ADMIN_EMAILS env)
- mp_lang cookie is written if a user pref exists
- The 30-min TTL is enforced
knownLimitations: Tokens are tied to email at issue time — a user can't 'transfer' a magic link to another email; new email = new link
The 30-min TTL is hardcoded both client-side ('expires in 30 minutes' copy on /login/sent) and server-side; changing it requires editing both places
Verify doesn't rate-limit — a brute-force attack against the hash space is impractical (2^192 token space) but a future tightening could add a per-IP cap on /api/auth/verify too if abuse emerges
Admin detection re-runs isAdmin every verify; that's a string-set lookup on ADMIN_EMAILS env var which is cheap. A future tenant-scoped admin model would need to plumb tenant into isAdmin

statusHistory.reason: Endpoint shipped with all four failure-mode reason codes and cookie issuance.
Code-first doc co-located. Verification flow has been stable since launch.

features.title: GET /api/health — uptime + D1 connectivity probe
features.problem: Synthetic monitoring (Marc's curl, UptimeRobot, etc.) needs a cheap reachable endpoint that confirms the runtime is up AND D1 is responsive. /api/health does exactly that: runs SELECT 1 against D1, returns ok+200 on success, fail+500 on D1 exception. Public, unauthenticated, no tenant lookup — caller can be anyone.
features.analysis: GET only. Bypasses tenant resolution conceptually (handler doesn't read ctx.data.tenant). Executes env.DB.prepare('SELECT 1').first() — D1's smallest valid roundtrip. On success: 200 { ok: true, db: 'ok', ts }. On exception: 500 { ok: false, db: 'fail', ts }. No commit hash today (would require build-time inject; relies on the SPA's static stamp at /meta for 'what's deployed').

decisions.decision: Run SELECT 1 against D1, not just confirm the worker is up
decisions.rationale: Worker-up-but-DB-down is the realistic partial failure mode. A no-D1 health check would return 200 during a D1 outage and not alert. SELECT 1 is the cheapest possible probe that catches the real failure modes.
decisions.recommendation: Don't add more checks (R2, Stripe, Resend) here — each is a graceful-degrade dependency, not a hard-down trigger. If R2 is down, the worker still serves; the synthetic monitor should treat R2-down differently from worker-down.

decisions.decision: 500 on D1 failure, NOT 200 with fail flag
decisions.rationale: Synthetic monitors (UptimeRobot, etc.) treat any non-200 as down. Returning 200 even when D1 is broken would silently disable monitoring. 500 is the truthful signal.
decisions.recommendation: Keep the 500. The JSON body still has the structured 'db: fail' so a custom monitor can read details, but the status code is the load-bearing signal.

knownLimitations: No commit hash in the response today — would require a build-time define plumbed through the Functions bundle; SPA's static commit stamp at /meta is the workaround
Doesn't probe R2 / Workers AI / Stripe / Resend; those are graceful-degrade dependencies and a unified health would lie about partial failures
Timestamp resolution is millisecond; clock skew between worker instances is bounded but theoretically the ts could be slightly stale

statusHistory.reason: Endpoint shipped with the D1 probe + truthful status code.
Code-first doc co-located. Probe minimalism is stable.

features.title: POST /api/intake/transcribe — Whisper edge transcription, transcribe-and-discard
features.problem: The intake form lets a visitor record a short voice note instead of typing — useful for tradespeople more comfortable speaking than writing. The recording needs to be transcribed; doing it client-side would either require a heavy model bundle or third-party API. Cloudflare Workers AI ships a Whisper binding that runs at the edge — perfect fit. This endpoint accepts a multipart audio upload, transcribes via Workers AI, returns the text. The audio is immediately discarded — nothing is written to R2 or D1. The smallest possible Loi 25 footprint: no stored recording, no retention question.
features.analysis: POST with multipart/form-data, audio file under field name 'file'. The handler is open (no auth) because the intake form is open. CSRF-exempt for the same reason as /api/vouches — anon visitor has no cookie context. Per-IP rate limit (functions/_lib/ratelimit.ts) caps abuse. Validation: content-type must be audio/*; file size capped at MAX_BYTES (a few MB; precise cap in handler). env.AI binding is the Workers AI gateway; if unset (graceful-degrade), returns 503. Whisper inference: env.AI.run('@cf/openai/whisper', { audio: bytes }) returns { text }. The bytes are released as soon as Whisper returns; nothing stored. Returns 200 { transcript: text } — the SPA folds the transcript into the intake form's body field. Future submissions of the intake form carry the transcript text; the audio bytes are gone forever.

decisions.decision: Transcribe and discard — no R2 write, no D1 write
decisions.rationale: Storing the recording would create a retention question (how long? where? with what consent?) that the intake form's open-to-anon nature can't satisfy. Discarding immediately means the only persistent artifact is the transcript text the visitor sees and chooses to submit. Loi 25 footprint shrinks to zero for the audio half.
decisions.recommendation: Don't add an R2 write 'just in case the transcription is wrong'. If a future feature wants 'play the original audio back', architect it differently (e.g. opt-in store after sign-up).

decisions.decision: Open to anon (no auth), CSRF-exempt, IP-rate-limited
decisions.rationale: The intake form must work for anon visitors — that's the funnel-entry surface. Requiring sign-in before transcription would break the funnel; the visitor hasn't decided to sign up yet. CSRF-exempt because there's no cookie to defend. IP rate limit is the abuse defense. Same threat model as /api/vouches and /api/auth/request-link.
decisions.recommendation: Keep open. If abuse emerges, tighten the rate limit or add Cloudflare Turnstile to the intake form; don't gate behind auth.

decisions.decision: 503 with a clear message if env.AI binding unset
decisions.rationale: Workers AI is optional in dev environments — a dev without it set should still see the rest of the intake form work; only the voice transcription is unavailable. 503 'transcription unavailable' is the truthful response; the SPA hides the voice-record button when this happens. Graceful-degrade pattern, consistent with the rest of the codebase.
decisions.recommendation: When adding any new Workers AI-backed feature, follow the same env-check-then-503 pattern.

features.successCriteria:
- An anon visitor can upload audio and receive a transcript without sign-in
- Audio bytes are not persisted (R2, D1, or otherwise)
- env.AI unset → 503 with a clear message; the rest of intake still works
- Per-IP rate limit caps abuse
- Non-audio content-type → 415
- Oversized file → 413
- Transcript is returned as plain JSON { transcript: string }
- CSRF-exempt status is preserved in _middleware.ts CSRF_EXEMPT_PATHS
knownLimitations: Whisper accuracy is mode-dependent — accented French, low-quality mics, background noise all degrade quality. The visitor can edit the transcript before submitting, mitigating this
No language hint passed to Whisper today — Whisper auto-detects but accuracy improves with explicit language code; a future enhancement could thread language from the form state
MAX_BYTES is per-request; chunked uploads (long recordings) would need a different shape. Current intake recordings are short (<60s), well within the cap
Rate limit is per-IP; a visitor doing many re-record attempts could hit it. Tunable via the limit constants
Workers AI billing is per-call; abuse via rate-limit bypass would cost. Per-IP cap is the defense

statusHistory.reason: Endpoint shipped with Whisper transcription + transcribe-and-discard.
Code-first doc co-located. Pattern is stable; the no-storage Loi 25 posture continues to hold.

features.title: GET + POST + DELETE /api/intake-drafts — cross-device intake autosave
features.problem: The intake form autosaves to localStorage every keystroke so a refresh doesn't lose work. But localStorage is per-device — a visitor typing on their phone, then switching to desktop for the magic-link sign-in, would lose everything. This endpoint is the cross-device fallback: every autosave also POSTs the draft to D1, keyed by the visitor's email; sign-in pulls from D1, restores the draft. The draft is purged on successful session creation.
features.analysis: Three methods. GET returns the draft for the signed-in email (or null). POST upserts { payload } — the draft JSON the visitor's typed so far. DELETE drops the draft (called after a successful createSession). All three require sign-in: the auth cookie pins which email you can read/write — an attacker can't read another email's draft. Ungated POST would also be a write amplifier for the rate-limit table on /api/auth/request-link, so requiring auth is two-fold defense. Payload size cap at PAYLOAD_LIMIT_BYTES (a few KB; the intake form is text-heavy but not enormous). Draft is keyed by lower-cased email so 'X@Y.com' and 'x@y.com' share one draft (matches the magic-link table's casing).

decisions.decision: Auth-required, even though intake itself is open-to-anon
decisions.rationale: Anon writes would let any visitor write to any email's draft. That's both an abuse vector (overwrite legitimate drafts) and a write-amplifier on the rate-limit table. Requiring auth means a draft is only writable by the signed-in user. The trade-off: the cross-device benefit only kicks in AFTER the magic-link sign-in completes. Pre-signin, the visitor is on local-storage-only. That's the right model for the current flow: type intake on phone → magic-link to desktop email → sign in on desktop → pull from D1 to continue.
decisions.recommendation: Don't add an anon write path. If a future flow needs anon-cross-device (unlikely), it'd need its own threat-modeled mechanism (e.g. one-time draft tokens).

decisions.decision: Email key is lower-cased; matches magic-link table casing
decisions.rationale: Email casing inconsistency is a classic bug source — 'X@Y.com' magic-link sent, then 'x@y.com' lookup in a different table — silent miss. Normalizing to lower at the key level removes the class of bugs. Magic-link, drafts, prefs, sessions all consistently lower-case.
decisions.recommendation: When adding any new email-keyed table, lower-case at the SQL boundary. AUDIT P2.1 is a future centralization that would put this in one helper.

features.successCriteria:
- A signed-in visitor can GET / POST / DELETE their own draft
- A signed-in visitor cannot read another email's draft (auth pins the email)
- Payload size cap rejects oversized writes with 413
- DELETE called after createSession() succeeds, so the draft doesn't linger
- Drafts are keyed by lower-cased email for cross-table consistency
knownLimitations: Only one draft per email at a time — if the visitor wants to keep two intake-in-progress projects, the second overwrites the first. Acceptable since intake is intended as a single conversion event
No history of past drafts (e.g. 'recover this draft I had 3 days ago') — overwrite semantics. The /me dashboard has the session list, not draft history
Payload is opaque JSON; size cap is byte-level, not per-field. A malicious payload of legal size could be oversized in semantics; the form server-side validation catches it on createSession
Drafts auto-stale: there's no TTL today. A neglected draft sits forever. A future janitor cron could prune (e.g. >30 days untouched)

statusHistory.reason: Endpoint shipped with the cross-device draft persistence; intake form integrated.
Code-first doc co-located. Auth-gating and email-casing patterns stable.

features.title: GET + PATCH /api/me/prefs — per-account language and display name
features.problem: Two per-account preferences: lang ('fr' | 'en') and firstName (display name, used in greetings + signature lines). One endpoint, two methods. GET reads, PATCH writes. The lang preference is also the source of truth for the mp_lang cookie that drives the bare-'/' redirect in middleware — so a PATCH that changes lang also writes the cookie so the cookie-only middleware path stays in sync with the DB. Marc's own preference is what every admin-side notification email renders in.
features.analysis: GET handler: returns { lang, firstName } for the signed-in account, defaulting to { lang: 'fr', firstName: null } if no row exists. PATCH handler: requires CSRF (state-changing). Body may include lang and/or firstName; at least one must be present (otherwise badRequest). Lang validation: must be 'fr' or 'en'. firstName: trimmed, max length, may be null to clear. Writes to user_prefs table (UPSERT on email). Side effect on lang change: writes the mp_lang cookie via Set-Cookie so the next bare-'/' middleware redirect sees the new language without needing a DB read on the hot path. The cookie write is one-way (DB → cookie); a cookie change without DB write doesn't persist across browsers but is fine for the bare-'/' flow.

decisions.decision: GET defaults gracefully if no row exists
decisions.rationale: A freshly-magic-linked user hasn't written any prefs yet — their user_prefs row doesn't exist. GET could either return 404 or default to { lang: 'fr', firstName: null }. Defaulting is friendlier: the SPA gets a usable JSON body it can render; 404 would require special-case handling. The defaults match the bare-'/' middleware's default behavior (FR canonical).
decisions.recommendation: Keep defaulting in GET. If a user_prefs row is needed for some other feature (e.g. seed it at sign-up), do it at request-link time, not at the GET prefs side.

decisions.decision: PATCH writes both DB and cookie on lang change
decisions.rationale: The middleware's bare-'/' redirect reads only the mp_lang cookie (no D1 hit on the hot path). If PATCH wrote only the DB, the cookie would drift — the user changes their pref to EN but the next time they hit bare '/' they're redirected to '/' (FR) until cookies expire. Writing both keeps the two sources of truth in sync at PATCH time. The cookie is duplicate data but it's a tiny payload (a few bytes) so the cost is negligible.
decisions.recommendation: Keep the dual write. If a future feature reads lang from somewhere else (e.g. an HTTP header), thread that source the same way — every authoritative pref source should be updated by PATCH /api/me/prefs.

decisions.decision: One endpoint, two methods (not /api/me/lang + /api/me/name)
decisions.rationale: Two prefs today, more later. Splitting per-pref means N endpoints to add + maintain. Unified prefs endpoint handles whatever shape the body has; client can update one or both in a single request. The shape is simple (one field per pref) so collision/ambiguity isn't a concern.
decisions.recommendation: When adding a new pref (e.g. timezone, notification opt-out), extend the PATCH body shape. Don't add /api/me/<pref>. Document the new field in this feature.json's analysis.

features.successCriteria:
- GET returns { lang, firstName } for the signed-in account; defaults if no row
- PATCH requires CSRF (verified via middleware)
- PATCH validates lang as 'fr' | 'en'; rejects others with 400
- PATCH allows firstName to be null (clearing the display name)
- A successful PATCH that changes lang also sets the mp_lang cookie
- The next bare-'/' redirect via middleware uses the new language without a DB hit
- Marc's prefs change is reflected in admin notification emails on next send
knownLimitations: Cookie write is one-way DB → cookie; a manual cookie edit doesn't persist. The DB is authoritative for cross-device consistency

Validation is minimal (lang enum, firstName length); no profanity filter on firstName or anti-spoofing checks — the display name is shown in dashboards and emails, so a hostile user could pick a misleading name but the impact is limited to their own surfaces

PATCH writes BOTH fields even when only one is supplied (UPSERT semantics); the unchanged field retains its prior value. Semantically a partial update, but implemented as a full-row UPSERT for simplicity
No batching across multiple rapid PATCHes — if the UI debounces poorly, multiple writes can hit in succession. Acceptable at this scale; D1 handles it fine

statusHistory.reason: Endpoint shipped with both GET and PATCH; lang + firstName supported.
Code-first doc co-located. Pref endpoints are stable; cookie-sync side effect remains essential to the middleware's hot path.

features.title: GET + DELETE /api/me — current identity + Loi 25 self-serve erasure
features.problem: Two endpoints on one resource. GET /api/me is the SPA's identity oracle — the very first call on bootstrap, returns { email, isAdmin } for authenticated visitors and { email: null } for unauthenticated. Returning 200 in both cases (not 401) keeps the SPA's bootstrap simple. DELETE /api/me is the Loi 25 self-serve erasure — a signed-in visitor can hard-delete everything they own in one call: sessions (cascades to messages + attachments via FK), magic-link tokens for their email, R2 attachment blobs (best-effort), and finally their own row.
features.analysis: GET handler: read mp_session via currentEmail(); if absent return 200 { email: null, isAdmin: false }. If present, also self-heal the mp_csrf cookie (sessions issued before the CSRF rollout had no mp_csrf — handler mints one if missing). Returns 200 { email, isAdmin } with isAdmin derived from env.ADMIN_EMAILS. DELETE handler: requires sign-in; gated by CSRF (middleware). Runs the erasure in order: (1) DELETE FROM magic_link_tokens WHERE email = ?; (2) SELECT all sessions owned by email, collect their attachment R2 keys; (3) DELETE R2 objects (best-effort — failures logged but don't block; the DB row going is the legal commitment); (4) DELETE FROM sessions WHERE email = ? — FK cascades drop messages, advancements, attachments rows, payments. The cookie is cleared on response so the visitor's next request shows them as signed-out. The visitor-side flow then navigates to /au-revoir (the ritual page) via the mp_just_erased sessionStorage flag set by the SPA before calling here.

decisions.decision: GET returns 200 { email: null } for unauthenticated, NOT 401
decisions.rationale: The SPA bootstraps by calling /api/me before any UI renders. If unauthenticated returned 401, every signed-out visitor's first request would be an error — the SPA would either need to swallow the error or render an error state. 200 with null email is cleaner: one response shape covers both states, the SPA gets a JSON body either way, no error-handling branching needed for the very common signed-out case.
decisions.recommendation: Don't switch to 401. If a future need requires distinguishing 'expired session' from 'never signed in', surface that in the JSON ({ email: null, reason: 'expired' }) rather than by status code.

decisions.decision: Erasure cascades via FK; explicit R2 cleanup is best-effort
decisions.rationale: D1's FK ON DELETE CASCADE handles sessions → messages, advancements, attachments rows, payments — one DELETE on sessions removes everything related. R2 (blob storage) is separate and doesn't auto-cascade, so attachments must be enumerated and deleted manually. R2 deletion is best-effort because: (a) the legal commitment is the DB row going (no more record we hold this), (b) an R2 failure shouldn't block the erasure from completing on the DB side, (c) a future garbage-collection sweep catches orphan R2 objects.
decisions.recommendation: If R2 errors become common, add a retry layer rather than blocking the DB erasure. The orphan-attachment GC piggybacks the digest cron and catches stragglers in any case.

decisions.decision: Self-heal CSRF cookie inside GET /api/me
decisions.rationale: The CSRF rollout (mp_csrf cookie) shipped after some users already had mp_session cookies. Those sessions had no mp_csrf and would fail every state-changing request silently. Self-healing in GET /api/me means the SPA's first bootstrap call mints the cookie for grandfathered sessions, no migration script, no user action required. Clean self-fix.
decisions.recommendation: Keep the self-heal. It's a write inside a GET, which is normally a smell, but the write is purely additive (no existing data changes) and the alternative is a forced sign-out for every grandfathered user.

features.successCriteria:
- GET returns 200 { email, isAdmin } for authenticated; 200 { email: null, isAdmin: false } for unauthenticated
- mp_csrf cookie is minted on GET if absent (self-heal for pre-CSRF sessions)
- DELETE requires sign-in (401 if missing) and CSRF (403 if missing)
- DELETE cascades through sessions/messages/advancements/attachments via FK
- R2 attachment blobs are deleted best-effort; failures don't block the DB delete
- Response clears mp_session cookie so subsequent requests show signed-out
- After DELETE, GET returns { email: null } for the same browser
knownLimitations: R2 cleanup is best-effort; orphan attachments accumulate if R2 fails for a session. The orphan-attachment GC sweep (piggybacked on the digest cron) catches them eventually

Erasure is per-email; if a visitor has multiple emails registered (technically possible with two magic-link flows), only the signed-in email's data is erased — the other emails remain. Mitigation: a visitor wanting full erasure can sign in with each email and erase, or contact Marc directly
DELETE clears the mp_session cookie via response header but not via active D1 server-side state — there's nothing to clear server-side because sessions are stateless cookies
GET self-heal does a write inside a GET (mp_csrf cookie mint when missing); this is normally a code smell but is purely additive and only fires for grandfathered sessions

statusHistory.reason: GET /api/me shipped as the bootstrap identity oracle.
DELETE /api/me added with the Loi 25 erasure flow: FK cascades + R2 best-effort cleanup.
Code-first doc co-located. Both endpoints stable; ton-passage arc's /au-revoir consumes this flow.

features.title: GET /api/meta/stats — the live half of the /meta scorecard
features.problem: The /meta page grades itself in public: how many features documented, what fraction frozen, what the practice's actual triage SLA hit rate looks like. The static half (feature count, frozen ratio) comes from src/data/lac-features.json at build time; the dynamic half (shipped count, median response hours, SLA compliance) needs live D1 data. This endpoint is that. Aggregate-only — never an email, never a body, just counts and median.
features.analysis: GET only. Two queries: (1) COUNT sessions WHERE status='shipped' AND deleted_at IS NULL — the shipped count. (2) SELECT status_history FROM all sessions, parse each for the FIRST 'triage' → 'active' or 'triage' → 'rejected' transition; compute hours from session creation to that transition. Take the median, drop outliers above MAX_PLAUSIBLE_HOURS (24 * 365 = a year, defends against clock-skew or bad data). Returns { shippedCount, medianResponseHours, sampleSize, slaHours }. slaHours is the SLA_HOURS constant (72) that /me/intake copy promises; surfacing it here lets /meta compare the promise to the median.

decisions.decision: Compute median in-handler instead of SQL
decisions.rationale: status_history is a JSON blob column (deliberate — see sessions/[id] feature.json's decision). SQL median over JSON would require JSON1 extension and complex unnesting. In-handler parsing is simpler, runs once per /meta page load, and stays maintainable. The dataset is bounded (every session ever, currently <100) so the compute cost is negligible.
decisions.recommendation: Don't move to SQL aggregation unless the dataset grows past ~10k sessions, which is years away.

decisions.decision: Outlier filter at 24 * 365 hours
decisions.rationale: Clock skew or data import errors could produce a session with an absurd response time (negative time, century-old timestamp). Capping the plausible range at 1 year prevents one bad row from dominating the median.
decisions.recommendation: If a legitimate 'we paused for 6 months' case ever appears, raise the cap. Don't lower it — the goal is to defend against bad data, not slow responses.

features.successCriteria:
- Aggregate-only response — no PII
- Shipped count + median triage response in hours
- Sample size is included so a reader can judge confidence
- SLA hours surfaced for comparison against the median
- Median is null when sample size is zero (don't fake a number)
- Outlier sessions don't dominate the median
knownLimitations: Median is computed at request time, no caching — fine at current load
Status history parsing depends on the JSON blob schema; a future status-history table migration would require updating this handler too
Outlier cap is hardcoded; if a legitimate long-paused session occurs, it'd silently get excluded from the median

statusHistory.reason: Endpoint shipped with the live scorecard math.
Code-first doc co-located. Median computation + outlier filter stable.

features.title: POST /api/payments/portal — Stripe Customer Portal session
features.problem: Custodian subscribers need a way to update their card, view invoices, and cancel without going through Marc. Stripe's hosted Customer Portal handles all of that — this endpoint mints a temporary URL the browser redirects to. Cancellation via the portal triggers customer.subscription.deleted → webhook flips custodian to 'switched_to_tout_a_toi' per the /handoff promise. No payment-method UI in this codebase; Stripe's portal IS the UI.
features.analysis: POST { sessionId, lang? }. Auth: canAccessSession() (self or admin). Loads the session, finds its stripe_customer_id (set at the first subscription's checkout.session.completed). createBillingPortalSession() (functions/_lib/stripe.ts) calls Stripe's billingPortal.sessions.create with the customer + a return_url back to /me. Returns { url } so the SPA can redirect. STRIPE_SECRET_KEY guard returns 503 if unset (consistent with the rest of the payments family). 404 if the session has no stripe_customer_id (means no subscription was ever created — portal would be empty).

decisions.decision: Use Stripe's hosted portal, don't build our own card/invoice UI
decisions.rationale: Building a PCI-compliant card UI is months of work + an ongoing audit burden. Stripe's portal is hosted, audited, and free to use. The visitor sees Stripe's UI for ~30 seconds, then comes back to /me. The trade-off is a brief UX shift to Stripe's brand; acceptable for the visitor (they understand Stripe handles billing).
decisions.recommendation: Don't build a custom card UI. If Stripe ever discontinues the portal (extremely unlikely), evaluate alternatives at that point.

decisions.decision: 404 when session has no stripe_customer_id, not an empty portal
decisions.rationale: A session that never had a subscription has no customer in Stripe — Stripe would reject the create-portal-session API call with an unhelpful error. Returning 404 from our endpoint is a cleaner UX and matches the semantic ('no portal to open for this session'). The SPA can render 'no active subscription' instead of letting the user click into an error.
decisions.recommendation: Keep the explicit 404. If a future flow creates a Stripe customer without a subscription (e.g. saving card on file), thread it through this gate too.

features.successCriteria:
- A signed-in visitor (or admin) can mint a Customer Portal URL for any session they own with a custodian subscription
- 404 when no stripe_customer_id (no subscription ever existed)
- 503 when STRIPE_SECRET_KEY unset
- The portal's return_url is /me so the visitor lands back on the dashboard
- Cancellation in the portal triggers the webhook flow → custodian_status switches
knownLimitations: Portal session URLs are short-lived (60 min typical); clicking a stale URL fails on Stripe's side
Portal can't be themed to match marc-portal — it's Stripe's brand for the duration. Acceptable
No way to inline-cancel within this codebase; the portal is the only path. If the portal is ever unavailable (Stripe outage), the visitor has to email Marc to cancel

statusHistory.reason: Endpoint shipped with portal session creation; integrates with the customer.subscription.deleted webhook flow.
Code-first doc co-located. Stripe portal pattern is stable; no plans to build a custom UI.

features.title: POST /api/payments/webhook — Stripe → us event handler
features.problem: Stripe is the source of truth for whether a charge succeeded — the browser-side success redirect after Checkout could be lost (the user closes the tab), so the DB must reconcile from the server-to-server webhook. Six event types matter: checkout.session.completed (mark paid, link Stripe ids), invoice.paid (subscription renewal — first invoice attaches to the original row, renewals insert new), invoice.payment_failed (mark sub past_due), customer.subscription.deleted (auto-switch custodian to 'tout_a_toi' per the /handoff promise), customer.subscription.updated (logged only — wait for terminal events), charge.refunded (track partial + full refunds; only flip to 'refunded' when fully refunded).
features.analysis: POST handler reading the RAW body (no JSON parse before signature verification). Stripe-Signature header is verified via HMAC-SHA-256 over the raw bytes using STRIPE_WEBHOOK_SECRET — if it fails, return 400 without touching D1. Idempotency: event.id is inserted into webhook_events on first arrival; a duplicate event.id (Stripe retry) short-circuits with 200 and no side effects. This prevents duplicate admin-notification emails on retry storms. Per event type, the handler runs the appropriate DB UPDATE + possibly sends a Resend notification to Marc. UPDATEs are themselves idempotent (UPDATE WHERE status != 'paid' etc.) so a slipped-past-idempotency-check duplicate doesn't double-write. Returns 200 on every internal failure path so Stripe doesn't retry into errors that are our problem to fix — Sentry catches and the admin sees it in /admin/digest. CSRF-exempt because the auth IS the signature; no cookie context to defend.

decisions.decision: Verify the HMAC signature over the RAW body, not the parsed JSON
decisions.rationale: Stripe's signature is computed over the exact bytes they sent. Parsing to JSON and re-stringifying would change the bytes (whitespace, key order) and break the signature. Reading raw via request.text() preserves the bytes for verification. After verification passes, the same string is parsed once for use.
decisions.recommendation: Don't refactor to 'parse once, verify over the JSON.stringify'. The raw-bytes pattern is the only safe path; any framework that parses before signing must be sidestepped.

decisions.decision: Always return 200 on internal failures, never echo errors back to Stripe
decisions.rationale: Stripe retries non-200 responses aggressively. If a non-Stripe issue (D1 timeout, Resend hiccup) causes a 500, Stripe would retry — and the next retry might hit a different failure or duplicate the side effects. Returning 200 + logging the failure to Sentry breaks the retry loop; the engineer fixes the underlying issue and may need to re-run reconciliation. The trade-off is silent failure visibility, mitigated by /admin/digest synthetic monitoring.
decisions.recommendation: Don't return 500 from this handler under any circumstances. The only legitimate non-200 is 400 on signature mismatch (which IS Stripe's problem to retry-into).

decisions.decision: customer.subscription.deleted → custodian_status = 'switched_to_tout_a_toi'
decisions.rationale: The /handoff page promises that a custodian subscription canceled (not renewed) auto-switches the session to 'tout-à-toi' mode — the visitor keeps the code but Marc stops monitoring. The webhook is where this promise becomes operational. Other terminal events (deleted, canceled-via-customer-portal) all flow through the same path. The wording 'tout_a_toi' is the internal enum; UI translates.
decisions.recommendation: When adding a new custodian terminal event, route it to the same status. If a buyer ever wants different semantics (e.g. 'pause but don't switch'), introduce a new status; don't reuse this one.

features.successCriteria:
- Stripe-Signature is verified against STRIPE_WEBHOOK_SECRET over the raw body; mismatch returns 400
- Duplicate event.ids short-circuit at the top with 200 and zero side effects
- All six event types route to their right DB UPDATE + notification
- Internal failures (D1, Resend) are logged to Sentry, response is 200
- DB UPDATEs are idempotent (WHERE clauses prevent double-flip)
- customer.subscription.deleted flips custodian to 'switched_to_tout_a_toi' per /handoff
- charge.refunded tracks partial refunds; flips status to 'refunded' only on full
knownLimitations: The dedup window relies on webhook_events table; if that table is dropped + recreated, recent duplicates could replay. Don't drop the table casually
Always-200 on internal failures means a misconfigured DB or Resend silently fails until /admin/digest synthetic monitoring catches it — the digest is what makes this safe

Some webhook events not handled (charge.succeeded for one-shot payments — we use checkout.session.completed instead). If Stripe ever deprecates the events we DO subscribe to, the handler needs an update

STRIPE_WEBHOOK_SECRET rotation: rotate in Stripe Dashboard, then immediately via `wrangler secret put STRIPE_WEBHOOK_SECRET` — between those operations, signature verification will fail. RUNBOOK covers the recovery
Subscription renewals create NEW payments rows (one per invoice); a long-running custodian sub accumulates rows. By design — each invoice is its own paid event

statusHistory.reason: Endpoint shipped with signature verification + all six event types + idempotency.
Code-first doc co-located. Webhook is load-bearing for payment reconciliation; behaviour stable.

features.title: GET /api/public/projects — unauth showcased-projects gallery feed
features.problem: The home-page hero projects strip and the /projects gallery both need a list of admin-curated showcase entries — sessions with showcased_at set, pulling in their pinned current-build advancement for the iframe preview. The data is public by design but pulls from session rows that ALSO contain PII (visitor email, intake content). This handler is the projection that strips PII at the SQL level and returns only what the gallery needs.
features.analysis: GET only. SELECT showcased sessions (showcased_at IS NOT NULL AND deleted_at IS NULL), JOIN their pinned current_build advancement when present. Project to ShowcaseRow: id, showcased_at, showcase_title, showcase_tagline, status, tier, current build_url + iframe_path + commit_sha. No email, no intake_json, no message bodies, no internal advancements. parseFlags is used to read the pinned-current-build flag from the advancement's flags JSON. Ordered newest-showcased first. No pagination — the showcase is small by intent (the practice doesn't ship 200 projects), and a curator-bounded list doesn't need infinite scroll.

decisions.decision: Strip PII at the SQL SELECT, not in the handler
decisions.rationale: Same defense-in-depth pattern as /api/public/vouches. A future handler refactor that accidentally returns the full row should STILL not leak PII because the projection doesn't include those columns. TypeScript bound to ShowcaseRow catches anyone reaching for email.
decisions.recommendation: When adding a new public field, add it to the SELECT explicitly. When adding a private field to sessions, NEVER add it here.

decisions.decision: No pagination
decisions.rationale: The showcase is small (~10-30 entries over 3 years per the root feature.json's projections). Pagination adds client + server complexity for zero benefit at this scale. If the list ever grows past ~100, the client can trim for display; if it grows past ~500, revisit.
decisions.recommendation: Don't pre-emptively paginate. The current trade-off (one round-trip for the full list) is fine.

features.successCriteria:
- An anon visitor can fetch the showcase list with no auth
- The response carries zero PII (email, intake_json, message body) regardless of session row state
- Pinned current-build advancement is included when set; null when not
- Soft-deleted sessions never appear
- Ordering is newest-showcased-first
knownLimitations: Caching: no Cache-Control header set; CF Pages handles edge caching via its own rules. If a future need wants tighter cache control, set headers here
iframe sandbox attributes on the current_build URL are decided by the consumer (Home.tsx, Projects.tsx) — see AUDIT P1.9 for the deferred tighten-the-sandbox work
No tenant scoping in the query: tenant resolution happens in _middleware.ts before the handler; the session rows are already tenant-scoped via FK

statusHistory.reason: Endpoint shipped with the PII-stripped projection.
Code-first doc co-located. Projection-strip + no-pagination patterns stable.

features.title: GET /api/public/sessions/:id/advancements — unauth share-link feed
features.problem: The /share/:id page is the URL-as-capability share view — anyone holding a session id can see the advancements admin flagged allowedForPublic for that session. This endpoint is its data-side: load the session, then return ONLY advancements with allowed_for_public=1. Session ids are 72-bit base64url tokens; guessing one is astronomically unlikely so the URL IS the access control. Default-private means nothing leaks unless admin explicitly opts in per-advancement.
features.analysis: GET only. Validates the session id param; loadSession() returns the session or null. If session doesn't exist OR deleted_at IS NOT NULL, return 404 — consistent with /api/public/sessions/[id]/advancements returning 404 for soft-deleted sessions, preserves zero-knowledge about whether a given id corresponds to anything real. If session exists, listAdvancementsForSession() returns ordered advancements; the handler filters to those with allowed_for_public=1 (parseFlags on the flags JSON). Returns the public projection of each advancement: id, label, body, date, iframe_path, build_url, commit_sha. No internal flags, no admin notes.

decisions.decision: 404 the recipient if no public advancements exist — don't differentiate 'no such session' from 'session has nothing public'
decisions.rationale: Differentiating would leak the existence of sessions whose admin hasn't flagged anything public yet. A consistent 404 across both cases preserves zero-knowledge about session existence to anyone holding only an id. Mirrors /share/:id's expectations.
decisions.recommendation: Keep the 404 generic.

decisions.decision: Default-private: no flag means no leak
decisions.rationale: Every new advancement lands with allowed_for_public=0 unless admin explicitly opts in. This is the safer default — admin should ACT to publish, not REMEMBER to unpublish. Combined with the session-id-as-capability model, the only way an advancement reaches the public is admin's explicit per-row flag.
decisions.recommendation: When adding new advancement fields, default-deny them too. A new field that's public by default would be a regression in this model.

features.successCriteria:
- Anon visitor with the session id sees only allowed_for_public=1 advancements
- Internal advancements (work-in-progress notes admin hasn't published) NEVER leak
- 404 is uniform across 'no such session' and 'session has zero public advancements'
- Soft-deleted sessions return 404
- Ordering matches the admin-side ordering
knownLimitations: Session id is the only access control; a leaked URL is unrevokable short of clearing every advancement's allowed_for_public flag
No rate limit on this endpoint — public-by-design with high cost per request would be a vector; advancement reads are cheap so not a concern today
Iframe sandbox tightening (AUDIT P1.9 deferred) affects the consumer page, not this handler — it just returns URLs

statusHistory.reason: Endpoint shipped paired with the /share/:id page; URL-as-capability + default-private flag model live.
Code-first doc co-located. 404-uniformity and default-private behaviors stable.

features.title: GET /api/public/vouches — unauth approved-vouches feed
features.problem: The home-page social-proof block and the dedicated /vouches page both render approved testimonials. The vouches table also holds author_email for moderator follow-up; the public endpoint must strip that field at the projection level. Optional ?sessionId filter lets /share/:id surface vouches scoped to that specific project.
features.analysis: GET only. SELECT FROM vouches WHERE status = 'approved' AND deleted_at IS NULL ORDER BY created_at DESC. ?sessionId=X filter adds AND session_id = ?. toPublicVouchRow (functions/_lib/vouches.ts) projects each row to PublicVouchRow — no author_email. Volume is bounded by moderation throughput (~50/year ceiling at this practice's scale), so no pagination. Public consumers: home, /vouches, /share/:id with session filter.

decisions.decision: Centralize projection in _lib/vouches.ts (toPublicVouchRow)
decisions.rationale: Two consumers (this endpoint + future endpoints that surface vouches) need the same projection. Centralizing the function + the PublicVouchRow type means there's one place to change if the public shape needs adjustment, and the type system catches any new consumer trying to surface a private field.
decisions.recommendation: Don't inline a projection in handlers. If a new public surface needs a different cut, add a sibling function in _lib/vouches.ts; keep them all in one file.

decisions.decision: ?sessionId filter is optional; default returns all approved
decisions.rationale: /share/:id needs scoped vouches (only this project's). The home + /vouches pages want everything. One endpoint with an optional filter is simpler than two endpoints with different defaults.
decisions.recommendation: Don't add other filters (by relationship, by date range) here — those are display-time concerns. The client trims.

features.successCriteria:
- Anon visitor gets approved + not-deleted vouches
- author_email never appears in the response (projection strip)
- ?sessionId=X scopes to that session's vouches
- Soft-deleted vouches and pending/rejected vouches are excluded
- Newest-first ordering
knownLimitations: No pagination — fine at current scale (single-curator moderation queue)
?sessionId is a string filter, not validated for format here; non-existent sessions return an empty array which is the correct UX (no leak)
Vouches table also holds anti-spam metadata (IP, rate-limit hits) that's intentionally not in PublicVouchRow

statusHistory.reason: Endpoint shipped with the PII-stripped projection + optional sessionId filter.
Code-first doc co-located. Projection contract stable.

features.title: GET + POST /api/sessions — list and create sessions
features.problem: The collection endpoint for sessions. GET lists the visitor's own sessions (admin sees all + optional ?deleted=true for the trash view). POST creates a new session from an intake submission — body carries the intake_json blob and the problem type; status defaults to 'draft'. Both endpoints uphold the load-bearing capacity cap (1 active + 1 in triage); POST refuses with 409 when adding a new draft would push triage past 1. The cap check is atomic-via-subselect in the INSERT, not a read-then-write, so two concurrent POSTs can't both win past the cap.
features.analysis: GET handler: currentEmail() gates; isAdmin distinguishes scope. Visitor view: SELECT WHERE email = ? AND deleted_at IS NULL. Admin view: optionally include deleted (?deleted=true → SELECT WHERE deleted_at IS NOT NULL). The columns SELECT'd are the SessionRow projection in functions/_lib/sessions.ts. POST handler: currentEmail() gates; rate-limit checks (per-email + per-IP) prevent flood. The cap check happens IN the INSERT statement: ... WHERE (SELECT COUNT(*) FROM sessions WHERE status='triage' AND deleted_at IS NULL) < 1. result.meta.changes === 0 means the WHERE failed (at cap) → return 409 conflict. Atomic guard documented in AUDIT P1.7 — replaces an earlier read-then-write that two concurrent POSTs could both pass. POST body validated for intake_json shape (size cap 1MB per AUDIT P3.5, problem_type enum).

decisions.decision: Capacity cap enforced in the INSERT's WHERE, not as a separate SELECT
decisions.rationale: A read-then-write capacity check (SELECT count → if ok → INSERT) is racy: two concurrent requests both see count=0, both INSERT, capacity becomes 2. Folding the count check into the INSERT's WHERE subquery means the DB does the count + insert atomically; the second request's INSERT affects 0 rows and we return 409. This is the AUDIT P1.7 fix; the prior version had the race documented in commit history.
decisions.recommendation: When adding any 'check then mutate' pattern, look for the same atomic-WHERE solution. read-then-write across a request boundary is the smell.

decisions.decision: GET visitor view filters by email; admin view sees all
decisions.rationale: A visitor seeing other visitors' sessions would be a data leak. Filtering by the cookie's email at the SQL level (not in app code) means even a buggy admin-only check elsewhere can't accidentally expose cross-visitor data. isAdmin determines whether the filter is applied; this is the only privilege boundary in the handler.
decisions.recommendation: If a future 'shared session' feature emerges (multiple emails owning one session), thread it through canAccessSession() in _lib/sessions.ts — keep this handler's filter simple.

decisions.decision: ?deleted=true is admin-only AND requires explicit opt-in
decisions.rationale: Soft-deleted sessions are visible to admin via the trash UI for recovery. Two safety properties: (a) visitors NEVER see deleted=true rows even if they pass the query param — the admin check is required; (b) admins see live rows by default, must opt in to see deleted ones. The opt-in prevents an admin accidentally treating a deleted row as live in routine work.
decisions.recommendation: Don't make ?deleted=true the default for admins. The opt-in is the safety property; defaulting would silently change what admin tools display.

features.successCriteria:
- GET as visitor returns ONLY their own non-deleted sessions
- GET as admin returns all non-deleted sessions; ?deleted=true returns only the trash
- POST refuses with 409 when triage is at cap (1 row); the refusal is atomic against concurrent POSTs
- POST rejects intake_json bodies larger than 1MB (P3.5)
- New sessions land status='draft'; status transitions happen via PATCH /api/sessions/:id
- Per-email + per-IP rate limits protect POST
knownLimitations: The 1MB intake_json cap (P3.5) is per-payload; an attacker could submit many medium payloads to fill DB. Rate limit caps that; the napkin PNG → R2 work (P1.8 deferred) would lower per-payload size further

Capacity cap is hardcoded to 1 triage + 1 active — both numbers live in functions/_lib/sessions.ts as constants. Changing them would also require updating /api/capacity (the public read-side) and the home counter copy

POST returns 409 without explaining WHY (cap reached); the client maps the code to a friendly 'I'm at capacity' message. Adding a structured error body would be defensible but creates a vector for testing the cap from outside (probing the response)
Soft-delete (?deleted=true) is admin-only; a visitor wanting to see their own deleted history can't — Loi 25 right-of-access is satisfied by their data being gone, not by surfacing tombstones

statusHistory.reason: Endpoint shipped with capacity cap + visitor/admin scope.
Atomic capacity guard (AUDIT P1.7) replaced the racy read-then-write check.
Code-first doc co-located. Capacity cap, scope filter, and atomic guard all stable.

features.title: GET + POST /api/sessions/:id/advancements — list and create build advancements
features.problem: An advancement is a single named build step — 'I shipped the napkin sketch', 'I added auth', 'I deployed v2 of the search'. Each advancement carries a label, body, optional iframe path (for the embedded build preview), and a flags array (allowedForPublic, milestone, etc.). The list endpoint scopes view: visitors see only public-flagged advancements, owner + admin see all. POST is admin-only — creates a draft advancement with build_url null; CI stamps the URL + commit sha on the next deploy via scripts/stamp-revisions.mjs.
features.analysis: GET: canAccessSession() then scope-filter — visitor view shows only advancements with allowed_for_public flag set, owner/admin sees all. listAdvancementsForSession() (functions/_lib/advancements.ts) handles the projection. POST: admin-only (return 403 for non-admin). Body { label, body, date?, iframePath?, flags? }; all fields go through normalize helpers (normalizeLabel, normalizeBody, normalizeIframePath, parseFlags+stringifyFlags) so the stored data is canonical. build_url is null at insert; the deploy workflow (scripts/stamp-revisions.mjs) runs after each prod deploy and stamps build_url + commit_sha on any advancement that doesn't have one. So a new advancement is 'unstamped' between creation and the next deploy; the UI shows 'pending stamp' for that window.

decisions.decision: Visitors see only public-flagged advancements; admin/owner sees all
decisions.rationale: Some advancements are work-in-progress notes Marc doesn't want the visitor to see yet (e.g. 'tried approach X, didn't work'). Defaulting to admin-only and explicitly flagging the publicly-visible ones gives Marc control over what the visitor reads. The owner (session creator) sees all to match what they'd see if they were Marc — they're the engagement's primary stakeholder.
decisions.recommendation: When designing UI affordances for the flag, make it explicit (a checkbox 'show on /share/:id'); avoid implicit defaults that could leak.

decisions.decision: build_url stamped by CI, not set at write time
decisions.rationale: The advancement is created BEFORE the deploy that builds it; the build URL doesn't exist yet. CI (deploy.yml) runs scripts/stamp-revisions.mjs which scans the commit's touched feature.jsons + the recent advancements, and stamps build_url + commit_sha. The visitor sees the URL once the deploy lands. This shape lets the admin author advancements in advance of a deploy without coordinating timing.
decisions.recommendation: Don't manually set build_url unless you really know which commit it should map to. The stamp script handles it.

decisions.decision: Use normalize helpers (label, body, iframePath, flags) at the boundary
decisions.rationale: Centralizing the normalization means the same input format produces the same stored value, no matter which path writes it (this handler, the [advId] PATCH handler, future bulk imports). Examples: iframePath is normalized to start with '/', flags are sorted before stringify, label is trimmed and length-capped.
decisions.recommendation: When adding a new field, write the normalize helper next to the others in _lib/advancements.ts and use it consistently.

features.successCriteria:
- GET returns visitor-visible advancements for visitors, all advancements for owner/admin
- POST is admin-only; visitors get 403
- New advancements land with build_url null; CI stamps them on next deploy
- All inputs pass through normalize helpers
- allowedForPublic flag controls visitor visibility
- 404 for missing or unauthorized session
knownLimitations: Advancement creation is admin-only; visitors can't self-flag a milestone. By design — advancements are Marc's record of what's been built
build_url stamp window is between advancement creation and next deploy; if no deploy happens for a long time, advancements sit unstamped. The /admin/inbox UI shows 'pending stamp' for these
Flags is a JSON-as-text column; adding a new flag is purely additive (no schema change) but a future relational flags table would be cleaner. Not needed at current scale
No 'undo delete' for advancements — DELETE on the [advId] handler is hard. Soft-delete could be added if the workflow needs it

statusHistory.reason: Endpoint shipped with the public-flag scope + CI stamping integration.
Code-first doc co-located. Stamp + flag + normalize patterns all stable.

features.title: PATCH + DELETE /api/sessions/:id/advancements/:advId — edit and remove
features.problem: Per-advancement edit and remove operations. PATCH supports partial updates of label / body / date / iframePath / flags; the same normalize helpers from the index handler ensure stored values stay canonical. Admin-only writes (visitors don't edit Marc's records of what he built). There's also an admin override to clear or replace the CI-stamped build_url + commit_sha for the rare case where the stamp landed on the wrong revision (e.g. a hotfix re-stamped over the original).
features.analysis: PATCH: admin-only. loadAdvancement() + loadSession() to validate the advId belongs to the sessionId. Body fields are optional partials; each normalized via the helper if present, left unchanged if absent. The build_url + commit_sha admin override is rarely used (CI stamps are usually correct) but covers the hotfix scenario. DELETE: hard delete (no soft-delete on advancements). Cascades nothing (advancements have no children). Returns 200 { ok: true }.

decisions.decision: Hard-delete advancements (no soft-delete)
decisions.rationale: Sessions soft-delete because the admin trash UI needs to be able to recover them. Advancements are a per-build record; if you delete one you've decided the record shouldn't exist, and recovery from a 'I deleted it by accident' is one re-add away (no original work lost). Soft-delete would add a deleted_at column + filter on every read for ~zero benefit.
decisions.recommendation: If a 'recover deleted advancements' UI is ever needed, the right answer is probably 'just re-add' rather than retrofitting soft-delete.

decisions.decision: Admin can override build_url + commit_sha; document the rare-use case
decisions.rationale: CI stamping is usually correct, but edge cases (a hotfix re-stamped over the wrong advancement, a manual deploy that didn't trigger the stamp script) need a manual escape. The override is admin-only and tied to this specific endpoint to keep the surface small. UI affordance is hidden by default.
decisions.recommendation: Don't surface the override in a 'edit advancement' UI by default. It's an operator-grade tool; admin grep the field name in /admin/inbox source if needed.

features.successCriteria:
- PATCH allows partial updates of any combination of fields
- All inputs pass through normalize helpers consistently with the index handler
- DELETE removes the row hard, no orphans (advancements have no children)
- Admin-only enforcement; visitors get 403
- 404 when advId doesn't belong to sessionId
- build_url override is available but explicit
knownLimitations: No edit history — a PATCH overwrites silently. If an audit trail of changes is ever needed, advancements would need a revisions sub-table
Hard-delete is irreversible; combined with no edit history, the only protection against accidental delete is operator caution
build_url override applies only to this advancement; no bulk-re-stamp for a whole commit's advancements. If a re-stamp is needed across many, run scripts/stamp-revisions.mjs manually

statusHistory.reason: Endpoint shipped paired with the index handler; admin edit/delete affordances live in /admin/inbox.
Code-first doc co-located. Edit + delete patterns stable; override is rarely-used escape valve.

features.title: GET + DELETE /api/sessions/:id/attachments/:attId — stream and remove
features.problem: Per-attachment operations. GET streams the file content from R2 with the right content-type so the browser can render or download. DELETE removes the row + the R2 object — but is gated: the uploader can only delete WHILE the attachment is still unlinked (no message_id), admin can delete at any time. This means a visitor can recall a pre-message upload but can't go back and delete an attachment they already sent. Admin retains full control for moderation.
features.analysis: GET: canAccessSession + loadAttachment. SELECT row, fetch R2 object via env.MEDIA.get(key). Return as Response with content-type from the row. 503 if env.MEDIA binding unset. 404 if attachment doesn't exist or doesn't belong to the session. DELETE: gated by (uploader AND message_id IS NULL) OR isAdmin. The uploader window protects against the 'I sent it, then thought better' case becoming a way to erase the recipient's record of what was shared. Admin override exists for moderation (an inappropriate file slipping through). DELETE flow: env.MEDIA.delete(key) (best-effort, log on failure), then DELETE FROM attachments WHERE ... — the row going is the source of truth, R2 orphans are GC'd by the digest cron.

decisions.decision: Uploader can DELETE only while message_id IS NULL
decisions.rationale: Letting the uploader delete a linked attachment would let either party rewrite the conversation history ('I never sent that'). The pre-link window IS the recall opportunity — once the message is sent, the attachment is part of the audit trail. Admin override exists for moderation, not for either party to revise history.
decisions.recommendation: Don't widen the uploader's window. If a 'mistakes happen' UX need is real, surface a 'mark as withdrawn' status that keeps the row but visually crosses it out — don't delete.

decisions.decision: R2 deletion is best-effort; D1 row deletion is the source of truth
decisions.rationale: Same pattern as the erasure DELETE on /api/me: R2 can fail transiently, blocking the D1 row would leave inconsistent state. Better to flip the D1 row off (so reads stop returning the attachment) and let the digest cron GC the orphan R2 blob.
decisions.recommendation: If R2 reliability ever becomes a concern, add a retry layer to the R2 delete; don't block the D1 row.

features.successCriteria:
- GET streams the file from R2 with the correct content-type
- 503 when env.MEDIA unset
- DELETE allowed for (uploader AND unlinked) OR admin
- DELETE never removes a linked attachment for the uploader
- R2 delete failures don't roll back the DB row removal
- 404 for missing or unauthorized attachment
knownLimitations: GET streams the file synchronously; very large attachments hold the worker open. Per-attachment size cap from the upload side keeps this bounded
No range requests — GETting a 10MB file always streams it whole. Acceptable since files are <10MB typically
DELETE doesn't notify the OTHER party (visitor doesn't know admin deleted their attachment); deliberate, deletes are operational not conversational
If R2 succeeds but D1 delete fails (network blip mid-handler), the attachment row exists pointing at a non-existent R2 key. Rare; UI handles gracefully (renders 'attachment unavailable')

statusHistory.reason: Endpoint shipped with GET stream + DELETE-with-uploader-window.
Code-first doc co-located. Uploader-window and admin-override semantics stable.

features.title: GET + POST /api/sessions/:id/messages — chronological thread
features.problem: The single conversational channel between visitor and Marc for a session. GET returns the full thread in chronological order. POST appends a new message; the author is derived from the viewer's identity (admin → 'marc', visitor → 'visitor'), never sent by the client. Both parties get a Resend notification on the OTHER's message — visitor-posted triggers a Marc notification, Marc-posted triggers a visitor notification. This is the conversation feature operationalized.
features.analysis: GET: canAccessSession() gates; SELECT messages WHERE session_id = ? ORDER BY created_at ASC. Each message's attachments are loaded in a parallel batch via listAttachmentsForMessages (functions/_lib/attachments.ts) — N+1 avoided by a single query joining messages → attachments by message_id. POST: validate body length, rate-limit check (per-IP and per-user to prevent flood), determine author from currentEmail() + isAdmin(), INSERT message with id from randomTokenB64url. If the message has any attachments pre-uploaded (the upload-then-link flow — see attachments index handler), link them by updating attachments.message_id. The right notification is queued: visitor → sendMarcMessageNotification; Marc → sendVisitorMessageNotification. Notification is best-effort — failure logs but doesn't roll back the message INSERT.

decisions.decision: Author is server-derived from cookie identity, never client-sent
decisions.rationale: Letting the client send the author field would let a visitor post messages as 'marc'. Deriving server-side from the auth cookie is the only safe model. Admin sends as 'marc', visitor as 'visitor'; there are no other authors today.
decisions.recommendation: If multi-admin support emerges (multiple humans on the practice side), thread the actual email into the author field, not a generic 'marc'. UI translations handle the display.

decisions.decision: Attachments are uploaded BEFORE the message that links them
decisions.rationale: The alternative is a multipart POST containing message + attachments in one shot. That fails for any non-trivial attachment size (large files would hold the request open for a long time, fail on intermittent connections). The pre-upload pattern lets each attachment stream to R2 independently; the message-send call is then small and fast, linking the already-uploaded attachments by their ids. Failed uploads can be retried without re-sending the message text.
decisions.recommendation: Don't refactor to multipart message-with-attachments. The two-phase upload pattern is the right scale-friendly shape.

decisions.decision: Notifications are best-effort, not transactional
decisions.rationale: Same as the session PATCH handler: a Resend hiccup shouldn't block the message INSERT. The DB is the source of truth; the notification is a courtesy. Failures are logged + visible in /admin/digest. AUDIT P1.3 (Resend outbox) would harden this; deferred.
decisions.recommendation: Keep best-effort. If a future critical notification needs guaranteed delivery, give it its own outbox.

features.successCriteria:
- GET returns all messages for the session in chronological order, with their attachments
- POST records the message with the correct server-derived author
- Attachments pre-uploaded via the attachments POST get linked at message send
- Rate limits prevent flood (per-user + per-IP)
- Notification email is sent to the OTHER party on every new message
- Notification failure doesn't roll back the DB write
- Body length cap rejects oversized messages with 400
knownLimitations: Messages are immutable once posted — no edit, no delete. A typo stays. Acceptable for an audit-trail communication channel; if a real edit need emerges, it would need additive history (insert correction, don't mutate)
Notifications carry the message body in plain text — a long message creates a long email. No truncation today; acceptable since messages are short by social convention
Rate limits are tunable in functions/_lib/ratelimit.ts; current settings are conservative

If both visitor and Marc post within the same second, ORDER BY created_at could tie — secondary order by message id (which is monotonic-ish from randomToken) breaks ties; not perfectly chronological but indistinguishable in practice
Notifications don't currently include attachment thumbnails in the email — only a 'with N attachments' note

statusHistory.reason: Endpoint shipped with the thread + author derivation + notification queuing.
Attachment pre-upload + link flow added; messages can now carry rich attachments.
Code-first doc co-located. Thread, attachment linkage, and notification patterns all stable.

features.title: GET + PATCH + DELETE /api/sessions/:id — session lifecycle
features.problem: Per-session lifecycle. GET reads (visitor sees their own, admin sees any). PATCH is the workhorse: admin-only status transitions (draft → triage → active → ...), visitor or admin intake_json edits with optimistic concurrency via ifUpdatedAt, custodian plan changes, tier assignment, withdrawals. Each transition triggers the right Resend notification (visitor → status email, admin → intake-edited email, etc). DELETE soft-deletes (sets deleted_at); FK cascades do NOT fire (soft delete preserves the row + its messages + advancements for admin trash recovery).
features.analysis: GET: canAccessSession() (functions/_lib/sessions.ts) gates ownership; admin bypasses. PATCH is the most complex: body shape varies by what's being changed (status, intakeJson, tier, withdraw, allYoursAck, custodianPlan). Most fields are admin-only; intakeJson can be visitor-edited only when status is in editable phases (draft, triage). ifUpdatedAt is a millisecond timestamp the client sends back from its last GET; the UPDATE's WHERE compares against the DB's updated_at to detect a concurrent edit — mismatch returns 409 with the latest version. Status transitions write to status_history JSON column via appendStatusHistory() (helper in _lib/sessions.ts) so the lifecycle audit trail accumulates. Each transition queues the right notification email; emails are best-effort (failures logged, transition still commits). DELETE: soft-delete — set deleted_at + status='deleted-by-X', preserve row + descendants for trash recovery. Hard delete only via DELETE /api/me (full erasure flow).

decisions.decision: Optimistic concurrency via ifUpdatedAt, not a lock or last-write-wins
decisions.rationale: Two parties (visitor + admin) can edit the same session via different routes. A pessimistic lock would require a session-edit-lock table + lease renewals. Last-write-wins silently discards one party's edit. Optimistic concurrency via ifUpdatedAt rejects the second writer with a 409 + the latest row, letting the UI prompt the user to merge. The client (src/lib/sessionsApi.ts) handles the 409 by re-fetching + offering a merge UI.
decisions.recommendation: When adding a new mutable field on the session, plumb it through the same ifUpdatedAt path. Don't add a per-field 'last writer wins' for convenience — silent data loss is worse than a 'reload, please' prompt.

decisions.decision: Status transitions write to a status_history JSON column, not a separate table
decisions.rationale: status_history is a read-mostly audit trail; writes only happen on transitions (which are rare). A JSON column on the session row avoids a JOIN on every GET and keeps the audit trail co-located with the parent. The trade-off is no SQL-level history querying (would require JSON1 extension); for the current use case (render the history on /admin/inbox/:id), in-app parsing is plenty fast.
decisions.recommendation: If history queries become hot (e.g. 'show me all sessions that transitioned from triage to active in the last 7 days'), the JSON column can be migrated to a transitions table — but JSON1 SQLite functions would handle it first. AUDIT P2.3 deferred this.

decisions.decision: Email notifications are best-effort, not transactional with the DB write
decisions.rationale: If Resend is down, blocking the status transition means the admin can't move sessions through the pipeline until email service is restored — too tight a coupling. Email is the notification, not the system of record. Failures are logged + appear in /admin/digest synthetic monitor; the DB transition is the source of truth.
decisions.recommendation: Don't make emails transactional. If a critical notification ever needs guaranteed delivery (e.g. payment-related), use Stripe webhooks or a dedicated outbox queue, not the session-PATCH path.

features.successCriteria:
- GET enforces ownership (visitor sees own, admin sees any); 404 for missing or unauthorized
- PATCH supports all transition kinds with the right auth gates (admin-only for status, mixed for intakeJson)
- ifUpdatedAt mismatch returns 409 with the current row
- Status transitions append to status_history JSON; never overwrite
- The right Resend notification is queued per transition kind
- DELETE soft-deletes (sets deleted_at) and preserves the row for trash recovery
- Email failures don't roll back the DB transition
knownLimitations: Optimistic concurrency only covers fields included in the UPDATE; a future field added without threading ifUpdatedAt would be a silent gap
status_history is JSON in a single column; large histories (e.g. 50+ transitions) make the column large and the GET payload bigger. Not a concern at current scale
Soft-deleted sessions don't auto-purge; admin must manually trash-clear. A future janitor cron could TTL them (e.g. permanently delete after 90 days)
Email queue is best-effort with no retry — a one-time Resend hiccup means the notification is just lost. AUDIT P1.3 (Resend outbox) deferred
isAdmin is the only privilege gate; there's no per-resource ACL (e.g. 'admin X can view session Y but not Z'). The threat model is one Marc, one practice — multi-admin would require schema work

statusHistory.reason: Endpoint shipped with the full transition matrix + optimistic concurrency.
Code-first doc co-located. Lifecycle, concurrency, and notification patterns all stable.

features.title: PATCH /api/tenant/theme — operator-only theme JSON edits
features.problem: Future white-labelled tenants need a way to set their own theme tokens (accent colour, eyebrow text, etc.) without a code change. PATCH /api/tenant/theme accepts a partial theme JSON, validates per-key length limits, and writes it back. The render path (_lib/tenant.ts themeToCssVars) sanitizes values into CSS custom properties so an XSS via theme is bounded — but the endpoint still rejects breakouts at the input boundary. Operator-or-owner-only.
features.analysis: PATCH only. Auth: requireTenant(ctx) + currentEmail() must match tenant.owner_email OR isAdmin. CSRF gated by middleware. Body { theme: Partial<ThemeJSON> }. Validation: KEY_LIMITS is an exhaustive allowlist of ThemeJSON keys → max char length per value. Unknown keys are SILENTLY REJECTED (not 400) so a partial schema mismatch between client and server doesn't fail noisy. Each value is also string-typed before storage. The full updated theme is UPSERTed; on success returns the new theme back.

decisions.decision: Silent reject of unknown keys instead of 400
decisions.rationale: A future client could send keys the server doesn't yet know about (during a deploy window). Returning 400 would break the client until the deploy completes; silently dropping unknown keys preserves the known-key writes. The trade-off is debuggability: a misspelled key from a real user is silently ignored. Mitigation: the operator UI mirrors KEY_LIMITS so misspellings are caught client-side.
decisions.recommendation: Don't 400 on unknown keys. If a future need wants strict-schema enforcement, add it as opt-in (?strict=true) so legacy clients don't break.

decisions.decision: Per-key max length, not a per-row size cap
decisions.rationale: Theme JSON is a structured record, not free text. Each key has a real-world limit (e.g. CSS color value is short, eyebrow text is a phrase). Per-key limits catch a hostile input where one field is enormous and another is normal-sized.
decisions.recommendation: When adding a new theme key, set its limit thoughtfully — too high invites abuse, too low is annoying.

features.successCriteria:
- Only operator OR tenant.owner_email can PATCH
- CSRF gate enforced via middleware
- Per-key length limits reject oversized values
- Unknown keys are silently dropped, not 400
- The render-side (themeToCssVars) sanitizes for CSS injection
- Response carries the full updated theme
knownLimitations: Silent unknown-key drop means a typo is hard to diagnose; the operator UI mitigates
themeToCssVars sanitization is the load-bearing render-time guard — never bypass it when surfacing theme values into HTML
Theme history isn't versioned; an overwrite is permanent. Acceptable for current single-operator practice
No way to revert via this endpoint to a 'default theme' state explicitly — submit the default values yourself

statusHistory.reason: Endpoint shipped with KEY_LIMITS allowlist + operator-or-owner gate.
Code-first doc co-located. Validation + write-back pattern stable.

features.title: GET /api/tenant — public tenant identity for the SPA
features.problem: The SPA needs to know WHO the current host is — the tenant's display name, theme tokens, owner email, and whether the signed-in viewer is an operator. Resolving by Host happens centrally in _middleware.ts; this endpoint just returns that result as a JSON payload the SPA can read on mount. One endpoint serves both the marc-portal canonical host and any future white-labelled tenant.
features.analysis: GET only. ctx.data.tenant is attached by the middleware; this handler returns a public projection (TenantPublic) — id, display name, theme JSON, owner_email (for sign-in UX), operator role determination. If the request reached this handler, the tenant exists (middleware would have 404'd unknown hosts already). For the legacy pre-migration window, ctx.data.tenant may be null; the handler returns a default 'unknown tenant' shape that the SPA can render gracefully.

decisions.decision: Public projection (TenantPublic) — no secrets
decisions.rationale: The SPA needs the public-facing fields. Anything secret (Stripe keys, internal flags) lives only in env or in tenant rows the SPA never sees. TenantPublic is the contract; new fields earn their place via this projection only.
decisions.recommendation: When adding tenant fields, default them to private. Surface only if the SPA genuinely needs them.

decisions.decision: Return a default shape when middleware passed through tenantless
decisions.rationale: Pre-migration safety net. The middleware lets requests flow through if the tenant_domains table doesn't exist yet; this handler's response stays usable instead of 500'ing. Once 0002 is universally applied this branch is dead but harmless.
decisions.recommendation: Don't remove the null-branch. It's the safety net for the same reason the middleware's branch exists.

features.successCriteria:
- The SPA can fetch tenant identity on mount in one request
- Unknown hosts never reach this handler (middleware 404s first)
- Pre-migration tenantless requests get a usable default shape
- TenantPublic projection never carries secrets
- A signed-in operator gets their role flag set in the response
knownLimitations: No caching at this handler; CF Pages edge caching handles it. SPA also caches via useTenant() context
TenantPublic shape evolves carefully — any consumer in the SPA that uses a field must check for its presence (TS optional) to handle older deploys

statusHistory.reason: Endpoint shipped with TenantPublic projection.
Code-first doc co-located. Public projection contract stable.

features.title: POST /api/vouches — anonymous vouch submission, IP- and email-rate-limited
features.problem: Open submission endpoint paired with /vouch. Visitor types vouch + email; this endpoint inserts a row with status='pending' for Marc's moderation queue. No auth required (requiring a magic link first would kill the funnel for the people most likely to vouch — past clients, peers — who don't have accounts here). Email is captured for moderator verification but never appears in the public projection. Per-email + per-IP rate limits keep the moderation queue from being flooded; tight URL validation (http/https only) prevents javascript:/data: vectors.
features.analysis: POST { name, email, relationship, body, link?, sessionId?, displayPref? }. CSRF-exempt (no cookie context for anon submitter; see _middleware.ts CSRF_EXEMPT_PATHS).
