Validation: VOUCH_LIMITS thresholds for body/name/link length, isPlausibleEmail for email format, relationship must be in VOUCH_RELATIONSHIPS enum, link if present must start with http:// or https:// (javascript:/data: rejected). If sessionId supplied, validate it matches an active session (typo-rejection — Marc shouldn't see ghost attributions). Rate limits: 3/hour per email, 5/hour per IP — both lower than session-creation rates because a vouch carries less value to the submitter and is a tempting spam vector. INSERT with status='pending'; moderators see it at /admin/vouches; approved rows surface at /vouches via the projection-stripped public endpoint.

decisions.decision: Open submission (no auth), CSRF-exempt — same rationale as /api/auth/request-link
decisions.rationale: The audience most likely to vouch (past clients, peers, mentors) doesn't have portal accounts. Requiring sign-in would filter them out. CSRF is for cookie-context attacks; anon visitor has no cookie. Per-IP + per-email rate limits + manual moderation are the anti-spam layer.
decisions.recommendation: If automated abuse ever overwhelms the rate limit, add hCaptcha (privacy-friendlier than reCAPTCHA, documentable in /pia). Don't move to authentication.

decisions.decision: Tighter rate limits than session-creation (3/h per email, 5/h per IP)
decisions.rationale: A session is high-value to the submitter (they want help); a vouch is low-value to the submitter and high-value to a spammer (free message in Marc's queue). Tighter limits filter against the typical spam pattern (one IP, many fake emails, low per-email volume) and against the targeted-email-flood pattern.
decisions.recommendation: If legitimate volume hits the limit, tune up — but track WHO hit the limit; legitimate users tend to be one or two, spammers tend to be many.

decisions.decision: URL field requires http(s); javascript:/data: rejected
decisions.rationale: The link is rendered on /vouches as a real <a href> for the visitor to click. javascript:URL or data:URL would let a hostile submitter inject script when an unsuspecting visitor clicked. The protocol check at submission rejects these vectors before they reach the DB. The same protection also stops file:// URLs which could be confusing.
decisions.recommendation: Keep the strict protocol check. If a future vouch wants to link to a mailto: target, evaluate explicitly; don't relax the rule by default.

decisions.decision: If sessionId supplied, must match a live session
decisions.rationale: Some vouches reference a specific engagement ('Marc shipped X for me'). The submission form lets them attach a sessionId for the link. If the id is a typo, the vouch attributes to a non-existent session — confusing in /admin/vouches review and impossible to link from the engagement page. Validating at submission catches the typo class of mistakes.
decisions.recommendation: If a vouch needs to reference a session that's been soft-deleted, the right answer is moderator-side resolution, not relaxing this check.

features.successCriteria:
- An anon visitor can submit a vouch with no auth or CSRF
- VOUCH_LIMITS thresholds enforce body/name/link length
- Email format check rejects malformed addresses
- Relationship enum check rejects unknown values
- Link protocol check rejects javascript:/data:/file:
- sessionId if present matches a live session, else 400
- Per-email and per-IP rate limits cap abuse; over-limit → 429
- Insert lands with status='pending'; moderator review required for public surfacing
knownLimitations: No CAPTCHA — the rate limit + manual moderation is the anti-spam layer. If automated abuse leaks through, hCaptcha is the next escalation
Email isn't verified (no double opt-in) — a determined spammer could use throwaway emails. Moderation catches output
VOUCH_LIMITS duplicates client + server validation; if updated in one place the other could drift. Server is the source of truth
Rate limit IP is from Cloudflare's forwarded headers; misconfigured proxy upstream could mis-attribute requests. Production deploy is direct CF Pages, so theoretical
URL protocol check is exact-prefix; a future requirement for relative URLs would need a relaxation. None today

statusHistory.reason: Endpoint shipped with all validation + rate limits + moderation pipeline.
Code-first doc co-located. Open submission + tight rate limits + URL protocol check all stable.

features.title: GET /og/certificate/:id — auth-gated handoff certificate PNG
features.problem: A shipped session deserves a keepsake — the 'certificat de passation' the visitor (or buyer) can download and keep as the artifact of ownership. Renders project + date + transferred-assets summary + the v1.0-handoff tag on a 1000×1400 portrait PNG. Auth-gated (unlike the share OG card) because the certificate carries the visitor's name; only the session owner or admin may fetch it. Deliberately text-only to keep satori's CPU cost well clear of the worker's CPU ceiling.
features.analysis: GET only. Auth: currentEmail() + canAccessSession() (visitor self OR admin). loadSession; if missing or not the visitor's, 404. Render via workers-og's ImageResponse at 1000×1400 portrait (suited for printing on letter/A4). Text-only by design — no embedded images, no decorative SVG paths — so satori's render stays fast and CPU-cheap. The handoff date is from the session row's shipped_at; the assets line lists what was transferred (URL, GitHub repo, 1Password vault). ?lang=en swaps copy; default is FR. ?debug=1 returns the resolved fields as JSON. captureWorkerException wraps render failures; no static fallback here (the certificate is personalised — a generic substitute would be misleading), so a render failure returns 500 with a useful error body.

decisions.decision: Auth-gated; no public certificate URL
decisions.rationale: The certificate carries the visitor's name and transfer details. A public URL would let anyone with the session id download personal data. Same authorisation as the session itself — visitor self OR admin.
decisions.recommendation: Don't make this public. If a future need wants a visitor-shareable proof, generate a different artifact (e.g. a redacted version with only the project URL).

decisions.decision: Text-only render, no embedded images
decisions.rationale: satori on CF Workers has a tight CPU budget — complex layouts with image rendering trigger the 1102 error (CPU exceeded). A text-only layout renders well within budget; the certificate looks professional without imagery (typography does the visual work).
decisions.recommendation: Don't add embedded images. If a future need wants a logo, evaluate satori's actual CPU cost with the change; the worker CPU limit is a hard ceiling, not a recommendation.

decisions.decision: 500 on render failure, NOT static fallback
decisions.rationale: The share OG card falls back to a static image because a 'preview is generic' is acceptable. A handoff certificate is personalised — a generic substitute would be misleading and undermine the keepsake value. A 500 with a clear error body lets the UI render 'certificate temporarily unavailable, retry' instead of handing the visitor a wrong document.
decisions.recommendation: Keep the 500-not-fallback. If failure rate is high, fix the root cause (font, layout); never serve the wrong certificate.

features.successCriteria:
- A signed-in visitor (or admin) can download their certificate at /og/certificate/<id>
- Anon visitors and other-session visitors get 403 (or 404 to preserve session-existence privacy)
- Render is text-only, well within satori's CF Worker CPU budget
- ?lang=en swaps copy
- ?debug=1 returns resolved fields
- Render failure returns 500 with a useful error, NOT a generic fallback
knownLimitations: No static fallback by design; a transient render failure shows 500 to the visitor
Text-only means no logo on the certificate; if branding ever needs visual marks, evaluate satori CPU carefully
Worker CPU limit (50ms typical) is the hard ceiling; complex content additions could trip 1102 errors. Test render time after layout changes
Portrait orientation (1000×1400) is good for letter/A4 print but unusual for social previews — that's fine, this isn't a social card
The visitor's name comes from session metadata; a session without a name renders blank in that field

statusHistory.reason: Endpoint shipped with auth gate + text-only certificate layout + bilingual support.
Code-first doc co-located. Auth gate + no-fallback decision stable.

features.title: GET /og/home — dynamic OG card for the home page
features.problem: The home page's social unfurl should reflect the practice's actual state — shipped count, currently-active project's title — not a static logo. /og/home is the dynamic version: editorial layout (cream paper, mono eyebrow, trio headline, sage accent), live stats footer pulled from D1 at request time. FR by default, ?lang=en for the English variant.
features.analysis: GET only, unauth. Pulls two live numbers: shippedCount (COUNT sessions WHERE status='shipped') and activeTitle (the showcase_title of the currently-active session if any). Renders via workers-og's ImageResponse at 1200×630 PNG. ?lang=en swaps the dictionary; default is FR. Same render-with-fallback pattern as /og/share/:id — anything broken falls back to /og-image.png (FR) or /og-image-en.png (EN). ?debug=1 returns JSON of resolved fields; ?debug=render returns satori's actual error message on a failed render (deeper diagnostics than debug=1).

decisions.decision: Pull live stats at render time, not from a precomputed manifest
decisions.rationale: Pre-rendering the home OG card means recomputing every shipped-count change; the practice ships rarely but every ship would invalidate the cache. Pulling at request time + caching at the edge per unique URL is cheaper to maintain and stays current. The two D1 queries (count + active title) are cheap.
decisions.recommendation: Don't precompute. If a future need wants the active project's iframe preview composited in (image work), revisit — but the current 'numbers footer' is exactly the right amount of liveness.

decisions.decision: Two debug modes (?debug=1 + ?debug=render)
decisions.rationale: When the OG endpoint is broken, you need to know WHICH layer broke. ?debug=1 (resolved fields) verifies the handler is reached and the D1 read worked. ?debug=render captures satori's actual error message — needed when the field load works but the render fails (font missing, layout breaks satori's parser). Two modes is cheap; one composite mode would conflate the layers.
decisions.recommendation: Mirror these in any new OG endpoint. RUNBOOK § OG triage assumes their presence.

features.successCriteria:
- A scraper requesting /og/home gets the FR editorial card by default
- ?lang=en serves the EN editorial card
- Live shippedCount + activeTitle are reflected in the footer
- Render exceptions fall back to /og-image.png or /og-image-en.png
- ?debug=1 and ?debug=render both return useful diagnostics
knownLimitations: Two distinct languages means two cache entries (FR + EN); not a problem at this scale
activeTitle uses showcase_title; if a session is active but not showcased, the footer renders generic copy
The editorial layout is hand-built in satori JSX inside the handler; complex layout changes require touching this file

statusHistory.reason: Endpoint shipped with editorial layout + live stats footer + bilingual support.
Code-first doc co-located. Live-stats + fallback pattern stable.

features.title: GET /og/ping — diagnostic probe for the OG renderer
features.problem: When /og/share/:id or /og/home misbehaves, the FIRST question is 'is the functions/og/ tree even being picked up at deploy?'. A 404 there could mean Pages dropped the bundle, the bundle size exceeded the limit, or _routes.json drifted. /og/ping is the answer: plain-text 'og-ok' if reachable, JSON probe of font asset state if requested. Smaller, faster, and disjoint from the satori render path.
features.analysis: GET only. Default returns text/plain 'og-ok' with cache-control no-store (so a probe always hits the function). ?fonts=1 returns JSON: a per-font load result (ok + size + content-type per file in functions/_lib/og-fonts.ts's probe list). Returns 200 if all fonts load, 503 if any failed. Used during deploy verification and RUNBOOK § OG triage to isolate 'the function is reachable' from 'the render pipeline works'.

decisions.decision: Plain text 'og-ok' as the default response, not JSON
decisions.rationale: Plain text is the smallest possible signal that the function is alive. JSON or HTML would imply we're testing something else (parser, framework). The ASCII string is unambiguous — a curl pipe to grep just works.
decisions.recommendation: Don't elaborate the default response. If new diagnostics are needed, add another ?flag mode.

decisions.decision: Separate ?fonts=1 mode, not always-on
decisions.rationale: Font probe loads every TTF/OTF, which takes ~50ms cold. Doing it on every /og/ping would slow down the basic reachability check. Opt-in via the query param keeps the basic probe instant and surfaces detailed font state only when asked.
decisions.recommendation: When adding another probe mode (e.g. ?cache=1), follow the same opt-in pattern.

features.successCriteria:
- /og/ping returns 200 + 'og-ok' as plain text
- /og/ping?fonts=1 returns 200 + JSON when all fonts load; 503 when any fail
- Cache-control is no-store so probes always reach the function
- Disjoint from the satori render path — works even when satori is broken
knownLimitations: ?fonts=1 loads all fonts; ~50ms cold. Not suitable for high-frequency uptime monitoring
Doesn't probe the satori render itself — that's intentional, would require rendering a test image which has its own failure surface
If the worker is broken before reaching this handler (e.g. cold-start crash), the response will be CF's generic 5xx page rather than 'og-ok'; that's still useful signal

statusHistory.reason: Diagnostic endpoint shipped alongside the OG renderer.
Code-first doc co-located. Two-mode probe stable; RUNBOOK depends on it.

features.title: GET /og/share/:id — dynamic OG card for the share-link surface
features.problem: When someone shares a /share/:id URL on Slack/iMessage/LinkedIn/etc, the unfurl preview needs a personalised card — project title, tagline, tier, status — instead of a generic site image. Generated at edge-request time via workers-og (satori + resvg WASM) so the card reflects the live session state without a pre-render step. Falls back to the static /og-image.png if anything in the render path fails — the share URL still works, the preview just isn't personalised.
features.analysis: GET only, unauth. loadSession by :id; if missing or not showcased, fall back to a 302 → /og-image.png. Otherwise: build OgFields { title, tagline, tier, status } from the session row, load fonts via loadOgFonts (functions/_lib/og-fonts.ts), render through workers-og's ImageResponse. PNG output is 1200×630 (the OG spec sweet spot Slack/iMessage prefer over SVG). Aggressive edge caching headers since the underlying showcase title rarely changes. ?debug=1 returns the resolved fields as text/plain JSON — useful for verifying reachability without bootstrapping satori. captureWorkerException on errors so Sentry sees rendering failures; fallback still serves so the visitor experience never breaks.

decisions.decision: PNG output, not SVG
decisions.rationale: Slack and iMessage's OG preview implementations explicitly refuse SVG (Twitter accepts it). PNG works everywhere. The cost is the resvg WASM render step at the edge, which adds ~150ms per cold cache hit; cached responses are instant.
decisions.recommendation: Don't switch to SVG. If a future fix wants smaller payloads, evaluate WebP — both Slack and iMessage accept it.

decisions.decision: Fall back to static /og-image.png on ANY render failure
decisions.rationale: A failed dynamic OG card shouldn't break the share UX. The static fallback is generic but works; the share URL still resolves to the real /share/:id page. captureWorkerException reports to Sentry so the failure is visible operationally, not silent.
decisions.recommendation: Keep the fallback. If failures spike, fix the root cause (font loading, satori bug); don't ever 500 to the unfurl scraper.

decisions.decision: ?debug=1 returns plain text instead of PNG
decisions.rationale: When debugging an unreachable or misconfigured endpoint, the FIRST thing to verify is 'is the handler reached at all?'. A debug mode that returns the resolved fields as text lets a curl distinguish 'function is firing, just rendering wrong' from 'function never reached'. Same shape mirrored in /og/home and /og/certificate/:id.
decisions.recommendation: Don't remove the debug mode. It's how RUNBOOK § OG triage starts.

features.successCriteria:
- A scraper requesting /og/share/<live-id> gets a personalised PNG within ~150ms cold
- A missing/unshowcased session falls back to the static /og-image.png
- ?debug=1 returns the resolved fields as JSON
- Render exceptions are captured to Sentry without breaking the preview UX
- PNG dimensions are 1200×630
knownLimitations: satori font support is limited; new font files must go in functions/_lib/og-fonts.ts and be loadable as TTF/OTF
PNG render adds ~150ms to cold-cache hits; warmed cache is instant
Fallback to static og-image.png means a broken render is silent to the user; Sentry capture is the only signal
The id is the session id; a typo in the URL falls back gracefully — no leak of session existence
Edge cache TTL is set generously; a rapid title change won't propagate immediately to social scrapers

statusHistory.reason: Endpoint shipped with workers-og pipeline + static fallback + debug mode.
Code-first doc co-located. Render-with-fallback pattern stable.

features.title: Edge middleware — tenant resolution + CSRF gate + locale + OG rewrite
features.problem: Every Functions request needs four cross-cutting things done before any handler runs: (1) resolve which tenant this Host serves, (2) gate state-changing /api/* requests against CSRF, (3) on the bare '/' request redirect to the visitor's preferred language, (4) rewrite OG tags in HTML responses so social-share scrapers (which don't run JS) see the right card per-page. Doing any of these per-handler creates four places to forget; centralizing them in _middleware.ts means a new handler inherits the four guards for free. The middleware is the single piece of code every request executes through.
features.analysis: Single onRequest function called by Cloudflare Pages for every /api/* and SSR path; static assets bypass it. Steps in order: (1) resolveTenant(Host) → ctx.data.tenant — on a 'no such table' error (migration 0002 not yet applied), the request flows through tenant-less so legacy handlers don't break (admin handlers that strictly need a tenant call requireTenant and 500 cleanly); on unknown Host returns a terse 404; on tenant.status='frozen' returns 503 with a 'paused' message. (2) For state-changing /api/* requests (POST/PUT/PATCH/DELETE), check verifyCsrf unless the path is in CSRF_EXEMPT_PATHS (logout, request-link, admin/digest, payments/webhook, vouches, intake/transcribe). On gate failure, 403 'CSRF mismatch'. (3) For bare '/' GET: read mp_lang cookie OR Accept-Language; if EN preferred, 302 to /en. (4) For HTML responses, use HTMLRewriter to swap OG image, append per-page hreflang link, and rewrite og:url to the absolute canonical. Sentry exception capture wraps everything; uncaught exceptions are reported then rethrown (observe-and-propagate pattern, NOT swallow).

decisions.decision: CSRF gate is centralized here, not per-handler
decisions.rationale: Per-handler CSRF means every new POST/PATCH/DELETE handler must remember to call requireCsrf() — a forgotten one is a silent vulnerability. Centralizing means the default for any new state-changing /api/* path is 'CSRF-protected'; opting out requires editing CSRF_EXEMPT_PATHS, which is reviewable. Every exempt entry carries an inline comment justifying why.
decisions.recommendation: When adding a new POST endpoint, the default is 'no exemption needed' (it inherits the gate). Only add to CSRF_EXEMPT_PATHS if you have a real justification (no cookie context — anon submission OR third-party webhook with its own auth). Document the justification inline.

decisions.decision: Graceful pre-migration tenant fallback ('no such table' → flow through tenantless)
decisions.rationale: Fresh deploys go through a window where the tenant_domains table exists in the migrations directory but hasn't been applied to that environment yet. A hard 'tenant lookup failed' would brick every endpoint until the migration ran. The fallback keeps legacy handlers working; handlers that strictly need a tenant call requireTenant explicitly and 500 cleanly. Once 0002 is universally applied this code path becomes dead but harmless.
decisions.recommendation: Don't remove the 'no such table' branch even if every prod env is past 0002. It's the safety net for any future migration that introduces a new lookup table before handlers expect it.

decisions.decision: OG rewrite via HTMLRewriter, not server-side SSR
decisions.rationale: Pages Functions don't render the SPA — Vite ships static HTML + JS, and the JS is what builds the live DOM. Social scrapers (Facebook, Twitter, Slack, LinkedIn) don't run JS; they grab the static HTML's <meta> tags and quit. SSR would solve this but adds a render layer to maintain. HTMLRewriter streams over the static HTML at request time and patches only the OG-relevant tags — cheap, no SSR, scrapers see the right card per-page. The same rewriter handles hreflang injection.
decisions.recommendation: If a future need requires SSR (e.g. dynamic content above the fold), evaluate whether HTMLRewriter can do it before reaching for a render pipeline. Most 'I need server-side X' for marketing-stack content can be done with edge string substitution at lower complexity.

features.successCriteria:
- Every Functions request gets ctx.data.tenant attached (or null in the documented fallback case)
- State-changing /api/* paths require CSRF unless explicitly exempt
- Bare '/' GETs respect mp_lang cookie + Accept-Language and 302 to /en when EN-preferred
- Social scrapers see the right OG card per-page (FR vs. EN image, dynamic share card for /share/:id)
- A 'no such table' tenant lookup degrades gracefully, not fatally
- Frozen tenants get a 503 (not a generic error)
- Sentry captures uncaught exceptions then rethrows; nothing is silently swallowed
knownLimitations: CSRF_EXEMPT_PATHS is hardcoded — there's no dynamic exemption rule (e.g. 'requests from this user-agent'). Static set is intentional; dynamic exemption would be a regression in reviewability
The locale redirect only fires on GET '/' — visitors landing on a non-bare path are assumed to have a language signal in the path; the mp_lang cookie isn't applied to non-bare paths
HTMLRewriter only applies to text/html upstream responses — JSON responses don't get OG injection (correct, but means /api/* paths are skipped explicitly)

Tenant lookup happens on every request; D1 is fast (<10ms typical) but adds a hop. A future cache layer in _lib/tenant.ts could memoize per request — not done today since the cost is unmeasurably small
Sentry reports + rethrows uncaught exceptions; if the SENTRY_DSN secret is unset, captureWorkerException no-ops silently (graceful-degrade)

statusHistory.reason: Middleware shipped with tenant resolution; CSRF gate followed 2026-05-12, OG rewrite 2026-05-15.
Code-first doc co-located. All four guards (tenant, CSRF, locale, OG) have been stable for the past week.

features.title: Tiered public pricing
features.problem: 'Cheap' with no number attached is just rhetoric — every visitor imagines a different price, and many imagine the wrong one. This feature shows actual prices: a free self-service tier, then three paid tiers with concrete dollar figures and a one- or two-line scope each. Each paid tier links to the real projects shipped at that price, so the pricing table and the project gallery anchor each other. It is the opposite of the usual consulting move of hiding price behind 'let's have a conversation' — and the whole pitch depends on the cheapness being real and visible, not negotiated.
features.analysis: A four-tier pricing table where each card shows a tier name, a concrete price, a short scope, and a note about what happens after the work ships. Tier 0 is the free self-service off-ramp; the three paid tiers climb from a small fixed-price project to a larger custom-quoted one. One tier is marked as the anchor — the sweet-spot tier the practice expects most engagements to land in — and is highlighted so a visitor's eye settles there first. Every paid card links into the project gallery filtered to that tier, so a visitor can immediately see real examples at the price they are reading about. The table is a homepage section, placed after the self-qualification block so a visitor only reaches price once they have decided the practice is for them.

decisions.decision: Prices are public and concrete — no 'starting at', no 'depends on scope'
decisions.rationale: Without numbers, every visitor self-imagines a price, and the 'cheap' positioning collapses. Concrete figures anchor expectations in seconds.
decisions.recommendation: If a project does not fit a tier cleanly, custom-quote it after triage but anchor the conversation to the public tiers.

decisions.decision: Each tier links to the real projects shipped at that tier
decisions.rationale: Abstract tier descriptions do not anchor expectations the way actual shipped examples do. Linking each card into the filtered project gallery turns the gallery into the living definition of the tier.
decisions.recommendation: Tag every engagement with its tier so the per-tier gallery link is always populated and accurate.

decisions.decision: Tier 0 is a self-service redirect, not a paid product
decisions.rationale: A graceful off-ramp for budget-mismatched visitors belongs in the pricing table so the option is visible, but the off-ramp itself is a separate feature.
decisions.recommendation: Keep the Tier 0 card linking out to the self-service redirect page rather than describing patterns inline.

features.successCriteria:
- The pricing table shows a concrete dollar figure for every paid tier
- Each paid tier links to the project gallery filtered to that tier
- The Tier 0 card links to the self-service redirect
- A visitor can calibrate expectations within a few seconds, without leaving the section
- Nowhere in the funnel is there a 'contact us for pricing'
- Reusable as a pattern for any pricing-transparent service
knownLimitations: Pricing copy lives in the shared i18n dictionary rather than in this feature's own data, so reusing the component elsewhere means carrying the copy along
Prices are stated in US dollars with an 'approximately' qualifier — there is no currency switch or live conversion

statusHistory.reason: Pricing table renders four tiers with concrete prices; Tier 0 links to the self-service redirect; the anchor tier is highlighted.
Code-first verification — feature.json co-located at src/components/Pricing.feature.json. Confirmed the per-tier gallery links and the handoff note against the shipped component.

features.title: Vibe filter — a 'we do / we don't' self-qualifier
features.problem: Most 'ideal client' filters are demographic — industry, company size, budget. This one is temperamental: it filters for cultural fit, not budget fit. A public 'we do / we don't' list lets a visitor self-qualify before investing any effort in the intake form, which saves both sides the goodwill cost of a triage rejection. Someone who reads the list and thinks 'yes, exactly' is the right client regardless of price; someone who thinks 'but my project is special, you'll see' is the wrong client regardless of how much they would pay. A cheap-but-pushy client is worse than a well-aligned one, because the wrong client is what breaks family-first.
features.analysis: A two-column ledger on the homepage — what the practice does, and what it does not — placed before the pricing block so a visitor meets the cultural fit question before the price question. Each line is one concrete constraint drawn from how the practice actually works: no calls or meetings, async only, no urgent fixes, no scope changes mid-build, nothing real-time. The items are deliberately about temperament, not demographics. The same ledger appears again, in a lighter form, as an optional non-blocking checkbox at the start of the intake flow, so a visitor passes the self-qualification idea twice — once while browsing and once just before committing.

decisions.decision: The filter is temperamental, not demographic
decisions.rationale: A cheap-but-pushy client is worse than a well-aligned one — the wrong client breaks family-first regardless of revenue, and demographic filters miss exactly the temperament that matters.
decisions.recommendation: Keep every list item tied to a real engagement constraint; avoid filtering by industry, size, or budget.

decisions.decision: The list is published before the intake form, not after
decisions.rationale: Self-qualification only works if the visitor sees it before investing effort in the form. A 'we do / we don't' list shown afterward is useless.
decisions.recommendation: Place the ledger above the pricing block in the homepage flow, and offer an optional 'I read this and I agree' confirmation at the start of intake.

features.successCriteria:
- The 'we do' and 'we don't' lists are visible on the public homepage
- Both lists derive from documented engagement constraints, not marketing instinct
- The lists appear before the pricing block in the page flow
- The intake flow offers an optional confirmation that reuses the same ledger
- Reusable as a pattern for any service that filters for cultural fit
knownLimitations: The confirmation gate is non-blocking — checking it is encouraged but a visitor can proceed without it, because the real filter is the triage read
The lists are the same content in both languages — there are no language-specific items where culture-specific wording might land better
There is no A/B variation tracking — the lists are tuned by hand

statusHistory.reason: Vibe filter live on the homepage; the confirmation gate is wired into the intake flow.
Code-first verification — feature.json co-located at src/components/VibeFilter.feature.json. Confirmed the homepage ledger and the reused VibeGate against the shipped components.

features.title: Async status canvas — the public engagement demo
features.problem: Clients of conventional consultants get sporadic email updates and scheduled milestone meetings, with the actual work hidden between checkpoints. The async status canvas makes the work continuously visible inside the portal: a status bar shows what stage the engagement is at, a single chronological thread holds every message, and a preview slot embeds the work-in-progress so the client can poke at the real thing instead of waiting for a demo day. This feature is the public demo of that canvas — a worked sample anyone can open without signing in, so a visitor can see exactly what their own engagement page would look like before they commit to anything.
features.analysis: The engagement page replaces both the status email and the demo-day meeting. A persistent stage bar (triage, planning, building, review, shipped) sits at the top so the client never has to ask where things are. Below it, one chronological thread carries every message between the two parties, each tagged by author and by kind (update, decision, question, system). A preview slot embeds the deployable artifact when one exists. This particular page is the read-only public demo of the pattern: it renders a single hand-written composite engagement so a visitor sees the canvas fully populated. The real, private, two-way version of this surface — bound to live engagement data and gated behind sign-in — is the engagement runtime feature; this demo and that runtime share the same visual vocabulary on purpose.

decisions.decision: Engagement stage is shown globally, so the client is never unsure where things stand
decisions.rationale: Async work without visible status feels like ghosting. A persistent stage indicator removes that anxiety without anyone having to write a 'just checking in' message.
decisions.recommendation: Keep the stages few and named; make each transition a structured, timestamped event rather than free text.

decisions.decision: One chronological thread is the only client-consultant channel
decisions.rationale: Email and phone are excluded by the practice's architecture. Routing everything through the canvas keeps async-only enforceable and leaves a complete record that the public case study can later be built from.
decisions.recommendation: Treat any notification email as read-only — replies happen in the portal, not by replying to the email.

decisions.decision: Ship this as a public, fully populated demo before any real engagement exists
decisions.rationale: A visitor deciding whether to send an intake needs to see the canvas working, not an empty shell. A worked sample is more persuasive than a description and costs nothing once the renderer exists.
decisions.recommendation: Keep one composite sample current as the renderer evolves; mark it clearly as a demo so it is never mistaken for a real client.

features.successCriteria:
- A visitor can open the demo and see a fully populated engagement canvas without signing in
- The status bar always communicates the current stage
- All messages live in one chronological thread, tagged by author and kind
- The preview slot embeds a working artifact or explains when one will appear
- The demo is clearly labelled so it is never confused with a real client
- Reusable as a pattern for any project tracking long-running async collaboration
knownLimitations: This page is a hand-written demo fixture — it is intentionally not wired to live engagement data; that is the engagement-runtime feature's job
The demo is read-only — there is no reply box, because a public sample has no client to reply
The preview slot's iframe sandbox is permissive enough for self-hosted demos but too lax for untrusted third-party content

statusHistory.reason: Engagement canvas live at /engagement/:slug with a composite sample demonstrating the stage bar, thread, and preview slot.
Code-first verification — feature.json co-located at src/pages/Engagement.feature.json. Confirmed the page still renders the fixture and clarified its relationship to the engagement-runtime feature.

features.title: Portal app shell — the composition layer
features.problem: Every other feature in this portal is a reusable pattern — an intake form, a pricing table, a capacity gate — that could be lifted into another project. The app shell is the one piece that is not. It is the composition layer: the single place where those patterns are wired into a coherent site, where the routing tree lives, where the bilingual machinery is set up, and where the homepage decides which sections appear and in what order. Without a deliberate composition feature, this wiring would be scattered and nobody could point at 'the part that makes this Marc's portal and not a template.' This is that part.
features.analysis: src/pages/Home.tsx is the composition root: it imports each section component and renders them in a fixed order, so the homepage's narrative sequence lives in exactly one file. The shell is intentionally the only non-reusable feature in the portal — it is where Marc-specific copy, ordering, and visual identity collect, leaving every pattern it composes generic. Routing is a separate concern in src/router.tsx (react-router's createBrowserRouter), with a French canonical tree at / and a full English mirror under /en; a RootByTemplate wrapper sets the lang prop and the document language. All copy is one bilingual dictionary in src/i18n.ts keyed by 'fr' | 'en'. The site is a client-rendered single-page app: hot-path routes (home, intake, login) are bundled eagerly, cold-path routes are code-split.

decisions.decision: French primary, English secondary, mirrored route trees
decisions.rationale: Quebec context — a commercial site here is expected to lead in French. Mirroring the whole route set under /en rather than a runtime toggle keeps each language a real, linkable, indexable URL.
decisions.recommendation: French content at /, English mirror at /en, language switcher in the header. Every page added must add both routes.

decisions.decision: The homepage section order is fixed in one composition file
decisions.rationale: The home page is a sequence that answers a visitor's questions in order — what is this, how does it work, is it for me, what does it cost, then the call to act. Keeping the order in Home.tsx alone means re-sequencing is a one-file edit and the narrative is auditable at a glance.
decisions.recommendation: Add a section by writing its component, importing it in Home.tsx, and placing it deliberately in the sequence. Resist letting section order leak into other files.

decisions.decision: Static-first, client-rendered SPA — no UI framework, no CSS-in-JS, no state library
decisions.rationale: A boring-tech mandate: the portal must stay cheap, fast, and low-maintenance over a roughly three-year horizon. Design tokens are plain CSS custom properties; routing and context are enough for state.
decisions.recommendation: Keep dependencies pinned and minimal. If first-paint SEO ever becomes urgent before the project gallery builds gravity, revisit pre-rendering — but not before.

features.successCriteria:
- The public homepage renders every section in the intended order, in both languages
- /en is a structurally identical English mirror of the French tree
- Adding a section or a route is a localized edit (one component, one import, paired FR/EN routes)
- The shell stays the only Marc-specific feature — the patterns it composes remain generic
- Build stays a plain tsc + vite build with no framework lock-in
knownLimitations: Client-rendered SPA — Google indexes it, but first paint needs JavaScript; pre-rendering is deferred until SEO timing demands it
Section order is a hand-maintained sequence in Home.tsx — there is no CMS or config-driven ordering, by design for a single-operator site

statusHistory.reason: Homepage live in both languages; composition root at src/pages/Home.tsx wires every section; routing serves all pages.

Code-first verification — feature.json co-located at src/pages/Home.feature.json and rewritten from the shipped code. Section list, routing model, and componentFile re-checked against Home.tsx and router.tsx.

features.title: Structured intake form
features.problem: Most consulting engagements start with a 60-minute discovery call where the consultant interviews the client live. That model demands real-time scheduling, consumes hours per lead, and excludes a side-practice operator with a day job and a family. The structured intake form replaces the discovery call with a guided form a visitor fills at their own pace: they pick the shape of their problem, answer fields tuned to that shape, save a draft, and submit when ready. It is read in one batched sitting, not in real time. Because there is no AI interviewer between the visitor and the consultant — that first read of a client's pain stays human — the form itself has to be good enough to extract a usable brief from people who cannot write a brief.
features.analysis: A step machine where each problem-type carries its own field set, so structure is captured at intake time instead of being reverse-engineered from prose afterward. A short routing step asks what shape the problem is (paperwork, tracking, coordination, or other) and the matching field schema renders. The form auto-saves continuously: always to the browser's local storage, and — once the visitor is signed in — also to the server, so a draft started on a phone can be finished on a desktop. No AI sits between the visitor's words and the consultant's first read. Submitting either creates a real engagement record (if the visitor is already signed in) or sends a magic-link email so they can claim what they typed; either way nothing is lost. The capacity gate is enforced by the server, not the form.

decisions.decision: Schema-driven, not free-text — each problem-type has its own field set
decisions.rationale: A wall of free text forces the consultant to turn prose into a structured brief by hand. Per-type schemas extract that structure at intake time; free-text fields are kept only where structure genuinely cannot capture the nuance.
decisions.recommendation: Start with the few problem-types that match the practice's wedge and add types only when triage data shows real demand.

decisions.decision: No AI interviewer between visitor and consultant at first contact
decisions.rationale: The consultant reads every submission himself. An AI summary would put a model between him and the raw client voice — the worst version of AI-assisted consulting.
decisions.recommendation: AI may help a visitor phrase their own answer in the form, but must never replace the consultant's reading of the original text.

decisions.decision: Drafts save locally always, and to the server once signed in
decisions.rationale: A dead-end form that loses work on a refresh is a known failure mode. Local storage covers the anonymous visitor; server-side draft sync covers cross-device resume for the signed-in visitor without forcing an account before the first keystroke.
decisions.recommendation: Keep account creation lightweight — email only, magic-link, no password during the draft phase.

features.successCriteria:
- A visitor can fill the form at their own pace with no real-time scheduling
- Drafts auto-save and survive a refresh; a signed-in visitor can resume on another device
- Each problem-type renders its own field schema
- The consultant receives one structured record per submission, not free-text prose
- Submitting at capacity routes to a waitlist acknowledgement rather than failing
- Reusable as a pattern in any project that needs user-supplied structured input
knownLimitations: Intake messages and fields are text and sketches — no file uploads inside the form itself
Email format is checked with a regex; there is no deliverability verification
A full screen-reader and tab-order accessibility audit is still outstanding

statusHistory.reason: /intake live with the five-step flow, per-type schemas, continuous autosave, and capacity-gated submit.
Code-first verification — feature.json co-located at src/pages/Intake.feature.json. Re-checked the step flow, the server-side draft sync, and the magic-link submit path against the shipped code.

features.title: Public project gallery — shipped engagements as case studies
features.problem: Every shipped engagement is potential portfolio and search-engine compounding — but only if it is published as a public page. Most consultants treat case studies as marketing fluff bolted on after delivery. This feature treats them as primary acquisition infrastructure: when an engagement is finished, the consultant flags it for the showcase from inside the admin view, and it appears immediately in a public gallery with its tier, status, and a short title and tagline written in the client's natural language. A visitor Googling their own everyday problem can land on a real, finished example instead of an abstract pitch.
features.analysis: The gallery is generated from real engagement records, not from a separate marketing CMS. An engagement lives as a session in the database; when the consultant decides it can be public, the admin view sets a showcase flag, a public title, and a tagline on that session. The public gallery page reads only the showcased subset through a dedicated public endpoint, so nothing private leaks. Each card carries the engagement's tier and status, which means the gallery doubles as the living definition of what each pricing tier looks like in practice. The page supports filtering by tier and status. The companion 'advancements' share page exposes the progress timeline of a single engagement at its own public URL.

decisions.decision: Case studies are generated from real engagement records, not hand-authored marketing pages
decisions.rationale: A case study written from scratch is marketing; a case study that is just the public face of an actual engagement record cannot drift from what really happened, and costs almost nothing to publish.
decisions.recommendation: Keep publication a single flag plus a public title and tagline set in the admin view — no separate authoring step.

decisions.decision: Every card shows its tier, so the gallery is also the tier reference
decisions.rationale: Abstract tier names ('Starter / Pro') do not anchor expectations the way 'here are the actual projects shipped at each tier' does. Surfacing the tier on every card makes the gallery and the pricing table reinforce each other.
decisions.recommendation: Tag each engagement with its tier at triage so the gallery is correct the moment it is published.

decisions.decision: The consultant controls publication; only the showcased subset is ever public
decisions.rationale: Engagement records hold private client material. A dedicated public endpoint that returns only flagged, public-titled sessions keeps the boundary explicit — privacy is the default and publication is a deliberate act.
decisions.recommendation: Never widen the public endpoint to return un-showcased sessions; add fields to it only when they are confirmed safe to expose.

features.successCriteria:
- Flagging an engagement for the showcase in the admin view publishes it to the public gallery with no separate authoring step
- The public endpoint returns only showcased sessions and only public-safe fields
- Every gallery card shows the engagement's tier and status
- The gallery can be filtered by tier and status
- Reusable as a pattern for any project that turns finished work into public references
knownLimitations: Per-card preview images share the site-wide OG card — there is no per-engagement generated preview image yet
There is no self-serve client redaction flow — a client who wants something changed asks the consultant directly
Gallery cards do not cross-link to related engagements; each is standalone

statusHistory.reason: Public showcase renderer live, generating case-study pages from engagement records.

Code-first verification — feature.json co-located at src/pages/Projects.feature.json. The renderer is now Projects.tsx reading the public sessions endpoint; the earlier feature.json-glob implementation no longer exists.

features.title: Self-service redirect — the Tier 0 off-ramp
features.problem: Some visitors have a real problem but no commercial budget — civic coordination, household logistics, a hobby project. A consultant facing one of these usually either takes the job and loses money or rejects it and burns goodwill. The self-service redirect is a third move: a free, curated library of small patterns that says, in effect, 'your problem is small enough that hiring me would cost more than it is worth — here is how people solve this one themselves, come back if it outgrows you.' The visitor leaves with something usable, the goodwill is intact, and the page quietly earns long-tail search traffic.
features.analysis: Tier 0 replaces the binary accept/reject triage outcome with a curated off-ramp. The page lists a set of small, recognisable everyday problems, each presented as three plain blocks — the problem, a recipe to solve it, and a link to a free no-code tool that fits. The patterns are hand-picked, not generated: the value is the match, not the volume. The page opens with the principle behind it (a fifty-dollar problem deserves a free solution) and closes with an invitation to come back through the intake form when a pattern outgrows the visitor. Educating a visitor out of needing the consultant is the correct move when hiring would cost more than the problem is worth.

decisions.decision: The redirect library is curated, not algorithmic
decisions.rationale: The value of an off-ramp is the well-matched pattern, not the number of patterns. A short list of patterns the consultant actually chose feels respectful; a long auto-generated list feels dismissive.
decisions.recommendation: Add a pattern only when a real recurring request justifies it; keep each entry concrete enough to act on.

decisions.decision: Each pattern points to a free, no-code tool
decisions.rationale: A Tier 0 visitor has no budget and often no technical skill. Pointing at a spreadsheet template or a free poll tool gives them a result today without involving the consultant at all.
decisions.recommendation: Prefer well-known free tools; accept that an external tool can change its URL and keep the recipe useful even if a link rots.

features.successCriteria:
- A budget-mismatched visitor lands on a curated off-ramp instead of a flat rejection
- Each pattern gives the visitor a concrete recipe and a free tool to use today
- Adding or removing a pattern is a single edit to one data file
- The page invites the visitor back to /intake when a pattern outgrows them
- Reusable as a pattern for any practice that wants a Tier 0 without losing money
knownLimitations: There is no click-through analytics, so the page cannot yet surface which patterns are most in demand
Patterns are defined in code — fine for a curated list of this size, but not editable without a deploy
Template links point to third-party tools, which can change their URLs over time
There is no automatic inbound flow from triage into Tier 0 — a budget mismatch does not yet route a visitor here on its own

statusHistory.reason: /tier-0 live with a curated pattern library; the pricing Tier 0 card links here.
Code-first verification — feature.json co-located at src/pages/Tier0.feature.json. The pattern library has grown to roughly a dozen entries; re-checked against patterns.ts.

features.title: Triage queue with a hard capacity cap
features.problem: Without a hard limit on concurrent work, a side-practice consultant accepts every engagement that looks 'doable' and ends up with three clients at once eating evenings and weekends — and family-first, the value the whole practice is built to protect, quietly falsifies. This feature makes the load-bearing rule structural rather than aspirational: one active build plus one in triage, no more. The homepage shows the current state so a visitor knows immediately whether a slot is open, and the server refuses to create a new engagement past the cap. The public waitlist is not a flaw to hide — scarcity reads as seriousness. This is the single rule of the portal that does not bend.
features.analysis: The cap is enforced where it cannot be wished away — in the server. The current load is not a number someone maintains by hand; it is counted directly from the engagement records, so it is always true and it decrements on its own when an engagement ships. A public endpoint reports whether the practice is at capacity, and the homepage hero turns that into a plain-language slot indicator. When a visitor submits an intake while at capacity, the server declines to create a new engagement and the visitor is routed to a waitlist acknowledgement instead. Crucially there is no override: no admin toggle raises the cap, because the rule that must not bend should not have a soft switch in the code.

decisions.decision: Hard cap of one active build plus one in triage, with no exception path
decisions.rationale: This is the bedrock defence. If it breaks, family-first collapses — three concurrent clients against side-practice hours consumes weekends. The failure pattern it structurally prevents is the optimism of 'I can squeeze this one in'.
decisions.recommendation: Enforce it server-side, not as a soft warning, and provide no override anywhere in the admin surface.

decisions.decision: The public capacity indicator is an asset, not a bug to hide
decisions.rationale: Scarcity adds credibility. A visible 'currently full, waitlist open' positions the consultant as serious rather than unavailable, and the waitlist itself becomes acquisition infrastructure.
decisions.recommendation: Keep the indicator on the homepage hero and let it read straight from real engagement counts so it is never stale.

decisions.decision: Current load is derived from engagement records, never hand-maintained
decisions.rationale: A hand-edited capacity figure drifts the moment someone forgets to update it after an engagement ships. Counting active and triage engagements directly makes the figure self-correcting and removes a standing source of error.
decisions.recommendation: Keep the cap thresholds as named constants next to the counting logic so raising them is a deliberate, reviewable code change.

features.successCriteria:
- The active-plus-triage cap is enforced by the server, not by policy or a soft warning
- Submitting an intake at capacity is declined cleanly and routed to a waitlist acknowledgement
- The homepage shows the current capacity state, read from real engagement counts
- The current load decrements on its own when an engagement ships — nobody maintains it by hand
- There is no override path anywhere that can raise the cap
- Reusable as a pattern for any solo-operator service that wants the same structural rule
knownLimitations: The capacity figure reflects engagement records only — it does not model a richer queue with per-slot start dates
The homepage indicator is fetched on page load, so a visitor with a stale tab sees the state from when they opened it until the next load
The waitlist is an acknowledgement state on a declined submission, not yet a managed list with visible queue positions

statusHistory.reason: Capacity gating live; homepage hero shows the slot state and the intake submit path respects the cap.

Code-first verification — feature.json co-located at functions/api/capacity.feature.json. Capacity is now derived live from engagement records via the server; the earlier hand-edited JSON file is gone.
